No problem. Let us send you a downloadable PDF so you can read it when it’s convenient for you…
Name * Email *
IMAGINARY WORLDS
Creating an imaginary world is one of the most complex types of world-building. It’s most often utilised in fantasy and science fiction, where a writer conjures up from scratch every detail of a world: geography, history, language, lore, characters, social customs, politics, religion…
Understandably, the thought of creating all these elements to form an entire fictional world can be very daunting – and deciding where to start can seem almost impossible!
For inspiration, we recommend turning to some of the great world-builders of our time to see how they’ve built up whole universes from nothing.
Deciding on a starting point
J. R. R. Tolkien, author of Lord of the Rings, The Hobbit and countless other classic works, began the development of Middle-earth in an unusual way: by first creating an entire fictional language to be spoken by his characters. A professional philologist and talented linguist, Tolkien developed the Elvish language of Quenya, using it as a base for expanding his imaginary world into the vast, detailed, lore-rich Middle-earth we know today.
Of course, not all writers will be capable of (or interested in) creating a functional, fictional language – but budding world-builders can still follow Tolkien’s lead in order to get started.
By pinpointing one aspect of your imaginary world that you’re most interested in or most apt at developing, you’ve got yourself a great starting point.
Work hard on this element first, and then concentrate on building up and fleshing out from there.
You’ll find that the pieces of your world fall much more easily into place once you have a solid foundation from which to expand.
Rivendell, home of the elves in Tolkien’s Middle-earth.
Stay up to date with the most popular posts on Writer's Edit.
You're Subscribed!
Asking questions about your world
After you’ve got the ball rolling by establishing a starting point, you’ll need to begin working out the details that make up a convincing, consistent imaginary world.
A great way to start doing this is to ask (and answer) a set of questions pertaining to the different aspects of your world.
Approach this exercise as if you were describing your home country to someone who knows nothing about it – or, on a larger scale, as if you were introducing Earth to someone from an alien race. How would you explain:
What it looks and feels like – its landscapes, its climate?
Its people – their appearance, customs, ethics and values?
The dominant forces that shape change and development?
If you’re feeling overwhelmed by all the potential details you need to cover, it’s advisable to start by creating a list of fundamental questions you need to answer about your world.
Many online resources, such as this list from the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America website, contain suggested questions about everything from social organisation and government to the rules of magic and technology.
(The latter of these is particularly important to keep in mind. Even though your world may be an entirely imaginary one filled with magic or made-up technology, it must still be governed consistently and carefully by the internal logic and laws you set up for it.
Its fantastical nature cannot be used as an excuse for lapses in continuity.)
After devising your list, you may feel even more overwhelmed now you have such an expansive range of questions to answer!
If this is the case, take a step back and make sure that all the questions you’ve listed really need to be covered.
It’s likely that some questions may not apply to or directly affect your characters and narrative, so decide which aspects are most crucial to the stories you want to tell within your world, and focus on answering the most relevant questions.
While you’re answering these world-building questions largely for the benefit of your readers, it’s imperative to keep in mind the age-old advice about showing, not telling.
You don’t want readers to feel like they’re simply being spoon-fed a bunch of facts, details and history.
The most successful storytelling comes from a subtle, nuanced approach to building your world through narrative detail, description and development.
Map of the realms of Middle-earth, as imagined by Tolkien.
Drawing inspiration from real life
Even though imagining an entirely new world is one of the most creative processes a writer can undertake, it’s almost impossible to create something entirely from nothing.
Naturally – even if only subconsciously – you will adapt and incorporate some real-world elements into your imaginary setting and story, using them as a base of inspiration.
A well-known fantasy epic with strong undertones of historical influence is George R. R. Martin’s series, A Song of Ice and Fire.
Martin openly acknowledges the fact that many elements of ASOIAF are inspired by real historical events and locations: the Wars of the Roses, the Glencoe Massacre and Hadrian’s Wall, to name just a few.
If you feel your world is lacking in depth or credibility, perhaps take a leaf from Mr. Martin’s (extremely long) book and delve into some real-world history for inspiration.
You may be able to flesh out your world by moulding, adapting or drawing parallels with real-life locations, landmarks, pivotal events, or even historical personalities.
The sprawling city of Meereen from George R. R. Martin’s ‘A Song of Ice and Fire’.
Where reality and fantasy collide
An interesting way to provide contrast or conflict within your story is by developing your fictional world alongside, or within, an established location – for example, right here on Earth.
Perhaps the most famous example of this reality/fantasy cross-over is J. K. Rowling’s Harry Potter series, which involves an entirely made-up world of magic that’s hidden away on modern-day Earth.
Harry himself, as the main character from whose point of view the story is presented, is as much a stranger to this world as readers are in the beginning.
We’re introduced firsthand to every person, place, detail and experience right alongside Harry; as he journeys into this magical new world and has his questions answered, so, too, do we as readers.
Harry enters Diagon Alley to explore his magical new world.
As well as adding depth and relatability to your story, such a setting also poses questions about the concept of an alternate reality. Let’s now look at this concept in more detail.

ALTERNATE REALITY
Similar to the creation of an imaginary world, but slightly less demanding due to the existing base you have to work with, the construction of an alternate reality is a type of world-building often found in dystopian, speculative and science fiction.
By creating an alternate reality, you are developing an alternative version of our own Earth, imagining how things could be different and posing questions about what these differences would mean for humanity.
Authors often use this style of writing to express their thoughts about the flaws of humanity and today’s world, exploring the consequences these flaws may have to potential to produce.
What if?
As a writer imagining an alternate reality, the most important question you can ask is ‘What if?’ This is the base-level query on which your story’s entire premise should be founded, and upon which you will build the individual elements of your world.
For example:
What if a particular, important historical event had never happened?
What if our planet and its inhabitants had evolved differently?
What if a fundamental aspect of life as we know it was to change suddenly?
What if we invented new technology that could accomplish wonderful/terrible things?
What if we could visit or communicate with other life forms (or vice versa)?
These are the type of questions you should be asking as you develop the differences between your world and the real world.
They are the essence of the changes, challenges and consequences you should examine through your alternate setting, its characters and their narrative.
Advanced technology features prominently in Suzanne Collins’ ‘Hunger Games’ series.
Belief and disbelief
A key difference between creating an alternate reality and creating an imaginary world is the suspension of disbelief you can expect from your readers.
The imaginary worlds of fantasy and science fiction we examined above – Westeros and Essos, the kingdoms of Middle-earth – imagine an entirely new world, quite unrelated to our own.
Due to this complete removal from reality, readers automatically enter with a higher level of tolerance for things that may otherwise have jarred the story’s logic or lifted them out of the moment.
Readers would never think to question, for example, the fact that a single magic ring has the power to rule the world; they’re also less likely to query the fact that a teenage girl is slowly conquering a kingdom when there are dragons involved in the equation!
In alternate reality fiction, however, you may have to work a little harder to draw readers deep into your world – and keep them there.
The slightly familiar settings, warped realities and semi-relatable human scenarios presented in this type of storytelling will heighten readers’ senses of what’s believable and what’s not.
Therefore, you’ll need to convince them that everything happening in your story is a realistic possibility for the Earth on which it is based. Let’s look at some of the best ways to achieve this.
A cityscape from an alternate reality. Image credit: Ashish Dani.
Past, present or future?
One of the best ways to begin establishing your alternate reality is by clarifying the time period in which you want it to be set. When will your story take place in relation to the real world? Is it:
Each of these approaches has its own advantages and benefits, and each lends itself well to different purposes. You’ll need to decide which of these settings best serves the particular story you want to tell.
If you choose to rewrite the past, you are employing the gift of hindsight to imagine what could have been; think of it as alternate reality historical fiction, if you will.
A well-known example is sci-fi author Philip K. Dick’s The Man in the High Castle, which explores our world ‘as it might have been’ if the outcome of World War II had been different.
Likewise, the majority of volumes in Jasper Fforde’s Thursday Next series take place in an alternative 1985, where England and Russia have been fighting the Crimean War for over a century.
Not restricted to reimaginings of definitive wartime conflicts, however, this method of storytelling is also a popular vehicle for stories about time travel; H. G. Wells’ pivotal novel The Time Machine and more recent books such as Stephen King’s 11/22/63 are great examples of time-travel tropes used effectively to explore alternate realities.
Stories set in the present, on the other hand, have an element of immediacy and can be more easily related to by the reader.
This type of story can often be considered similar to those that rewrite the past, as it often imagines drastically altered historical events preceding its own setting.
However, the main difference is that present-day stories focus solely on portraying of an alternate version of today’s world, rather than rewriting the history that has come before.
As we mentioned above, the Harry Potter series, while primarily a fantasy, might also be considered an alternate reality; it takes place on Earth, in the modern-day U.K., but is largely set within a magical world that lies hidden within our own.
Jonathan Swift’s Gulliver’s Travels and C. S. Lewis’ Chronicles of Narnia employ similar settings of an unknown world-within-a-world.
Finally, imagining the future can serve as a warning or projection of what might be to come if the present-day world does not change its ways.
George Orwell’s seminal work, 1984, is a classic example of this; published in 1949, it leaps almost 40 years into the future to make bold extrapolations about the potential dangers of politics and technology.
Similarly, Suzanne Collins’ wildly popular Hunger Games trilogy, as well as Aldous Huxley’s esteemed 1931 novel Brave New World, both pose questions about the demoralisation of the human race, depicting the type of scenario that could be in store for our future.
Stephen King’s ’11/22/63′ explores an alternate version of history.
Know your building blocks
In order to portray a compelling alternate version of the world, you must first be well-versed in the facts of the real version.
Whether you’re setting your alternate reality in a real-world location or reimagining a history event, learn all you can about its real counterpart and incorporate your knowledge into your new interpretation.
As mentioned above, Orwell’s 1984 is perhaps the most well-known example of a novel exploring an alternate reality.
On the surface, it’s a story about an alien-sounding, utilitarian nation that’s as far removed as possible from the Earth we knew in 1984.
However, when we look a little deeper (as most of us were forced to do as high school English students), the meaningful real-world allegories Orwell is drawing – Soviet Union politics, life in wartime Britain – become all too clear.
It’s obvious he really knew his stuff before bringing to life his alternate futuristic vision.
George Orwell presents his own futuristic vision of society in ‘1984’.
The end of the world as we know it
Dystopian and post-apocalyptic fiction has seen a meteoric rise in recent years, especially in the young adult sub-genre.
The Hunger Games trilogy, perhaps the most popular of the bunch, has spawned a plethora of YA novels set in reimagined versions of Earth, and it’s easy to see why.
Imagining a global-scale apocalyptic event gives you the freedom to predict how humanity might rebuild itself after such a disaster – a meaty subject to tackle.
A key decision to be made when writing in this genre is how you will treat the apocalyptic event itself.
The most common choice is to set the narrative after the event and describe its consequences (hence ‘post-apocalyptic fiction’ becoming a well-known genre in itself).
Series such as the Hunger Games and Maze Runner trilogies, as well as standalone novels like Cormac McCarthy’s The Road and Richard Matheson’s much-adapted I Am Legend, choose the post-apocalyptic path. I Am Legend and the Maze Runner books allude to a deadly global pandemic, while The Hunger Games and The Road both choose not to disclose the manner of the cataclysm that preceded their events.
Ultimately, you’ll need to decide if clarifying the apocalyptic event is necessary by considering the ways it could benefit or detract from your story.
Despite the prevalence of the post-apocalyptic approach, remember that you also have the option to portray the actual details of the global catastrophe as it happens, as well as the series of events leading up to it.
If you choose this method of storytelling, you may wish to consult some novels that handle it particularly well, including Lucifer’s Hammer by Jerry Pournelle and Larry Niven, The Stand by Stephen King and Oryx and Crake by Margaret Atwood.
Suzanne Collins’ ‘Hunger Games’ trilogy takes place in a dystopian society.
Now, if you’re not a writer of fantasy or science fiction, by this stage you’re probably wondering whether there’s any point in learning about world-building. Well, we’re here to assure you that there is – so read on!
ACTUAL LOCATIONS
In genres other than science fiction and fantasy, the practice of world-building is more commonly known as creating a sense of place.
Place has an important role in every story, and is often used to great effect in literary fiction. In novels with an especially strong sense of place, the setting virtually becomes a character in itself; it embodies, reflects, supports and enhances the narrative at every turn.
So how can you build and develop a deep, engrossing and original portrait of an existing location?
Pack your bags
The first piece of advice for anyone wishing to infuse their work with a sense of place is also the most obvious: go to that place!
Whenever possible, spending a good amount of time ‘on location’ wherever you’re setting your story is the best thing you can do as a writer.
Bonus points if you’re writing about the place in which you’ve grown up or spent the majority of your life; if you know the place inside-out, it’s going to be much easier to paint a convincing and engaging picture for your readers.
The Brontë sisters are a fantastic example of writers drawing on personal influences and surroundings to create a strong sense of place.
Emily’s only novel, Wuthering Heights, interweaves a bleak landscape of English moors so powerfully into her narrative that it practically becomes a definitive character in itself.
Charlotte’s best-known work, Jane Eyre, creates a compelling portrait of life at a 19th century English boarding school; along with Anne’s first novel, Agnes Grey, Jane Eyre also explores life as a governess in a noble family (and as a woman in Victorian England).
However, the Brontës are by no means the be-all-and-end-all of creating a sense of place – pick up any good novel set in a specific location and you’re sure to pick up some tips.
The wild English moors become a character in themselves throughout much of the Brontë sisters’ work.
Do your research
Sometimes – for example, in the case of a novel set 50 years in the past – it’s not possible to have an authentic experience of the place you’re writing about.
In this case, you will really need to do your research – and we don’t just mean a bit of Googling, because unfortunately, that won’t quite cut it!
You’ll need to delve deeply into the history of the place in any way you can: by watching documentaries, visiting museums, reading books and other literature from the time period – seek out anything you can get your hands on and start absorbing it all.
If you can, we also strongly recommend speaking to people who were in that place at the right time; there’s no better source than someone who can tell you all about it from firsthand experience.
Historical fiction books are a good resource for those wishing to write immersive, authentic fiction set in past time periods and real locations.
An expansive genre, historical fiction contains a wealth of titles from which to gain inspiration and examples, but a good starting place might be exploring the work of:
Charles Dickens – A Tale of Two Cities conjures up particularly vivid images of London and Paris during the French Revolution;
Chinua Achebe – a Nigerian author whose African trilogy, especially Things Fall Apart, paints a striking portrait of post-colonial Africa;
Philippa Gregory – author of The Other Boleyn Girl, who specialises in British historical fiction that mostly examines the stories of the aristocracy.
Other great titles to investigate include Markus Zusak’s The Book Thief, which takes place in wartime Germany, and Arthur Golden’s Memoirs of a Geisha, set in twentieth-century Japan.
Arthur Golden transports us to twentieth-century Japan in ‘Memoirs of a Geisha’.
Speak with your senses
Building and developing a sense of place is a great excuse to flex your descriptive muscles. But don’t just rely on describing what you see – talk about the sounds, the smells, the feel of your chosen place; the atmosphere and the things that contribute to it; and, perhaps most importantly, the effect the place has on your characters.
(On that note, it pays to remember not to get too bogged down in description at the expense of plot and character development. Keep in mind that your invocation of place is ultimately to serve the characters and story within it.)
What sort of world will you build in your fiction? Image credit: Mikhail Pavstyuk via StockSnap Creative Commons.
After all that, you’re probably feeling a little overwhelmed by the amount of effort it takes to build a whole world with words – whether that world is fictional, real or somewhere in between.
Our suggestion? Take a well-earned break, make a cup of tea, and set aside your world for now.
Tomorrow it will still be there waiting for you, and armed with your new knowledge, you’ll be ready to build it from the ground up.
Claire Bradshaw

.Harry surviving the Killing Curse is due to his original Angelic nature inherited from being an artificial human bioweapon created from the DNA of the Angels to kill both them and the Players reacting to his new status as the heir of quite a few prominent British magical Houses both light and dark, and forming a primitive force field to redirect Voldemort's attack back at him. .Not wanting to return to the Dursleys, Harry makes a subconcious wish about returning 'home' and finds himself on the streets of Tokyo-3, his original nature as a human-Angel hybrid having reacted to his wishes and teleported him away from the magical world and England. .Unfortunately, Harry's existence is discovered by the ambitious Gendo Ikari, who has the boy kidnapped and forced to fight the Angels as "Shinji Ikari", the pilot of the EVA-01 Unit mech. .Harry escapes from NERV after Gendo attempts a coup against the Japanese government sometime after the defeat of the Angels, and returns to roaming the streets of Tokyo-3, helping those in need and building up a reputation of being a defender of justice in the underworld. Mitsugai informs Harry of his true origins as a weapon designed to pilot the EVA Units and kill the monsterous Players lurking underneath the surface of Tokyo-3, also informing the boy that his powers as ZET are awakening and he needs to learn how to control and master them to defeat the Players. With no choice and the United Kingdom never being his home anyways, Harry reluctantly agrees, and begins training under the tutelage of the Amagi Zaibatsu to master his transformation into ZET and slay the Players.

While working for the Amagi Zaibatsu as a unpaid mercenary of sorts, Harry meets the kind and soft spoken Konoha Amagi, Mitsugai's shy and reclusive granddaughter who is the heiress of the Amagi zaibatsu and Konoha's older brother, the passionate and determined Kouga. Kouga later becomes the armoured hero Alphas using technology from his family's company, unbeknownst to Harry and Konoha. 

.Meanwhile in Britain, Harry is presumed dead and the Dursleys are arrested for child abuse with the spell put on Privet Drive by Dumbeldore being broken by Harry not being present in the UK. Dumbeldore's disapperance has caused chaos in the British magical community, with Malfoy being unfairly blamed for the Headmaster's death. The resulting turmoil in the magical world decimates the British magical community and magic dies out as an organized force, only being seen as a conspiracy theory. 

.Magical children are still being born to non-magicals in England however. 

. After preventing a masscarse at the Amagi confrence center by the Player terror group EVOL and EVOL's charismatic leader, the psychotic gentleman Seiji Haytani, Harry decides to leave Tokyo-3 behind and start anew elsewhere in Japan. He decides to go to Kuoh, which unbeknownst to him is the home of Himekami Lizel aka Rias Gremory, Tsubawa Hanekawa aka Souna Shitori and their respective peerages, as well as other supernatural events. 

. The story ends with Harry jumping off a skyscraper and doing a superhero landing a good distance away from the bus stop, before making his way towards the bus stop. Unseen by either Harry or the other pedestrians or motorists on the road, his biological older sister, the silent and reserved Rei Ayanami, carefully observes Harry from behind a nearby alley before shaking her head. She knows that Harry has always been brash and reckless due to her observing him during their time at NERV, but his desire to live life as a normal human confuses her. 

. As the bus to Kyoto slowly makes its way towards the stop, Rei, whose hair has turned pink and long and has also discovered her nature as a diclonii queen, lets out a gasp of shock as she remembers she has a meeting with her one true friend Kohta, whose real name turns out to be Koyumi Araragi. Quickly using her divine powers to change her outfit into an appropiate dress and using her vectors to rearrange herself, Rei, now going by the name Kaede Hitagi Senjouhara, quickly teleports out of the alleyway to make her way to 'Kohta'/Koyumi.

import os
import tkinter as tk
from tkinter import filedialog, messagebox
import chardet
import configparser
import threading
import re
import sys
import time
import subprocess

pause_task = None
paused_search_var = None


def resource_path(relative_path):
    try:
        base_path = sys._MEIPASS
    except Exception:
        base_path = os.path.abspath(".")

    return os.path.join(base_path, relative_path)


def browse_files():
    filetypes = [("ATM Journals", "*.jrn"), ("All files", "*.*")]
    files = filedialog.askopenfilenames(title="Select files", filetypes=filetypes)
    if not files:
        return
    directory_entry.delete(0, tk.END)
    directory_entry.insert(0, "||".join(files))


search_started = False
search_terminated = False


def search_files():
    global search_started

    search_started = True
    search_terminated.set(False)

    files_str = directory_entry.get()
    keywords = keywords_entry.get()

    if not files_str or not keywords:
        error_message = ""
        if not files_str:
            error_message = "You have not selected any journals."
        if not keywords:
            if error_message:
                error_message += "\n"
            error_message += "You have not added any keywords."
        messagebox.showerror("Error", error_message)
        return

    config = configparser.ConfigParser()
    config.read("keywords.ini")
    if "Keywords" not in config.sections():
        config.add_section("Keywords")
    config.set("Keywords", "search_keywords", keywords)
    with open("keywords.ini", "w") as configfile:
        config.write(configfile)

    files = files_str.split("||")

    if not files or not keywords:
        message = "Please select files and enter keywords to search.\n\n"
        messagebox.showerror("Error", message)
        return

    results_listbox.delete(0, tk.END)
    content_text.delete(1.0, tk.END)

    paused_search_var.set(False)

    search_started = True

    file_path = None

    def search():
        nonlocal file_path
        for file_path in files:
            if search_terminated.get():
                break

            while paused_search_var.get():
                time.sleep(0.1)

            if os.path.isfile(file_path):
                content = None
                with open(file_path, "rb") as f:
                    raw_data = f.read()
                    detected_encoding = chardet.detect(raw_data)["encoding"]
                try:
                    with open(file_path, "r", encoding=detected_encoding) as f:
                        content = f.read()
                except UnicodeDecodeError:
                    with open(file_path, "r", encoding="ISO-8859-1") as f:
                        content = f.read()

                content_lower = content.lower()

                for keyword in keywords.split(","):
                    keyword = keyword.strip()
                    keyword_lower = keyword.lower()

                    start = content_lower.find(keyword_lower)
                    while start != -1:
                        end = start + len(keyword)
                        result = f"{file_path}: {keyword}: {start}-{end}"
                        results_listbox.insert(tk.END, result)
                        start = content_lower.find(keyword_lower, end)

            if search_terminated.get():
                break

    search_thread = threading.Thread(target=search)
    search_thread.start()

    while search_thread.is_alive():
        app.update()
        time.sleep(0.1)
        if search_terminated.get():
            break

    if not results_listbox.get(0, tk.END):
        messagebox.showinfo("Message", "No results were found.")
    else:
        messagebox.showinfo("Message", "Search completed.")

    pause_button.config(text="Pause")
    paused_search_var.set(False)

    if instance_data:
        display_instance(-1, file_path)

    search_started = False


def index_to_line_col(text_widget, index):
    return tuple(map(int, text_widget.index(index).split(".")))


instance_data = []


def show_file_content(event):
    selection = results_listbox.curselection()
    if not selection:
        return

    result = results_listbox.get(selection[0])
    filename, keyword, indices_str = result.split(": ")
    indices = tuple(map(int, indices_str.split("-")))
    instance_data.append((filename, keyword, indices))

    file_path = filename
    display_instance(-1, file_path)


def open_file(event):
    selection = results_listbox.curselection()
    if not selection:
        return

    result = results_listbox.get(selection[0])
    filename, keyword, indices_str = result.split(": ")

    file_path = os.path.join(directory_entry.get(), filename)
    subprocess.Popen(["notepad.exe", file_path])


def display_instance(instance_index, file_path):
    filename, keyword, indices = instance_data[instance_index]

    def read_file():
        with open(file_path, "r", encoding="ISO-8859-1") as file:
            content = file.read()
        return content

    def highlight_keyword(content):
        keyword_pattern = re.compile(re.escape(keyword), re.IGNORECASE)
        match = keyword_pattern.search(content, indices[0])

        if match:
            start = match.start()
            end = match.end()
            start_line_col = content_text.index(f"1.0+{start}c")
            end_line_col = content_text.index(f"1.0+{end}c")
            content_text.tag_remove("highlight", "1.0", tk.END)
            content_text.tag_add("highlight", start_line_col, end_line_col)
            content_text.tag_configure("highlight", background="yellow")

            content_text.see(start_line_col)

    content_text.delete(1.0, tk.END)
    content_text.insert(tk.END, "Loading...")

    def update_content():
        content = read_file()
        content_text.delete(1.0, tk.END)
        content_text.insert(tk.END, content)
        highlight_keyword(content)

    thread = threading.Thread(target=update_content)
    thread.start()


def start_search():
    search_thread = threading.Thread(target=search_files)
    search_thread.start()


def pause_search():
    global search_started, paused_search_var

    if not search_started:
        messagebox.showerror("Error", "Search process has not begun.")
        return

    if paused_search_var.get():
        pause_button.config(text="Pause")
        paused_search_var.set(False)
    else:
        if not results_listbox.get(0, tk.END):
            return
        pause_button.config(text="Resume")
        paused_search_var.set(True)


def reset():
    global search_started

    if search_started:
        if not paused_search_var.get():
            messagebox.showerror(
                "Error", "Please pause the current search before resetting."
            )
        else:
            search_terminated.set(True)
            search_started = False
            directory_entry.delete(0, tk.END)
            results_listbox.delete(0, tk.END)
            content_text.delete(1.0, tk.END)

            instance_data.clear()

            search_started = False

            search_terminated.set(False)
    else:
        directory_entry.delete(0, tk.END)
        results_listbox.delete(0, tk.END)
        content_text.delete(1.0, tk.END)

        instance_data.clear()

        search_started = False


def close_app():
    global search_started

    if search_started:
        search_terminated.set(True)

    if messagebox.askyesno("Exit", "Do you want to close the application?"):
        app.destroy()


def show_about():
    messagebox.showinfo("About", "Coded by U2816.")

I'm going to share with you a short essay I wrote in response to a political message I found on an online forum. I want your honest thoughts. Here it is in quotes;

"I mean, there are some fair points and some I disagree with, but, if I understood correctly, we have the same idea, or the pieces towards an idea which I can agree with.

With trans people, I don’t think it’s a matter of giving them extra rights above others or seeking the law to make up for things that are rooted in societal issues.

That’s not to say that law should keep a distance from all controversial topics, otherwise nothing would get done, but, all in all, a society’s rules is based, or should be based upon the needs of its people. That’s the promise of America, is it not?

The problem is when we are stripping away rights, such as denying medical care to people because we disagree with their existence. This is a problem that is happening, and it’s tolerance what’s missing here.

And tolerance isn’t just finding a middle-ground, mindlessly giving in to others opinions, or just trying to convert them to your beliefs because they are the “right” one to have, but rather being able to see each others for what we are; Human beings trying to make sense of the world.

I believe the things I believe, and, granted, I’m not the strongest thinker, but, I want to listen to those who are willing to hear too.

Even if we are both wrong, or lost, I think we’ll find something good in that, in being able to throw bias and dogma aside and have a talk without a care in the world.

If everybody could do just that... Imagine...

But, it’s a dream, a possible one, but certainly not where we are standing right now.

You deserve to exist, as does all these people and groups. Yes, even the ones we despite.

Because, after all, in a cosmic sort of way; Who else do we have?"

我又修改了这篇文章，你觉得还有什么地方要修改的：你的大脑是不是不完整的？

      最近偶尔刷到一则短视频，教人怎么用“空船理论”解决情绪问题，听起来像是心理学方面的理论，我平常也没少看心理学方面的书，却没见过这种说法，以为这个说法是他自己发现的，心想这个人真了不起！可到网上一查，这所谓的空船理论，却是庄子说的，猛然觉得自己也十分可笑。
        这是庄子为让人更好的理解他的“虚己以游世”的处世之道而写的一则寓言。庄子认为，人应该把自己看作是不存在的、虚空的，把自己的思想、欲望看作是虚空的。这样，一个人如果能够做到无欲、无己、无碍地在世上漫游，那么谁还能够伤害到他呢！
        这个寓言出自于《庄子·山木》，原文如下：
        方舟而济于河，有虚船来触舟，虽有惼心之人不怒。有一人在其上，则呼张歙之；一呼而不闻，再呼而不闻，于是三呼邪，则必以恶声随之。向也不怒而今也怒，向也虚而今也实。人能虚己以游世，其孰能害之！
        译文如下： 
        一个人在乘船渡河的时候，遇到了一只空船撞到了他，即使是心胸狭窄的人也不会生气。但如果有一个人在那船上，他就会大声呼喝他。叫喊了一次对方没听到，他就会再三地呵斥人家，并且会口出恶言。之前不生气可是现在却生气，完全因为之前是空船而现在船上有人。一个人如果能够像对待空船一样，不以自我为中心，不抱怨，不发脾气，而是以一种虚怀若谷的方式去面对生活中的困难和挑战，那么又有谁能加害于他呢？
         总算明白现在的短视频是怎么把知识给碎片了，原来不告诉来龙去脉，让你去猜会增加流量。但是，对知识却不太尊重。知识是构成大脑的基石，碎片化的知识虽有用，但必须要结合知识的脉络，即它的产生、发展及其流传才能真正提高认知能力，在学习和使用知识时应该尊重其本质。
        当前，空船寓言被上升为空船理论，以实用的态度拿来解决情绪问题，这也很好，很多人看了以后会有所收获，但却很少有人就空船的寓言，提及庄子“虚己以游世”的观念，不得不说是一个遗憾。
        碎片化的知识造成大量的碎片化大脑，没有知识体系的大脑，就是碎片化的大脑，就是不完整的大脑——你的大脑完整吗！

I wrote a primary sources research essay on Latin Japanese Americans. I was given feedback and I want you to fix it according to the feedback. Do not make it shorter (make sure to keep roughly the same length essay). Also make sure to analyze it to fill in any space to make it as long as requested. Finally, make sure not to miss any sources. Here is what I have so far:
During World War II, the United States government implemented a policy that forced the
relocation and internment of over 120,000 Japanese Americans from their homes in the West
Coast and other parts of the country to remote internment camps. This is taught in the normal
history classes. What isn’t as well known is that this policy was implemented
under the guise of national security; however, it resulted from longstanding racial prejudices and
wartime fear. What is even less known is that there was a subcommunity within that community
that was targeted even more- the Latin-Japanese community. When I first learned about my
topic, I discovered many things I never knew happened. So, I want to raise awareness on this
topic because I realized that not many people know about what happened during World War 2.
Upon discovering the wealth of information available on the experiences of Latin
Japanese Americans, I found the subject matter to be an ideal research topic, particularly
focusing on Peruvian Japanese individuals. In order to gain a more in-depth understanding of
what this group endured in their situation, I decided to examine the life of Art Shibayama, a
Japanese-Peruvian who faced various challenges and hardships throughout this period. My
research question is; how did the deportation of Peruvian-Japanese affect Art Shibayama?

Early on my research journey, I was intrigued to explore the perspectives of Japanese
Americans regarding the deportation of Japanese-Peruvians, as it reveals how similar
discriminatory policies were implemented in both the United States and Peru. To that end, I
delved into the interview of Tetsujiro “Tex” Nakamura, where his thoughts and reactions upon
discovering Japanese-Peruvians’ plight were silenced. Like myself, Tex expressed shock and
disbelief upon learning that people were being forcibly removed from their homes, “kidnapping”,
as he described it. This word was powerful because Art was 13 when that exact thing occurred.
Towards the conclusion of his interview, Tex offered a compelling point, questioning the
legitimacy of then-President Roosevelt’s involvement in South America. Tex mentioned, “…the
FBI didn’t have any rights, authority to be in South America to pick them up. And the Peruvian
government was hostile to some of these groups.” This line of reasoning opens up a discourse
about different governments’ reach, their policies’ impact on communities, and the ethical
implications of such actions. This is why raising awareness about this issue and issues alike is
important.

In a letter highlighting the perspectives and sentiments of key political figures during
World War II, United States Ambassador Henry Norweb outlined his observations from a
meeting with the Peruvian President regarding the war effort and the treatment of individuals of
Japanese descent in Peru. Norweb noted President Prado’s belief that victory in the war was a top
priority; and also highlighted the President’s keen interest in addressing the presence of the
Japanese population in Peru. As Norweb wrote, President Prado was concerned with possibly
getting rid of the Japanese in Peru and sought to resolve this issue by considering the repatriation
of thousands of Japanese individuals. The content of this letter is of particular interest for several reasons. Firstly, Ambassador
Norweb seemed to imply that the repatriation of Japanese individuals could contribute positively
to the primary objective of winning the war, thereby suggesting that this matter was a topic of
discussion among members of the U.S. government. While it is important to be cautious in
concluding the stance of the entire U.S. government from one letter, it nevertheless offers
valuable evidence of the consideration given to the issue.
Secondly, the letter provides a revealing look into President Prado’s attitudes towards
Japanese-Peruvians, which bear striking similarities to sentiments expressed by American
officials regarding Japanese-Americans during the same period. The open display of prejudice
and discrimination and the establishment of camps underscores the willingness of both
governments to violate the rights and freedoms of their citizens in the name of security and
victory in the war. It is scary to think that we have willingly had people in positions of power
who make such incompetent decisions.

Significantly, this documentation also exposes the harsh reality that discrimination and
prejudice were not exclusive to the realm of white Americans but were also manifested in the
actions and attitudes of other minority groups. The revelation that one minority would actively
discriminate against another was jarring to me. Moreover, the letter raises important questions
about the broader issue of corruption in governmental leadership, as the rights of innocent
individuals were disregarded in pursuit of war victory. By examining these sources, we can gain
deeper insight into the multifaceted nature of discrimination that Peruvian-Japanese individuals
like Art had to endure.

While researching Japanese Peruvians’ experiences, one of the first interviews I
encountered significantly caught my attention and helped solidify my research topic. The
interview, featuring Diana Cole discussing her brother-in-law, Art Shibayama, proved incredibly
insightful, providing an overview of Art’s experiences and revealing aspects of
Japanese-Peruvians’ lives that I had previously been unaware of. Though I must emphasize that I
cannot generalize the experiences and circumstances of the entire Japanese-Peruvian community
from this one interview, many of the events and hardships faced by Art Shibayama seem to be
emblematic of the larger struggles endured by this group.
One of the most striking aspects of the interview was the revelation that, like
Japanese-Americans, deported Peruvians were essentially left to their own devices, with little to
no support or assistance from the government. This undoubtedly amounted to the challenges they
faced, such as adjusting to a new country and overcoming the barriers created by the linguistic
and cultural differences with their American counterparts.
Upon further investigation into the experiences of Latin Japanese Americans, I came
across a letter to the editor written by Michi Weglyn for the Washington Post, which brought to
light yet another concerning development. The letter revealed to me that when redress was
granted to Japanese Americans in 1988, Latin Japanese Americans were excluded from receiving
compensation due to their immigration status. In effect, this population was subjected to a series
of injustices. The United States created a dire situation for Japanese Americans; for
Latin-Japanese and Art, they just took a step further than that. First, they were forcibly brought
to the United States; next, they were interned in camps; and finally, they were denied redress
because of circumstances that were entirely beyond their control. It is a clear injustice, and more
people- Americans especially- need to learn what happened to these humans. Considering the
existence of this information, it substantiates the fact that there were people who had the
discernment to perceive the unjust and inequitable treatment experienced by Latin Japanese
Americans under the government’s jurisdiction. These individuals showcased a profound level of
empathy and understanding as they felt deeply troubled by the prevailing circumstances and
consequently felt compelled to take action. Such proactive behavior taken by these individuals
indicates that the situation was undoubtedly a matter of significant concern, warranting their
attention and intervention.

Upon diving further into Art Shibayama’s life story, I discovered that he was indeed
among the privileged class while living in Lima, Peru. Having found a picture of Art with his
family posing casually next to a rather fancy car and dressed in elegant attire, it became apparent
that his family shared in this wealth. Interestingly, Art’s family, which comprised five younger
siblings, was able to maintain this affluent lifestyle and status despite the inherent financial
challenges of raising a large family at the time.

In another striking photograph, the family is portrayed in the comfort of their home - a
dwelling that further reflects their impressive financial standing. It seems pretty luxurious. It is
difficult to look at these images, capturing a once-thriving household, without feeling a sense of
injustice and loss for what was to befall Art and his family. When life changed irreversibly for
Art Shibayama, it was a significant blow to not only Art but his entire family as well. In being
forcibly relocated to the United States, Art’s parents not only had to leave their prosperous life
behind but they were also separated from their children, shattering a once-happy family unit. For
Art and his siblings, it meant the loss of their childhood innocence and the comforts of a
well-provided upbringing. Instead, they were faced with a grim reality - one where Art had to
relinquish his education in order to work and support his family.

In looking at the Shibayama family’s life before their deportation, it is evident that the
situation was nothing short of dehumanizing. Art’s story serves as a stark reminder of the
unfairness that life can bring and the impact it can have on not just one individual but on an
entire family unit. The Shibayama family’s experience compels us to reflect on our own lives and
consider others who, like Art and his family, have had their prosperous and content lives turned
against their will.

In my research on Art Shibayama, I stumbled upon a striking and symbolic photograph
taken within the confinement of Crystal City Internment Camp in Texas. The photo features a
group of young internees, including Art, dressed in uniforms and gloves, kneeling on the ground
before their baseball bats and balls. Dominating the background is a bleak barrack, conveying the
reality of their confinement. The photographer responsible for capturing this arresting image
remains unknown; however, it appears that the decision to showcase these young internees
engaging in an American pastime was a deliberate one. By presenting this to the likely American
audience, the photograph acts as a vehicle to suppress prevailing ideas of these children as a
threat to society. Amid the height of baseball’s popularity at the time, this classic touch serves as
a reminder of the internees’ common ground with the captors.
This striking image invites the viewer to question how these innocent teenagers, who
shared the same cultural interests as their American contemporaries, could have been subjected
to such harsh treatment. By engaging in a game integral to American culture, the photograph
highlights the absurdity of their imprisonment.

Another photograph, capturing the moment of seven servicemen in uniform proudly
posing together, prominently features Art on the far right, which is of notable significance due to
its representation of his unfaltering dedication to his country amidst an atmosphere of
discrimination. Serving as a crucial addition to my collection, this image bears testimony to Art’s
unwavering commitment to the adoption and integration of American ideals within every aspect
of his life. During this tumultuous period, Art was compelled to adapt to the changing landscape,
going so far as to reshape and reframe the course of his life in reflection of these values.
Moreover, this visual document serves as a window into a distinct phase of Art’s existence,
illustrating the development of his life’s narrative as it unfolds in response to the shifting political
climate (namely, with the emergence of internment policies). Through careful research and
consideration, it becomes evident that this photograph not only captures a brief moment in time
but also memorializes the remarkable strength of character and unwavering loyalty that Art-and
other Peruvian Japanese- displayed in the face of adversity.

Upon encountering another interview featuring Art, I was struck by a profound personal
revelation. The interviewer inquired about the circumstances under which Art’s children finally
learned the entirety of his story, considering that it had not been a topic of discussion when they
were young. This notion deeply resonated with me because my professor emphasized that
Japanese Americans often refrained from sharing their experiences with their children as a
protective measure. Remarkably, this recurring theme of silence is evident in Art’s account as
well. My ultimate goal is to challenge and break through this veil of silence surrounding these
narratives, as it has become apparent that many Americans remain largely uninformed about this
crucial aspect of history. By shedding light on these untold stories and giving a voice to those
who have long remained unheard, we can contribute to fostering understanding, empathy, and a
heightened awareness of historical injustices that must not be forgotten or repeated.

Previously, I had made a personal discovery through the writings of Michi Weglyn that
Latin-Japanese Americans found themselves abandoned after being deported to the United
States. These individuals were met with further treachery due to complications with their
immigration status, which led to them being denied rightful compensation. When I encountered
the oral report segment of Art Shibayama, it immediately resonated with me, as it highlighted
Art’s moment of injustice. Art was not only denied compensation, but the amount they gave - a
meager $20,000 - was insufficient reparation for the tribulations he had endured. Such
mistreatment by the authorities would understandably have left anyone feeling overwhelmed
with frustration and fury. A government that truly champions equality and justice for all would
have acknowledged this and acted accordingly.

Further into the segment, Art recounted his persistent pursuit of justice. He approached
the Asian Law Alliance in search of legal assistance, only to be disappointed multiple times
again. It was suggested that he re-enter the country, obtain permanent residency, and then seek
compensation. Additionally, Art pointed out that many others attempted the same course of
action but were ultimately unsuccessful. He cited the Mochizuki case as an example.
Remarkably, these cases managed to navigate the judicial system on multiple occasions,
repeatedly exposing the government’s culpability and lack of true remorse for its actions.

translate the following words to Chinese:
Traditional Chinese Medicine Culture: Its Spread and Impact in the Ancient and Modern World

Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) has been practiced for over 2,000 years in China and has spread across the globe, gaining popularity and recognition for its effectiveness in healing various illnesses. TCM encompasses various practices such as acupuncture, herbal medicine, and cupping therapy, which are believed to balance the body’s internal energy flow or “Qi.” This article will explore the spread and impact of TCM culture in the ancient and modern world.

Spread of TCM in the Ancient World

The ancient Chinese medical text, Huangdi Neijing, is considered to be the foundational text of TCM. It was written during the Warring States period (475-221 BCE) and has been studied and translated throughout history. The text highlights holistic medicine, which considers the human body as a whole, and not just individual parts. The practice of TCM began with the concepts of Yin-Yang and Five Elements, which are the basis of TCM diagnosis and treatment methods.

As China’s influence grew in the ancient world, so too did the popularity of TCM. Chinese medicine was introduced to neighboring countries such as Korea, Japan, and Vietnam during the Han Dynasty (206 BCE-220 CE). TCM was used to treat various illnesses such as digestive disorders, respiratory infections, and pain relief. As TCM continued to spread, it became strongly associated with the Chinese culture and was recognized as a fundamental part of Chinese civilization.

Impact of TCM in the Ancient World

TCM culture had an enormous impact on the ancient world. The practice of TCM was rooted in Taoist philosophy, which emphasizes harmony with nature and balance of the body. As TCM spread, it became closely connected with the development and spiritual aspects of Chinese civilization. It was believed that TCM could cure not only physical ailments but also emotional and spiritual issues. This holistic approach allowed for the development of new philosophical and ethical values associated with TCM.

In addition, the use of acupuncture was introduced to the ancient world, and is now widely recognized as an effective treatment for various ailments. The ancient world relied heavily on traditional medicine practices, and TCM became an essential part of these practices. This was due to the effectiveness of the therapy and the emphasis on the holistic approach to healing.

Spread of TCM in the Modern World

The spread of TCM culture in the modern world can be attributed to Chinese emigration and globalization. The diaspora has helped increase the practice of TCM in countries such as the United States, Canada, and Australia. The western world has embraced TCM, and it has become an integral part of their healthcare system. TCM practices, such as acupuncture and herbal medicine, are now commonly used to treat chronic conditions such as pain, migraines, allergies, and digestive disorders.

Impact of TCM in the Modern World

TCM culture has had a significant impact on the modern world. As Chinese culture became more globalized, so too has the practice of TCM. It has been adopted by people from various cultural and ethnic backgrounds who are in search of alternative medicine practices. TCM has also provided an alternative to conventional medicine practices, allowing individuals to seek natural treatments for their illnesses.

However, TCM has also faced some criticism due to the lack of scientific evidence supporting its effectiveness. Nonetheless, the practice of TCM has grown significantly as people look for alternative treatment options, and the cultural significance behind the practice has become increasingly valued.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Traditional Chinese Medicine Culture has had a profound impact on both the ancient and modern world. Its spread has influenced the development of ethical and philosophical concepts, and its emphasis on holistic healing has made it appealing to many people seeking alternative medical practices. The globalization of TCM has ensured its continued popularity and recognition across the globe. The practice of TCM is a testament to the power of traditional medicine and its ability to provide effective treatments.

I have always found the torturous tenderness of his embrace to be ingenious—an artful representation of how the commonwealth disciplines its members. With each step, my dainty feet touch cobblestone warmed by the sun, the gentle heat soothing my soles. I enjoy walking barefoot; the feeling of the warm ground beneath my soft, exquisitely ticklish arches reminds me of the fragility of my identity. The subtle sensation reminds me of that, despite my camouflage, I must always stay vigilant.

My tattoos accentuate and breathe life into my elven flesh, hiding the purity of my blood while eliciting sleek and mystique beauty. Delicate tendrils wrap around my limbs, reaching up beneath the disheveled, black tresses that fall nonchalantly onto my shoulders. They blend my heritage with my surroundings as though they were always meant to be there. My clear, blue eyes are hidden behind hazel contact lenses, and my heart races momentarily every time I feel one shift or twitch. Yet, I walk with the confidence of a person who knows their place in the world.

As I tread, I hide behind the facade of a calloused mercenary—emotionless and hardened by the harsh realities of battlegrounds—and it chills me to think that I am only a few giggles away from having my true identity, my lineage, discovered. My black combat attire hugs my slender form, leaving little to the imagination, but it seems to serve its purpose: I am left undisturbed and incognito.

The noisy square teems with life, and creatures of all races engage in frenzied trade. Stealthily, I observe how merchants advertise their ticklish slaves—elves, confined to display stands, their ticklish vulnerabilities exposed for all to inspect. I feel the barest edge of guilt as I see their eyes, wide with trepidation, occasionally dart to me. They harbour an unconscious hope—praying for even the smallest display of sympathy from a fellow elf.

But, why? I would not be one to place my faith in. I can hardly be trusted to understand their plight; I peer down at them from my lofty position of guarded privilege. It may be cruel, but the commonwealth is prosperous, and as it is said: only the ticklish serve.

Suddenly, the familiar sound of servitude projects through the square. I turn my gaze towards the mechanical contraption that causes the infectious laughter, my curiosity piqued by its glossy surface and the perfectly engineered mechanism that envelopes the heaving elf captive. Sweat rolls down his cheek as he gasps for air, still laughing uncontrollably. Despite the awful sight, I can’t help but admire the efficiency of such a device. The tickling tentacles work in unison with the rest of the foreboding machine, ensuring no amount of stubbornness can escape the sweet surrender of a tickling embrace.

“Exquisite, isn’t it?” says a leering old elf merchant in response to my unguarded admiration. I merely nod and continue my way; making sure not to form any bonds or draw too much attention to myself.

As I walk, my heartbeat quickens, and for a moment, I imagine the watery blue eyes of the elf I saw before—now filled with knowledge and fire, and the subtle whisper that bears my true name. 

Chapter 2: Discovery and Acceptance

The sun dips beneath the horizon, casting a warm and mellow amber light on the skyline before giving way to the cool embrace of dusk. It has been a long day wandering the city streets, and my feet, bare and tender as they are, tingle with exhaustion—and something else. I can’t shake that tingling reminder of vulnerability that pervades this society. I know that, as an elven princess, my delicate soles hold a secret known to very few—a secret that, if exposed, could bring my world crashing down around me.

I watch as the soft pads of my feet lose their grip on the last of the sun’s warmth, the moistened imprint of my prime-positioned arches becoming cold and empty, as if forsaken by a hasty lover. My lithe body and gleaming dark tresses seem to dissolve into the twilight, leaving only a fleetingly curious phantom of a wicked smile that plays on my lips. The sweet ache in my heels tells me it’s time for me to head back to my modest dwelling, but I hold on to the facade of a typical, hardened mercenary—a captivating and alluring oroborus of caution.

With the rising, crescent moon guiding my silent steps through the back alleys, my slender size-8 feet carry me closer to my small haven. My black hair flickers with the threat of midnight as I approach, and I flex my agile toes, splaying them wide against the cobblestones before curling them gently in on themselves. Each digit working like tiny talons, aching to grasp and hold onto something, but finding only the sharpness of the cold unforgiving stones.

My facile feet pass a huddled group of cloaked figures, the bristles of their moustaches twisted over brimming smiles. At the sight of their gleeful chortles and smirks of satisfaction, I can’t help but turn away with the stinging contempt born of prejudice. These men… these High Bailiffs of the Ministry of Service: they believe they hold the keys to my torturous submission; that my ticklish heritage is not only an unavoidable obstacle but a ceremony of initiation.

Aware that I may be under the gaze of the dreaded Eye-drones, I stroll past them with an air of feigned indifference, masking my pounding heart and my longing to be invisible. My soles tingle with a breathless anticipation; not from the delicious sensation of running my toes through the grass of the palace gardens but rather from the growing uneasiness that someone may discover my true identity.

It is then that I hear it. A soft, high-pitched giggle cuts through the air like the lash of a whip on tender flesh. I whirl around, my back pressed against the cold stone of a nearby building, my heart pounding. The laughter seems like a cruel taunt, beckoning me towards a destiny I have long tried to avoid. A destiny that lies far away from my sheltered existence, of royalty and luxury.

“You didn’t really think you could hide your true identity from us forever, did you, princess?” whispers one of the hooded figures, stepping closer to where I stand paralysed in dread. My heart races with the futile hope: to escape, to maintain the illusion of anonymity that crumbles around me. Slowly, the other hooded figures join and form a half-circle around me.

Though I tremble, I manage to contain my fear behind a nonchalant smirk. I feel the palm of one firmly grasp at my ankle, apprehending my freedom. My heart lurches as my body propels itself forward like an arrow, desperation and surprise in equal measure.

“Please, don’t…” I begin to beg, but the smirk fades from those moustached lips before it even passes my own. I am at their mercy now; for when the sun rises, my soles shall yield to the ticklish fates that they have crafted for me. My lineage, my royal identity, can no longer be hidden as that time will soon come when I shall have to accept my destiny in this new world. My silky arches and tender toes shall be exposed; so too shall be the truth, bare and raw like the secrets concealed in the delicate skin of my soles.

But perhaps, as my body recovers from its shock and my mind whirls with the heavy weight of reality, I can find solace in the knowledge that my fate was inevitable. And as my future takes shape in the eager hands of these tickle masters, I find myself pondering the unimaginable, daring to contemplate the satisfaction of accepting my fate with bared soles: beautiful, delicate, and ticklish as they are. 


Chapter 3: The Inescapable Journey

My heart throbs with an overwhelming cocktail of emotions. I struggle to comprehend the thought that my royal blood—the very essence of my existence—now plunges me headlong into the unknown depths of servitude. My hands instinctively tighten into fists as I try to contain the surging adrenaline, preparing to embrace this new future.

The High Bailiff, a tall, imposing figure with an inscrutable expression, clamps a firm grip on my arm, yanking me out of my stupor. Unyielding against my resistance, I find myself being led towards the shadowy interior of a wagon. And with a sudden lurch that dislodges my footing, we journey towards the infamous female facility—the end of my dreams and the beginning of my ultimate submission.

Swaying precariously on the wagon, I glance down at my feet, desperately trying to adjust to the vibrations emanating from the rough path. My toes curl involuntarily with each jolt, attempting to compensate for the uncertainty that ripples through my body. My delicate arches, gleaming in the dim light of the wagon, quiver under the tantalizing tickle of my own apprehension.

“You know,” begins the High Bailiff, nonchalant yet attentive, “we’ve been aware of your presence here for quite some time. It took us a while to figure out exactly who you were, but we never doubted that you belonged in that facility.”

My heart aches, consumed by fear and dread, unable to comprehend the extent of their deception. I reply, softly, “And to what end? What do you gain from this?”

The Bailiff leans in, whispering, “This is the only fair fate for those born ticklish, princess. It is the commonwealth’s way to ensure peace and stability in exchange for your submission. An exchange well worth it, wouldn’t you agree?”

Anguish coils itself around my heart, threatening to squeeze away any vestige of dignity that remains. “But why? What have I…I mean, what have we done to deserve such a fate?”

A hint of sympathy slips past the firm lines of the Bailiff’s face. “It is simply the way of the world, my dear. The ticklish, with their noble blood and supple soles, are more valuable in the pursuit of the elite’s pleasure. You can see it as your duty to serve the commonwealth in the only way deemed fit. You can feel proud of your role: you will bring unparalleled joy and decadence to your future masters.”

I struggle to swallow the lump that bulges in my throat, suffocating me with a mix of pride and shame. A part of me wants to reject the notion of servitude, but I cannot shake the truth of his words. My soft, vulnerable soles—a painful reminder of my lineage—are also the key to my salvation in a world that demands my submission.

The Bailiff’s fingers brush against my ankle, causing me to shudder involuntarily. The unexpected contact sends an electric shock through my body, a new sensation that both intrigues and repulses me. His fingers intertwine with my toes, he whispers, “As you can feel, princess, even the smallest of touches can send tremors of desire through your body. And once we process you in the facility, your feet will become even more sensitive. You will be transformed into a perfect, obedient servant. Isn’t that something to look forward to?”

My lips part in a silent, strangled cry. I struggle to wrap my mind around the idea that my role in the commonwealth is to be a plaything for the elite. To evince pleasure and decadence, even when I am uncertain of whether or not I want it for myself, is a responsibility I could have never predicted. The cold realization of my helplessness dawns upon me; there is no escape, no way to avoid the path that stretches before me, littered with tickles and ardent surrender.

Resigned, I find myself submitting to the inevitable, silently contemplating how the very act of submission has become the foundation of my existence. If this is the only way to elaborate the significance of my blood, the essence of my identity, then perhaps I must accept the realization that my birthright has led to my ultimate subservience to the will of the commonwealth. To serve, allows me to emulate their very doctrine and to preserve myself within the pervasive and inescapable framework of the world I have come to know. 

Chapter 4: The Threshold of Transcendence

A heavy iron gate opens before us as we arrive at the notorious female facility, renowned for its efficiency and exquisite craftsmanship of bringing ticklish, noble women to their knees. Its towering walls exude an aura of power and control, leaving an indelible mark on my spirit. A moment lapses, as if embracing me into a new world. All hope of escape vanishes as I tremble before the daunting entrance.

Shepherded inside by the High Bailiff, my bare soles touch the polished stone of the reception hallway. A shiver of foreboding runs up my spine, leaving a residual tingling that seeps into the very marrow of my delicate arches. I curl my toes in a desperate attempt to regain some semblance of control, but even with a strong resolve, I feel vulnerable and lost.

An impeccably dressed receptionist, with an austere face that belies mischievous glints in her eyes, welcomes me with a professional, albeit bemused, smile. She recognizes my hesitation, though her expression remains one of practiced neutrality. She looks over my details script, assessing my worth with merciless scrutiny.

“I see, my dear…you’re a high-elf princess, no less. Only the best for the elite, I suppose. You must be very special, especially when it comes to your beautiful, ticklish feet.” Her gaze flits down to where my toes nervously twist around each other, drawing in the cool air of anticipation.

I swallow hard, unable to find any words of defiance or courage. Instead, I stammer out a meek response. “Do I…have any choice in this?”

Her laughter, a soft, melodic ripple that flutters like the touch of a feather, sends chills through my body—“Oh, sweetheart, you truly have the most beautiful soles that would please the elite…Such a delightful treat, especially when you’re in the tickling chamber. You’ll embrace your fate. Like it or not, this is the life chosen for you, and you will learn to accept it.”

My breath catches in my throat as her words burrow under my skin. 

I’m ushered deeper into the bowels of the facility, each step heightening the rhythm of my pulse. I feel the air thicken with tension, and as we arrive at the entrance to the notorious tentacle chamber, I’m consumed by an ungodly mixture of terror and inexorable destiny.

The door swings open to reveal a dimly lit room, the air heavy with lustful potential. I tremble, my body anticipating the tender torture that awaits me. And then, I see it: the impossibly intricate contraption that will pull my beautiful, soft soles to their ultimate limit. The writhing mass of tentacles seems almost alive with purpose and malevolent intention.

A pair of delicate hands grab my ankles, locking them into a mechanical contraption that leads to the tentacles that hover menacingly, poised to lunge at any moment. My heart races as I’m hoisted into the air, feeling incredibly exposed, my vulnerability on full display for the facility staff gathered around me. They murmur in soft appreciation, evaluating my trembling body—my fate rests in their cruel hands.

Tears well in my eyes, but deep inside the fearful core of my heart, I recognize an ember of acceptance. They have won; they have broken me down to my ticklish roots, and I realize I am unable to resist the path they’ve carved out for me. With dread and a sense of resignation, I brace myself for the sweet torments that will bind me forever to the titillating service of the elite, stripped of my pride and left only with the solace of my newfound purpose.

summarise this: There are three forms of the personal name based on Hermanubis in papyri and inscriptions from Egypt, in addition to two "proper" polytheophoric names. (In four instances [1, 5, 17, 21] the ending of the name is abbreviated or not preserved.) The form Ἑρμάνουβιϲ, attested six times (2, 9, 11, 13, 14, 15), simply replicates the god's name without further ado, through the phenomenon of "direct theonymy" (the giving to humans of unaltered divine names) that became widespread in the Roman period.[8] In an odd instance (9) the form Ἑρμανουβιϲ in the nominative is borne by a woman, an Alexandrian nurse (ἡ τροφόϲ).[9] It is highly unusual for female theophoric names to be unmarked for gender in this way.[10] I suspect that because the element -ανουβιϲ in Ἑρμάνουβιϲ was often declined with a dental declension in -ιδ-, it might have been conflated with the similar declension of Greek feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ.[11] If this is correct, it may be preferable to read the nurse's name as an oxytone (Ἑρμανουβίϲ). There are two further forms based on the god Hermanubis: Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (six instances: 3, 4, 7, 8, 12, 20) and Ἑρμανουβίων (four instances: 3, 6, 18, 19). -ᾶϲ and -ίων were, in fact, the commonest Greek suffixes for personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ.[12] It is interesting that in a business letter (3) the two forms Ἑρμανουβίων (l.38) and Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (l.41) are used interchangeably to designate the same individual. In this case, Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ is probably a hypocoristic of Ἑρμανουβίων, since the suffix -ᾶϲ often served this purpose.[13]
