Modern cardiac surgery requires a wide range of pharmacology perioperatively and would be impossible without many currently available medications. Patients may receive several therapeutic agents throughout the perioperative period that affect cardiovascular (CV), pulmonary, and hematological functions (Rivera et al., 2021). Accordingly, members of the cardiac surgery team require an acute understanding of the available pharmacologic agents to safely manage patients and promote good outcomes. In order to facilitate the knowledge acquisition of common medications used in cardiac surgery, the medication profile of a fictitious surgical case study will be analyzed below, chronicling a patient’s medical journey – prehospital and perioperatively (emergency department, operating room, intensive care unit, and discharge).
Case Study & Patient Health History
The following case report describes a 23-year-old male trauma patient that suffered an unfortunate sporting accident causing cardiac arrest, presumably due to commotio cordis, and further resulting in DeBakey type II aortic dissection, requiring emergency aortic surgery (Bentall procedure) with cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB).
The accident occurred during a competitive university hockey game when the young man was quickly skating up the ice when an opposing player suddenly elbowed him extremely hard at a high rate of speed, with the principal point of contact being his sternum. The young man collapsed to the ice and went into immediate cardiac arrest from presumed commotio cordis (Link, 2012). Team trainers immediately initiated cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) and defibrillation. Ground paramedic services quickly responded to the call and assumed care. Defibrillation was eventually successful, and the patient was intubated for airway protection and transported to the nearest trauma facility. Unfortunately, the patient suffered a DeBakey type II aortic dissection requiring surgical intervention. Following a complicated aortic surgery, the patient had an uneventful postoperative recovery and was eventually discharged home.
The patient is 23 years old (182 cm, 83 kg) with a medical history of juvenile diabetes (Type I), managed with an insulin pump; he was also diagnosed with mild type 1 Von Willebrand Disease (VWD), and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome in his youth. The patient has never been previously hospitalized, and the only medication he takes is insulin (fast-acting insulin aspart via an insulin pump, variable dosing [units/hr])(Berget et al., 2019).
Patient Medication Profile
Prehospital
Advanced cardiac life support (ACLS) protocols were initiated when paramedics assumed patient care CPR efforts were continued; simultaneously, intraosseous (IO) access was obtained to administer resuscitation medications and intravenous (IV) fluids (American Heart Association [AHA], 2020). A 1 mg IO dose of rescue epinephrine was administered to the patient to stimulate sympathetic adrenergic cardiac activity (beta1 receptors) to facilitate electrical rhythm conversion and resuscitation (London Health Sciences Center [LHSC], 2023). A 1 L IO fluid bolus of Lactated Ringer’s (LR) crystalloid was also provided to help circulate the epinephrine and support the patient’s cardiovascular circulation and blood pressure (BP). Defibrillation attempts were repeated every two minutes per ACLS guidelines until the return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) was obtained (AHA, 2020); obtunded consciousness and ROSC were achieved following the fourth defibrillation attempt.
To protect the patient’s airway, paramedics elected to intubate the patient. Rapid sequence intubation was utilized using the following medications: 25 mcg of fentanyl (opioid analgesic) was provided for pre-induction analgesia, a low dose due to hypotension with mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 58 mmHg post-ROSC, and a combination of ketamine use (also having analgesic properties); 100 mg (1-2 mg/kg) ketamine IO (general anesthetic) was administered for primary induction agent; and 100 mg (0.6-1.2 mg/kg) rocuronium IO (non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocker) to facilitate airway relaxation for endotracheal tube insertion (LHSC, 2023). These medications provided the five critical components of general anesthesia (hypnosis, amnesia, analgesia, inhibition of autonomic and sensory reflexes, and muscle relaxation)(McNair, 2023). Following intubation, the patient was started on a 1.2 mg/kg continuous infusion of ketamine (1-5 mg/kg) for maintenance of sedation.
On transport to the hospital, the patient continued to be hypotensive (MAP 59 mmHg) and was in a sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 127 beats per minute (BPM); another 1 L of LR was administered to increase blood pressure, and a low dose of continuous IO norepinephrine infusion was started at 6 mcg/min (sympathomimetic inotrope/vasopressor) to simulate beta1 receptors to increase myocardial contractility and peripheral alpha1 vascular receptors to vasoconstrict, which increased the patient’s BP to a MAP of 66 mmHg (LHSC, 2023). The patient was successfully transported to the nearest trauma centre.
Emergency Department
Following arrival to the emergency room and brief neurologic assessment, the patient’s continuous IV sedation was changed based on institution protocols: propofol (general anesthetic, 1-5 mg/kg/hr) was started at 1.5 mg/kg/hr for maintenance of sedation, and 1 mg/hr hydromorphone (opioid analgesic, 0-5 mg/hr) was initiated for analgesia. Norepinephrine IV infusion was continued at 10mcg/min to increase MAP to 70 mmHg. Vasopressin (vasopressor, 0.9-2.4 units/hr) continuous IV infusion was also started at 1.8 units/hr for persistent hypotension to induce renal vasoconstriction (V1) and water reabsorption (V2)(LHSC, 2023). In addition, right radial arterial BP monitoring was established, and right internal jugular central venous access was inserted for medication and fluid administration.
Patient bloodwork (ABG, CBC, electrolytes, INR/PTT, fibrinogen) was completed following arterial and central line insertion (Appendix, Table 1), and reported low hemoglobin (69 g/L) and hematocrit (20.7 %), which was concerning for internal bleeding and hemodilution related crystalloid fluid resuscitation; mild prolonged coagulation studies (INR 1.3, PTT 47) were also noted, likely related to the patient’s VWD history, and reflecting a degree of trauma-induced coagulopathy (TIC) (Kornblith et al., 2019). Arterial blood gas demonstrated a mixed metabolic acidosis with a plasma lactate of 3.1 mmol/L. Two units of packed red blood cells (PRBC) were administered at this time for volume resuscitation and suspected bleeding; the patient was rushed to the CT (computed tomography) suite for emergent imaging.
CT Suite.
Several concerning findings were noted on the CT scan, including an acute ascending aortic dissection (DeBakey Type II), bilateral rib fractures, right pneumothorax, and myocardial contusions. As the patient was removed from the CT scanner, his BP precipitously dropped (MAP 50 mmHg) and was not responding to increased vasopressors (norepinephrine at 28 mcg/min, vasopressin 2.4 units/hr). The emergency nurse administered another 1 L LR fluid bolus and pushed 200 mcg IV phenylephrine (alpha1 adrenergic agonist vasopressor), which caused peripheral vasoconstriction and increased the patient’s MAP to 54 mmHg (LHSC, 2023). The emergency physician ordered a massive transfusion protocol, and blood quickly arrived at the CT suite; two more units of PRBC were immediately administered, and the patient’s MAP increased to 63 mmHg. Cardiovascular Surgery was emergently consulted, and the patient’s parents provided emergency telephone consent for a Bentall procedure for ascending aorta replacement; the patient was brought directly to the operating room (OR) for aortic surgery.
Operating Room 
On arrival to the OR, 1g (5-20 mg/kg) IV tranexamic acid (hemostatic agent) was administered to the patient in anticipation of potential significant hemorrhage (Vallerand, 2023); 1g IV cefazolin (cephalosporin) was also provided for prophylactic anti-infective purposes (Vallerand, 2023). Anesthesia medications were also adjusted in preparation for surgery: propofol was increased to 3 mg/kg/hr; hydromorphone infusion was exchanged with a 1.5 mcg/kg/hr fentanyl infusion for improved intraoperative analgesia; a rocuronium infusion was also started (1.2 mg/kg/hr) to maintain muscular paralysis to facilitate surgery (Vallerand, 2023); Sevoflurane (0.5-2%)(inhaled general anesthetic) was also available during CPB for additional sedation and management of intraoperative hypertension if required (DrugBank Online, 2023). The anesthetist administered a loading dose of 35 000 IU (400 IU/kg) of unfractionated IV heparin sulphate for systemic anticoagulation for CPB (LHSC, 2023); the perfusionist primed the CPB circuit with 1 L Plasmalyte A, 10 000 IU heparin, and three units of PRBC (calculated on-pump hematocrit 17%); anesthesia also administered PRBC to the patient. Due to patient instability, the team elected not to wait for an activated clotting time (ACT)> 480 seconds to initiate CPB. Once the perfusionist confirmed that anesthesia administered heparin, emergency CPB was initiated following one minute of heparin circulation. The ACT peaked at >999 seconds during the case due to the large heparin bolus, VWD, and TIC (Kornblith et al., 2019). 
Intraoperatively, the patient experienced significant hypotension and vasoplegia, in addition to anemia from moderate blood losses, requiring multiple blood transfusions throughout the case (two units PRBC, two units fresh frozen plasma (FFP), 1 unit of platelets, and 200 mL 25% albumin) to maintain oxygen carrying capacity, coagulation factors, and BP; hemodynamics were also supported with IV vasopressors (norepinephrine 26 mcg/min, vasopressin 2.4 units/hr, and CPB push-dose phenylephrine (100-200 mcg increments) to keep patient MAP > 55 mmHg.
Bloodwork was repeated intraoperatively (Appendix, Table 2) and demonstrated persistent metabolic acidosis, rising lactate, low venous oxygen saturation, and hypocalcemia; the perfusionist increased CPB flows, continued to monitor ionized calcium levels (which would be corrected following cross-clamp removal), and administered 100 mEq of sodium bicarbonate IV (alkalinizing agent) for severe metabolic acidosis (BE -10.6 mEq/L), which improved the patient’s acidosis (Vallerand, 2023). 
The perfusionist rewarmed the patient following aortic repair; during this time, they administered several anesthesia maintenance medications (50 mcg IV fentanyl for analgesia, 2 mg IV midazolam [benzodiazepine] for sedation, and 50 mg IV rocuronium for pharmacologic paralysis). When the cross-clamp was removed, the perfusionist administered 2 g IV magnesium sulphate (membrane stabilizer) to prevent fibrillation and 1 g IV calcium chloride for its positive inotropy effects and treatment of hypocalcemia. Weaning from bypass was complicated by severe myocardial stunning, requiring intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) initiation (via left femoral artery), in addition to a 10 mcg/kg/hr continuous IV infusion of dobutamine (positive inotrope) to improve cardiac contractility and cardiac output (Vallerand, 2023). Once CPB was discontinued, 350 mg IV protamine sulphate (heparin reversal antidote, 1 mg/100 IU heparin) was administered to reverse the effects of heparin (LHSC, 2023); an ACT of 150 seconds was achieved. A low-dose heparin anticoagulation protocol (aPTT 50-64s) was then initiated at 6 units/kg/hr (IV continuous infusion) for IABP therapy (Figure 1)(LHSC, 2023). The patient was then transported to the cardiovascular intensive care unit (CVICU) for recovery. 
Figure 1. Low-Dose Anticoagulation Protocol (LHSC, 2023).
 
Postoperative Recovery 
Cardiovascular Intensive Care Unit.
On CVICU admission, the patient’s hemodynamic support requirements continued to be high, requiring multiple IV vasopressors (norepinephrine 19 mcg/min, vasopressin 2.4 units/hr), inotropes (dobutamine 10 mcg/kg/min), and IABP (heparin at 6 units/kg/hr). Patient sedation was managed with continuous IV propofol (1.5 mg/kg/hr) and hydromorphone (1 mg/hr). Sedation, vasopressor, and ventilator support were weaned and discontinued as the patient tolerated clinically. Subcutaneous dalteparin (5000 units once daily), a low molecular weight heparin (LMWH), was administered to the patient for prophylaxis of venous thromboembolism following discontinuation of the IABP and IV heparin. The patient received daily subcutaneous LMWH until discharged (LHSC, 2023). 
The patient experienced rapid atrial fibrillation postoperatively, for which IV amiodarone (antiarrhythmic) was administered; a 150 mg IV loading bolus over ten minutes, followed by a 60 mg/hr continuous IV infusion for the following 48 hours, which eventually converted the patient back into a sinus rhythm (LHSC, 2023). In addition to atrial fibrillation, the patient also experienced postoperative fluid overload and pulmonary congestion, for which 40 mg IV direct furosemide (loop diuretic) was provided every eight hours and facilitated urinary diuresis and excretion of excess vascular volume (Vallerand, 2023). Inhaled salbutamol (inhaled bronchodilator) 6 puffs every four hours while invasively ventilated was provided to mitigate atelectasis and ventilator-associated pneumonia, and keeping the head-of-bed greater than 30 degrees (Dhand, 2017); ventilator support was reduced with these strategies. Continuous IV insulin regular (antihyperglycemic hormone) was provided to the patient to maintain blood glucose levels of 4.5-6.5 mmol/L; the initial starting dose was 2 units/hr and was titrated with frequent blood glucose checks to avoid potential hypoglycemia (LHSC, 2023). 
A nasogastric feeding tube was inserted on ICU arrival to administer enteral nutrition and oral medications to the patient until extubated. Oral medications the patient received during ICU stay included: metoprolol (beta-blocker) 25 mg every 12 hours to reduce BP and prevent aortic graft stress; amlodipine (calcium channel blocker) 10 mg once daily to aid in further BP reduction; atorvastatin (statin) 40 mg once daily to facilitate the management of cholesterol and BP; quetiapine (antipsychotic) 25 mg oral every eight hours was administered for its sedative effects to facilitate weaning from IV sedation and invasive ventilation; daily 81 mg aspirin (antiplatelet agent) therapy was started for its antiplatelet activity to prevent platelet aggregation to Gore-Tex vascular aortic graft (following IABP discontinuation), and lansoprazole (proton pump inhibitor) 30 mg once daily while in-hospital for prophylaxis of gastric stress ulcers (stress response to critical illness) was also provided (De Martino et al., 2014; Vallerand et al., 2023). The patient was extubated after 48 hours and transferred to the inpatient cardiovascular ward following another two days in ICU. Daily blood work was also completed in ICU; electrolytes and hematology were stabilized before the patient was transferred to the inpatient ward (Appendix, Table 3). 
Discharge
The patient spent four additional days in the inpatient cardiovascular ward before being discharged home. The patient was alert and mobilizing daily, all body systems were functioning, and bloodwork was within normal ranges. The patient was provided with the following medication scripts upon discharge: metoprolol 12.5 mg every 12 hours, amlodipine 10mg once daily, aspirin 81 mg once daily, 500 mg acetaminophen (nonopioid analgesic, antipyretic) for analgesia, 1 mg hydromorphone every 6 hours as needed for breakthrough pain, and lactulose (osmotic laxative) 30 mL (20 g)(as needed every hour until effect) was also prescribed to prevent constipation from opioids and to prevent stress on the aorta and graft (Vallerand, 2023). The patient was warned not to exceed 4g of acetaminophen daily due to the risk of overdose and liver injury, in addition to the potential hazards of opioids, including overdose and the possibility of dependence. 
Conclusion
The success of patients undergoing cardiac surgery in the modern era heavily relies on the use of a broad range of pharmacologic agents throughout the perioperative period. The patient case study illustrated in this report emphasizes the critical role of pharmacology in managing patients undergoing CV surgery. The patient was administered personalized medications from the prehospital phase to discharge home to meet their physiological and perioperative needs. It is crucial for the cardiac surgery team to possess a thorough understanding of available pharmacologic agents to ensure safe and effective patient management. Healthcare professionals must stay informed of the latest CV medications and their appropriate indications to continue providing the best possible patient care. While drugs such as beta-blockers, vasopressors, inotropes, antiplatelets, and anticoagulants are essential in cardiac surgery, their administration may lead to side effects and complications that the surgical team must manage. Thus, having comprehensive knowledge of CV medications is critical for promoting positive surgical outcomes and providing optimal care to patients throughout the perioperative period.

summarize the paragraph below into the respective headings

I. Introduction

Market failure is defined as a situation where the allocation of goods and services by free markets is not efficient. The causes of market failure stem from the inability of free markets to efficiently allocate resources, which influences detrimental effects on society. Understanding the various types and causes of market failure is essential for policymakers to make better decisions in correcting market inefficiencies and achieve optimum resource allocation.

II. Externalities

Externalities refer to the spillover effects of a market transaction on a third party not involved in the transaction. Positive externalities occur when the third party benefits from the transaction, while negative externalities occur when the third party incurs costs (Ayres and Kneese, 1969).

For instance, education and vaccination both generate positive externalities as they not only benefit the individuals receiving them but also lead to better societal outcomes. Conversely, negative externalities are exemplified by the use of fossil fuels for energy production, which adversely impacts the environment and public health (Ayres and Kneese, 1969). Externalities can lead to suboptimal market outcomes as producers do not account for the social costs or benefits of their actions.

Policy solutions such as the Pigouvian tax can be enacted to mitigate negative externalities by charging a tax on the producer equal to the social cost incurred by society, thus internalizing the externality and correcting market inefficiency.

III. Public Goods

Public goods are non-excludable and non-rivalrous, meaning that no one can be excluded from using the good, and one individual’s use does not diminish the availability of the good for others. Examples of public goods include national defense and scientific research.

The provision of public goods is prone to the free-rider problem, wherein individuals benefit from the good without contributing to its provision. This leads to an under provision of public goods in the market as private firms find it unprofitable to supply them. Policy solutions include the government directly providing or subsidizing public goods to ensure their efficient provision to society.

IV. Imperfect Information

Imperfect information refers to the incomplete knowledge that market participants possess about a good or service. It can result in inefficient allocation of resources as consumers make suboptimal decisions due to a lack of accurate information. An example is the health risks associated with consuming certain products, such as tobacco or processed foods (Hackett and Dissanayake, 2014).

Imperfect information can lead to market outcomes that are not socially optimal, as consumers may over-consume harmful goods or under-consume beneficial ones. Policymakers can address this by implementing measures such as labeling requirements or advertising regulations to improve consumer information and correct market inefficiencies.

V. Market Power

Market power refers to the ability of a firm to influence market prices and manipulate market conditions to its advantage. A firm with significant market power can restrict output, charge higher prices, and extract more surplus from consumers (Tietenberg and Lewis, 2016). For example, pharmaceutical companies can charge high prices for life-saving drugs due to their market power, often gained through patents and regulatory barriers.

Unchecked market power can lead to an inefficient allocation of resources, high prices, and reduced consumer welfare. Antitrust laws and other regulatory measures can be implemented to regulate market power and improve market competitiveness.

VI. Conclusion

Market failures, such as externalities, public goods, imperfect information, and market power, negatively impact market outcomes and can lead to an inefficient allocation of resources. Government intervention through policies and regulations is often necessary to mitigate these failures and achieve more socially optimal outcomes. Further research should focus on the design and implementation of effective policy measures to address market failures while minimizing unintended consequences and inefficiencies.

10 Lesser-Known Versions Of Famous Mythological Beings
by
Vlad Vekshtein
fact checked by Jamie Frater
Share553TweetWhatsAppPin91Share1Email645 SharesLet’s face it—in the world of mythological creatures, anything can happen. We’ve gone from vampires who eat their funeral shrouds to green hags who like to drown the young and old. And sometimes, we get offshoots. Well, not offshoots per se, but strange and peculiar versions of creatures and beings we know and love. Prepare to wonder why the heck we didn’t know about these versions any sooner. Quite frankly, some of these guys are a bit freaky.
10 Barbegazi
Swiss/French Folklore
Quick—what do you know about gnomes (aside from being tiny men in colorful pointed hats found in the gardens of retirement communities)? Actually, that’s all there is to them nowadays—short guardians of the garden. Their Swiss (or French) cousins, however, are much more interesting.
Found in The Fantasy Encyclopedia by Judy Allen, the Barbegazi is a short gnome of winter, who only comes out of his mountain home when snow begins to fall. The Barbegazi has many unique features, including an icy-white beard and enormous feet. Why the enormous feet? Well, they act like snowboards—Barbegazi like to slide down avalanches on their feet as a pastime. These much more interesting gnomes aren’t all fun and games: if they find someone trapped in the snow, they’ll make loud whistling noises to alert other people to the victim’s location, or try to dig them out if no one is around. Take that, garden-variety gnomes!
9 Jiang-shi
Chinese Mythology
I’ve made it pretty clear on some of my previous lists that there are still pretty awesome vampires out there—from Germany’s resident Nachzehrer who sucks the life out of his family by eating his funeral shroud to the witch/vampire of India that sucks the blood from toes. Here, we have a gift from China, the Jiang-shi. The Jiang-shi is the “hopping vampire,” a being of evil created either when a corpse is tainted by dark magic or the deceased was wronged in some way during his life. The Jiang-shi seeks to suck the qi, or life energy, from people and can be deterred by literally spilling rice. The Jiang-shi must stop following you and count the rice—every grain—before continuing. Who knew hopping corpses were so meticulous?
8 Tokoloshe
Zulu Mythology
In European mythology we typically have creatures like goblins and imps that are mischief-makers, but Europe isn’t alone in this, of course. The Tokoloshe is a creature from Zulu mythology, small and mischievous, created by a witch doctor to harm innocent families. The Tokoloshe’s activites range from pranks and mischief to rape and eating toes, and there are still cases of people attacking innocents believing they are manifestations of the Tokoloshe.
7 Orthrus
Greek Mythology
In classical mythology, the great hero Heracles (or Hercules to the Romans) was assigned Twelve Labors to complete without fail. His final Labor, and possibly the scariest, was to capture the dog who guarded of the gate of the underworld, Cerberus. Heracles succeeded, and Cerberus was eventually returned to Hades. So what does Cerberus have to do with anything? Nothing—it’s his brother we’re interested in. Yes, the big, scary, three-headed hound had a little brother. Orthrus was, like his brother, the son of the “father of monsters,” Typhon. Also, like his brother, he fell victim to Heracles. You see, Orthrus was the guard dog of the cattle of the giant Geryon. So when Heracles came to the Tenth Labor, which was to get these cattle, he killed Orthrus to get to them. Oh those poor demonic dogs . . .
6 Were-Hyena
African/Arabian Mythology
“Were” is an archaic Germanic term for the word “adult male.” Many different cultures have the concept of a were-creature—southeast Asia has the were-tiger, the Aztecs had the were-jaguar, and peoples of the Sahara believed in a being known as the butungin, a Kanuri word meaning “I change into a hyena.” It was believed that Ethiopian Jews were were-hyenas that dug up the bodies of Christians as meals. The creature can also be found in medieval bestiaries, along with numerous other bizarre beasties.
5 Hel
Norse Mythology
Plenty of mythologies have guardians and rulers of the realms of the dead and underworld: the Greek Hades is countered by the Roman Pluto and the Egyptian Anubis. Norse mythology, of course, has its own guardian at the gate. Her name is Hel—yes, Hel—and she presides over the realm of the dead also known as Hel, but to avoid confusion some scholars prefer the name “Helheim.” She guards the realm of the dead with her forever blood-soaked dog Garmr and makes sure that the souls of the dishonored and those undeserving of Valhalla stay there.
4 Karzelek
Polish Mythology
The Karzelek is the dwarf’s lesser-known Polish cousin. The Karzelek is the guardian of valuable ores, gems, and the veins of precious materials in the mountain mines he lives in. This friendly little fellow will lead miners away from danger and may help them find veins of value within the mines themselves. They aren’t completely friendly, though—if you disrespect one in some way (swearing, hurling stones), it may lead you to your doom by throwing you into dark chasms or causing collapses in the mines. Funnily enough, many European countries have equivalents to dwarfs—the Germans have a creature known as the kobold, and if you live in Cornwall you may know it as the knocker. Strange how mythology changes throughout the ages and across seas and borders, huh?
3 Sekhmet
Egyptian Mythology
Gods and goddesses of war are prevalent throughout mythology, from Ares to Thor to the winner of this spot on the list, Sekhmet. Although her job was guarding the disc of the sun, she was considered a symbol of war and battle, and she was feared and respected during Ancient Egyptian times. One legend tells of how, after a mighty battle, Sekhmet’s bloodlust was not satisfied and she began to kill and attack throughout the land. To appease her, the gods sent her a potion that resembled blood that would pacify her, and only then was Sekhmet’s rampage stopped.
2 Ro-Langs
Tibetan Mythology
The Tibetan version of the zombie, the Ro-Langs can be either good or evil, but it’s the evil ones you obviously need to look out for. The Ro-Langs that are evil are independent of any dark magic or evil sorcerer—their goal is to turn other people into Ro-Langs. If you are touched by a Ro-Lang, you will become one of them, a horrible being who cannot bend over or speak—your tongue’s movement is the only means of communication. Beats any brain-eater I know.
1 Czernobog
Slavic Mythology
All mythologies have some kind of god who receives a negative connotation these days. The Greeks had Hades (who wasn’t necessarily evil, but he did do some questionable things) and the Egyptians had Set, but you probably haven’t heard of the Slav’s god of evil. What is probably the pre-Christian Slavic version of the Devil or demons, Czernobog—or Chernobog—was considered the god of evil and the dead, a sadistic soul who enjoyed the pain of others. Although we actually know little about him, his existence was popularized by the novel American Gods and he even appears in Disney’s Fantasia. If you’ve seen that film, you probably know what he looks like—the enormous devil dude who summons the dark spirits? Yup, that’s him. Enjoy your nightmares. His name, by the way, means “the black god.”
Vlad Vekshtein is an eccentric writer, sci-fi geek and mythology buff. He’s trying to set up a blog with all his brains in it and hopes he doesn’t anger any creatures that may turn out not to be mythological. He’s always on the lookout for new list ideas, so sound off in the comments!

Een staande ovatie en een daverend applaus, dat is wat de zwangere zangeres Floor Jansen zaterdagavond kreeg als bedankje voor haar show in het voorprogramma van de metalband Metallica. Als ze na haar optreden weer backstage gaat, lopen de tranen over haar wangen. ‘Dit was prachtig.’

Niet Five Finger Death Punch, maar Floor Jansen trad op zaterdag in de Johan Cruijff ArenA op in het voorprogramma van Metallica. De Amerikaanse metalband moest ‘helaas door onvoorziene omstandigheden’ afzeggen. In een reactie zei Jansen eerder het ‘een enorme eer’ te vinden om zaterdag van zich te mogen laten horen. ,,De mogelijkheid om voor meer dan 50.000 fans in de Johan Cruijff ArenA in Amsterdam te spelen in het voorprogramma van Metallica, is een enorme eer. Ik kan niet wachten om mijn muziek met de fans te delen en Metallica te zien optreden.”

Het werd een bijzonder avond voor de Nightwish-zangeres. ,,Vorig jaar kreeg ik borstkanker. En nu ben ik zwanger’’, zei ze aan het eind van haar show, waarna een staande ovatie volgde. Als Jansen vervolgens onder leiding van een daverend applaus terug backstage loopt, is ze zichtbaar geëmotioneerd. ,,Het was prachtig’’, wist ze nog net onder woorden te brengen. ,,Ik had niet verwacht dat ik zo'n warm welkom zou krijgen.’’ Verwijzend naar haar zwangerschap zei ze: ,,Als ik nu kon drinken, had ik een fles gedronken.’’
Kadootje

Ook op sociale media zijn fans lyrisch over het optreden van de zangeres. ‘Je hebt de Arena gewoon platgespeeld en -gezongen. Wat een geweldig optreden’, vindt iemand. ‘Dit was het kadootje van de avond. Ik heb een ge-wel-dig weekend gehad!’ schrijft een ander. ‘Wat een powervrouw en wat een stem.’

Het was een bewogen jaar voor Jansen. Ze onderging vorig najaar een operatie nadat bij haar borstkanker was vastgesteld. Artsen verwijderden toen een tumor en een lymfeklier bij de metalzangeres. Uit nader onderzoek bleek dat de ziekte daarmee volledig uit haar lichaam was. Eind november stond ze weer op het podium. Vorige maand maakte ze bekend zwanger te zijn van haar tweede kindje. ‘Het leven is mooi!’ schreef ze destijds op social media.

Introduction:

The Arctic Cat Alterra 700 is a versatile and powerful all-terrain vehicle (ATV) that offers impressive performance for outdoor enthusiasts. However, like any vehicle, it can encounter some problems over time. In this article, we will discuss five common issues that Arctic Cat Alterra 700 owners may face, along with easy fixes to address them. By following this guide, you can save time and money on repairs, keeping your ATV in optimal condition for your outdoor adventures.

I. Problem 1: Overheating

Overheating is a common issue that can cause significant damage to your ATV's engine if left unaddressed. When the engine temperature warning light illuminates, you may also experience a loss of power or notice steam or coolant leaking from the radiator.


A. Causes and Fixes

Low Coolant Level: Ensure that the coolant level is within the recommended range. If the coolant level is low, refill it with the appropriate type and amount of coolant specified in your owner's manual.

Clogged Radiator: Inspect the radiator for debris or dirt that might be obstructing airflow. If necessary, clean the radiator with a hose or compressed air to remove any obstructions.

Malfunctioning Thermostat: Test the thermostat for proper operation. If it is not functioning correctly, replace it with a new one.

Faulty Water Pump: Inspect the water pump for any signs of leakage or damage. If the pump is not functioning properly, it will need to be replaced.

II. Problem 2: Stalling or Poor Performance
Stalling or poor performance can be frustrating, especially when you're in the middle of an off-road adventure. Symptoms of this issue include the engine stalling or hesitating during acceleration, rough idling, and decreased fuel efficiency.

A. Causes and Fixes


Dirty or Clogged Air Filter: Regularly inspect the air filter and clean or replace it as needed. A clean air filter ensures that your engine receives an adequate supply of clean air for optimal performance.

Fouled Spark Plug: Remove and inspect the spark plugs for signs of wear or damage. Replace any fouled or damaged plugs with new ones according to the manufacturer's specifications.

Fuel System Issues: Clean the fuel injectors and check the fuel system for leaks. If you notice any leaks, have them repaired by a qualified technician.
III. Problem 3: Electrical Issues

Electrical issues can lead to a dead battery, difficulty starting the engine, and malfunctioning accessories. To avoid being stranded in the wilderness, it's essential to address electrical problems as soon as they arise.

A. Causes and Fixes

Faulty Charging System: Test the battery and charging system using a multimeter. If the battery is not charging properly, it may need to be replaced or the charging system may require repairs.

Damaged Wiring or Connections: Inspect the wiring and electrical connections for signs of damage, corrosion, or wear. Repair or replace any damaged components as necessary.

Failing Electrical Components: Test all electrical components, such as relays, switches, and sensors, for proper function. Replace any faulty components as needed.

IV. Problem 4: Suspension and Handling Issues

A smooth and stable ride is crucial for ATV safety and comfort. Suspension and handling issues can cause an unstable or wobbly ride, excessive bouncing on rough terrain, and uneven tire wear.

A. Causes and Fixes


Worn or Damaged Suspension Components: Regularly inspect the suspension components, such as bushings, shocks, and springs, for signs of wear or damage. Replace any worn or damaged components as necessary.

Improper Tire Pressure: Check the tire pressure frequently and adjust it according to the manufacturer's recommendations. Proper tire pressure ensures better handling and reduces uneven tire wear.

3. Misaligned Wheels: If you notice that your ATV is pulling to one side or you observe uneven tire wear, it may be due to misaligned wheels. Have a qualified technician perform a wheel alignment to correct the issue.

V. Problem 5: Braking Issues

Braking issues are critical to address for safe and efficient operation of your ATV. Symptoms of braking problems include reduced braking performance, squealing or grinding noises during braking, and a soft or spongy brake pedal.

A. Causes and Fixes

Worn Brake Pads or Shoes: Inspect the brake pads or shoes for signs of wear or damage. Replace them if they are worn beyond the manufacturer's recommended limits.

Warped or Damaged Brake Rotors: Examine the brake rotors for signs of warping or damage. If necessary, have them resurfaced or replaced by a qualified technician.

Air in the Brake Lines: A spongy brake pedal may indicate air in the brake lines. Bleed the brake lines to remove any trapped air and restore optimal braking performance.

Conclusion:

By addressing these five common Arctic Cat Alterra 700 problems and implementing the suggested fixes, you can ensure a safe and enjoyable riding experience. Regular maintenance and timely repairs are crucial for prolonging the life of your ATV and preserving its performance. Always consult your owner's manual for specific maintenance schedules and procedures, and seek professional assistance when in doubt. With proper care and attention, your Arctic Cat Alterra 700 will provide you with countless hours of thrilling off-road adventures.

"According to a report by eMarketer, the global social commerce market is expected to grow from $492 billion in 2021 to $1,2 trillion by 2025. This indicates that the market is both big and rapidly increasing. In fact, the social commerce market is estimated to grow three times the speed of the traditional e-commerce market during this period. (https://www.insiderintelligence.com/insights/social-commerce-brand-trends-marketing-strategies/) The high growth may not seem as surprising when taking into consideration how many potential customers there are on the market. According to Statista, there were 4.59 billion social media users worldwide in 2022, and this number is expected to reach 5.42 billion by 2025. https://www.statista.com/statistics/278414/number-of-worldwide-social-network-users/. Social media platforms like Facebook, Instagram, Pinterest and TikTok have an enormous user base all over the globe, making them an attractive option for businesses to promote and sell their products. 

While social commerce was a big phenomenon previous to the COVID-19 pandemic, the pandemic accelerated the growth even further. With lockdowns and social distancing measures in place, more consumers have turned to online shopping, including social commerce. The growth of all types e-commerce was analyzed to be accelerated the equivalent of a projected 5 years growth in the first year of the pandemic alone.  As social commerce was part in that big leap, many consumers formed the habit of shopping on social media during the pandemic. https://www.socialmediatoday.com/news/the-rise-of-social-commerce-infographic/598525/

The trends and use of social commerce is rather different around the world. Thus we will focus on the different markets separately. Thus we will dive deeper into the social commerce trends in the three regions with the biggest social commerce markets. https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/social-commerce-market-107058

Asia-Pacific:
The Asia-Pacific region is on the cutting edge of social commerce development. 49% of the world’s social commerce sales were generated in the Asia-Pacific region (https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/social-commerce-market-107058), primarily driven by China. China is the undisputed leader in social commerce. The social medias used in china differ quite a lot from social media platforms used in other countries, which also changes how social commerce is used. In China there are several examples of e-commerce sites that successfully implements social features rather than the other way around, such as XiaoHongShu ​​(https://nealschaffer.com/social-commerce-platforms-shopping-apps-china/) and Alibaba with Taobao. These are primarily e-commerce sites that also future livestreaming, e-commerce, and community-building. However, some large social media platforms in China such as WeChat are also offering social commerce by utilizing mini-apps to enable consumers to shop directly from their feeds https://nealschaffer.com/social-commerce-platforms-shopping-apps-china. In China, live commerce is a big trend and can be seen as a subset of social commerce. This is when influential people livestream on platforms such as Taobao and encourage their followers to purchase the showcased products directly from the site.  https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/its-showtime-how-live-commerce-is-transforming-the-shopping-experience In addition to China, several other Asian countries are embracing social commerce. In India the social commerce market is expected to grow by 71,5% annually. Similar to China, social commerce is big on platforms that are unique to the country, for example, Meesho (https://www.globenewswire.com/en/news-release/2022/05/11/2440545/28124/en/India-Social-Commerce-Market-Report-2022-Homegrown-Start-ups-are-Capitalizing-on-the-Growing-Demand-for-Social-Commerce.html). Also, Thailand has a large market for social commerce which will be discussed more in detail in a later section. 

North America:
In North America, social commerce is much smaller than in Asia-Pacific, however, rapidly growing. Social commerce sales are projected to represent 5% of total e-commerce sales in the US market in 2025. Meanwhile, in China, 13% of total e-commerce sales came from social commerce already in 2021. Considering the success of social commerce in China, and that the conversion rate has been up to ten times as high as with traditional e-commerce, different actors in North America are making efforts to introduce social commerce to the region. https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/growth-marketing-and-sales/our-insights/social-commerce-the-future-of-how-consumers-interact-with-brands

Platforms such as Instagram, Facebook, and Pinterest have introduced shopping features to enable consumers to purchase products directly from their feeds. For instance, Instagram which is one of the major social media platforms in North America introduced the shop button in Novermber 2021. (https://jumpermedia.co/sell-on-instagram-shoppable-feed/). In North America, the trend seems to be that e-commerce features, such as shop buttons related to posts, are added to existing large social media platforms. 

However, a reason why these efforts have not yet achieved the same level of success as in Asia could be due to the large entrenched power of e-commerce platforms such as Amazon. Amazon is very much a pure e-commerce platform with limited social features. In December they introduced a social commerce feature called “Inspire” which was a feed combining short videos with shopping. It was introduced as late as of December 2022 and has not seen any major traction so far. https://www.marketplacepulse.com/articles/amazon-attempts-social-commerce. 

A second reason why social commerce still has not seen the same breakthrough in the North American market could be due to the lack of live commerce efforts, which is a big part of social commerce in Asia. In China, 10% of all e-commerce sales comes from live commerce, and thus makes up the majority share of the social commerce market. Major american brands such as Walmart and Tommy Hilfiger have had live streaming events on social platforms. However, there is no big platform in the North American market directly focused on live commerce. https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/its-showtime-how-live-commerce-is-transforming-the-shopping-experience 
Europe
The European social commerce market is very similar to the one in the North American market. This is because most European countries resort to similar social media platforms as consumers in North America. Thus, the spread of social commerce in Europe is very much dependent upon the moves of major actors such as Facebook, Instagram, Pintrest and Twitter.Similar to North America, popular platforms such as Instagram and Facebook are introducing shopping functions in the European market  https://www.insiderintelligence.com/insights/social-commerce-brand-trends-marketing-strategies/."

指纹技术是生物特征识别领域的开拓者，现已广泛应用于金融、政务、物流、医疗等多个领域。未来，随着科技的不断发展和社会需求的增加，生物特征识别技术将逐渐替代口令、密码等传统身份验证方式。指纹识别技术相对于传统的密码键盘具有更高的安全性便利性和可靠性，因为指纹是不可复制的，而密码和PIN码可以被猜测或窃取。指纹识别技术的安全性大大降低了密码错误和账户盗窃的风险。而且指纹识别技术的误判率和漏识率都很低，相对于密码和其它识别技术，其识别准确率更高，同时避免了用户忘记密码等问题。把这段话翻译成英文

Name: Iolana (meaning "to soar like an eagle" in Hawaiian, represents her future potential) (Subject 06)

Age: 12

Appearance: Iolana has long, wavy grey hair and sparkling cyan eyes. She is petite with a slim build and a youthful appearance. She often wears colorful and comfortable clothes, like a bright yellow sundress and white sneakers.

Personality: Iolana is a kind and curious young girl with an adventurous spirit. She is eager to explore the world and experience new things, including becoming a Pokémon trainer. Despite her traumatic past, she remains optimistic and tries to make the best of every situation. She has a deep love and connection with her Cyndaquil, Keahi, and considers it to be one of her closest friends.

Background: Iolana is the product of a secret project called “Project Infinity” run by the Unova government during the Cold War. Her Yveltal cells caused her to lose control during an experiment and go on a rampage, but she was eventually discovered and adopted by a young couple in Alola. She has no memory of her past and has grown up in a loving and supportive environment. Now, she is embarking on a journey to become a Pokémon trainer and discover more about herself.

Relationships: Iolana has a close relationship with her adoptive parents, who have given her a loving home. She also has a strong bond with her Cyndaquil, Keahi, who is her first Pokémon companion and best friend. While she doesn’t have any close friends or rivals yet, she is excited to meet new people and Pokémon on her journey.

Motivations: Iolana’s main motivation is to become a strong and skilled Pokémon trainer. She wants to explore the world and discover new places and species, while also making new friends and allies. She is also curious about her past and wants to find out more about her origins and the mystery surrounding her Yveltal cells.

Secrets: Iolana is unaware of her traumatic past and the fact that she’s part of a secret project that aimed to create biological weapons for military purposes. She has yet to discover the truth about her Yveltal cells and the potential danger they pose.


Name: Hana and Taro

Appearance: Hana is a kind and gentle woman with wavy brown hair and bright green eyes. She often wears simple and comfortable clothing such as T-shirts and jeans. Taro is a tall and muscular man with short black hair and deep brown eyes. He has a serious demeanor and often wears business attire.

Personality: Hana is a nurturing and empathetic person who loves and cares for Iolana deeply. She is patient and understanding and always provides guidance and support when needed. Taro is a protective and responsible father figure who is dedicated to keeping his family safe and provided for. He is often focused on work but always makes time for his family.

He ran as fast as he could, using all his speed and agility to outpace the giant’s pursuit. He knew that he had to keep running, to keep fighting, to keep surviving, no matter what the cost.

For he was not just an anthropomorphic cheetah. He was a warrior, a hero, a creature of indomitable spirit, who refused to be broken, to be weakened, to be enslaved by the forces of darkness. And with every breath he took, with every step he made, he proved that he was a champion, a fighter, and a survivor, who would never give up, who would never surrender, who would never stop until he had won his freedom and returned to his rightful place in the wild.
The cheetah stopped abruptly his run when he stumbled on a monstrous animal, it was looking like the horrible mix of a scorpion and a lizard and it was around four times his size. This animal walked toward him visibly interested in the cheetah too, but not to examine him with amazed eyes as the eight eyes of the creature were filled with hunger, it wanted to eat him. The cheetah took one of the giant sized blades of grass and used it to hit the monstrous animal. It did nothing and worse the creature cut the grass between his sharp pincers and moved closer of the cheetah. 
It cornered him and was going to kill him when suddenly the heel of an humongous boot slammed on the horrible creature and crushed it instantly. The trembling cheetah looked up to see it was the giant who came in the nick of time to save him. A mere second too late and the cheetah would have been torn apart and eaten.
The bearded giant looked down at the cheetah, disappointment was visible on his face. How could the magnificent tiny creature he treasured all these days could have decided to leave him ? He stood there in front of the diminutive anthro who was still lying on his tiny back, his chest heaving up and down rapidly as he was still under the shock of what just happened. The ground trembled as he moved back his foot, leaving the horrible animal crushed carcass on the ground. The cheetah looked at it. There must be lot of these things around he thought. They must be like the spiders and other creatures feeding on insects in his world, and despite his perfect big cat anthropomorphic body he was as vulnerable than an insect here.
The giant hesitated, should he pick this handsome tiny anthro cheetah with him and bring him home or should he let him there and leave ? 
But the thought of having him killed in an horrific way and eaten by a simple scorplizard was appalling too much.
His shadow spread around again as his immense and towering form squatted down, he reached his huge and meaty hairy hand to carefully seize the cheetah.

The term “third world” was originally used during the Cold War era to refer to countries aligned with neither the capitalist or NATO First World nor with communist or Soviet Union Second World blocs. Over time, the term has, however, taken on a broader meaning and is now commonly used to describe developing countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. These countries are often characterised by economic and social underdevelopment, political instability, limited access to education and healthcare, and high poverty rates. Despite its historical use, the term is now considered politically incorrect and potentially offensive as it implies a hierarchy of development and reinforces negative stereotypes about these regions of the world. This negative stereotyping of Third-World countries is also evident in the way Western feminists have portrayed Third-World women. Therefore, for this paper, the term “Third World women” will be used in the manner Western feminists employ it in their written works.

Regular Exercise May Help Protect Against Severe COVID-19
More exercise means less risk of developing severe COVID-19, according to a study of physical activity
and coronavirus hospitalizations. The study, which involved 48,440 adults from California who
developed COVID-19 during the period of Jan 1st 2020 to Oct 21st 2020, found that those who had
been the most active before falling ill were 70% less likely to be hospitalized.
This study was conducted at Kaiser Permanente Southern California (KPSC), an integrated healthcare
system that serves approximately 4.7 million residents in Southern California at 15 medical centers.
Subjects of the study have to be enrolled at KPSC for at least 6 months prior to their COVID-19
diagnosis, and they are required to have at least three outpatient visits for the trained medical
assistants or nurses to ask their typical exercise habits over the previous 2 months.
Of the 48,440 subjects in the study, 6,984 were classified as "inactive" (exercise < 10 mins/week),
3,118 as "most active" (exercise > 150 mins/week) and 38,338 as "some activity" (exercise 10 to 150
mins/week). It’s found that 732 of the inactive were hospitalized, compared with 99 from the most
active group.
A 10-minute walk might be especially beneficial for people awaiting their first vaccine, says Dr. Robert
Sallis, a family and sports medicine doctor at the Kaiser Permanente Fontana Medical Center, who led
the study. "I would never suggest that someone who does regular exercise should consider not getting
the vaccine. But until they can get it, I think regular exercise is the most important thing they can do
to lessen their risk. And doing regular exercise will likely be protective against any new variants, or the

The goal of the assignment is to have students write a public official regarding a problem that is affecting the world today. Here is the discussion to the same study, give me 3-5 sentences in the same style summarizing it, and then give me another 2-4 sentences explaining the significance of this study and the long term perspective - 

Isolates of C. albicans vary for a range of properties connected to biofilm, hyphae, and the regulation of hypha-associated genes. In this report, we sought to determine the basis for natural variation in gene expression responses, with a focus on the well-characterized biofilm and hyphal regulator Efg1. Our findings revealed that the genomic distribution of Efg1 binding regions is essentially uniform among strains, whereas the gene expression impact of bound Efg1 is not. We observed that modest differences in expression of TF genes with some functional connection to Efg1 can have substantial impact on Efg1-responsive gene expression; they can even determine whether genes are under positive or negative Efg1 control. This partner TF analysis revealed that Wor1, a white-opaque regulator known to antagonize Efg1 in cell type switching and commensalism, has an unexpected positive role in biofilm formation. The positive role depends upon determinants of the Wor1 PrLD, which has been shown to mediate Efg1-Wor1 interactions in vitro and in heterologous cells. It is possible that a PrLD-dependent Efg1-Wor1 interaction also functions in biofilm formation (Fig. 8).
Efg1 core direct target genes.
A group of 110 genes have 5′ regions bound by Efg1 and expression levels that are up- or downregulated by an efg1Δ/Δ mutation in all five strains examined. Most of these genes are under positive control by Efg1. This group (see Table S1, core direct Efg1 targets) includes well-characterized determinants of biofilm or hypha formation, such as ALS1, ALS3, HGC1, HWP1, and XOG1. The uniform regulatory response of these genes aligns well with the uniform biofilm and hyphal defect for efg1Δ/Δ mutants among these strains (13). In addition, two Efg1 core direct targets specify the biofilm and hyphal transcriptional regulators Brg1 and Ume6. Thus, the previously described Efg1-Brg1-Ume6 feed-forward loop is preserved among multiple strains, in support of its functional significance (3, 4, 15). Efg1 core direct target genes also include several central metabolic genes, such as ADH1, GLK1, HGT6, and PCK1. The products of these genes may help balance competing carbon demands from biofilm matrix synthesis and from energy production and small-molecule synthesis. Still, most of the Efg1 core direct targets lack known or easily rationalized roles in biofilm or hypha formation. This limitation in our understanding may reflect functional redundancy (2, 3, 16–22) or perhaps the limited range of biofilm-related phenotypes that have been assayed in mutant analyses.
Efg1 strain-limited direct target genes.
Each strain had ~100 to 200 genes whose 5′ regions were bound by Efg1 in all strains but displayed Efg1-responsive expression in only a subset of strains (see Table S1, direct repressed genes and direct activated genes). Strain-limited regulatory responses might be enriched for “noisy” genes (23); we tried to minimize this concern by using data from six biological replicates of WT and efg1Δ/Δ derivatives of each strain. We calculate that 51 to 62% of strain-limited direct targets have a variance in reads per kilobase per million values below the mean for our complete RNA-seq data set, compared to 31 to 58% of core direct targets (see Data Set S1, variance summary). Therefore, strain-limited direct targets do not show greater day-to-day variation than core direct targets.
Many strain-limited genes had expression changes that trended in the same direction in all strains but failed to meet our criteria for fold change or statistical significance in one strain. The Efg1-bound genes SFL2, TYE7, and YWP1 are examples. Several of these genes impact biofilm and hyphal formation, thus illustrating that strain-limited bound genes may contribute to the efg1Δ/Δ mutant phenotype. Even the strain-limited bound genes that showed significant expression changes in only one strain background included some with biofilm- and hypha-related functions, such as AHR1, CLN3, and ROB1. Their expression differences may have contributed to the efg1Δ/Δ mutant phenotype in the respective strains as well. However, enrichment for biofilm and hyphal functions is much greater among core direct targets than among strain-limited direct targets.
One class of strain-limited direct targets was activated by Efg1 in some strains and repressed by Efg1 in others (see Table S1, differential expression). Sugar transporter genes HGT1, HGT2, and HGT13 were members of this group, as were ferric reductase-like genes CFL4 and CFL5 and the chitinase gene CHT2. In the case of CHT2, our fusion gene analysis (Fig. 4) showed that the Efg1 impact on promoter activity was strain dependent. The existence of such divergent regulatory responses among strains suggests that the net impact of 5′ region-bound Efg1 depends upon the presence of neighboring TFs.
Wor1 function in the Efg1 regulatory circuit.
What are the determinants of strain-limited regulatory responses? TFs that share target genes with Efg1 are candidates that may modulate activity of 5′ region-bound Efg1. This idea led us to look among TF genes whose RNA levels differed between two strains, SC5314 and P75010. Two candidate TF genes specify known regulators of biofilm- and hypha-related gene expression, Brg1 and Tec1. Increased dosage of BRG1 in strain P75010, to mimic the naturally elevated BRG1 expression of SC5314, caused Efg1-responsive regulation of ECM331, HGT1, ECE1, and HYR1 resembling more closely that in strain SC5314. Increased dosage of TEC1 had a similar though less pronounced effect. Increased dosage of BRG1 or TEC1 also had a functional impact, leading to increased biofilm formation by P75010. Given the many interconnections in the biofilm and hypha regulatory network (24), it seems reasonable that increased expression of known biofilm and hyphal regulators would affect Efg1-target relationships.
This strategy also pointed to an unexpected connection between Wor1 and biofilm and hyphal gene expression. WOR1 was also expressed at higher levels in SC5314 than in P75010, and increased WOR1 dosage in P75010 caused SC5314-like phenotypes, including increased biofilm formation and features of Efg1-responsive gene regulation. Wor1 mutant derivatives lacking PrLD integrity, which are known to be defective in interaction with Efg1 (10), as well as an efg1Δ/Δ mutation, blocked WOR1 effects on Efg1-responsive gene expression and biofilm formation. These lines of evidence together argue that Wor1 acts in conjunction with Efg1 under biofilm and hyphal growth conditions.
The findings presented here broaden our view of the relationship between Wor1 and Efg1 (Fig. 8). White-opaque and white-GUT cell type switching reflect antagonism between Efg1 and Wor1 (1, 5–7, 9). The white state is at one extreme, in which Efg1 expression and function predominate; the opaque or GUT state is at the other extreme, in which Wor1 expression and function predominate (1, 5–7, 9). However, our analysis of natural variation indicated that a more delicate balance between Wor1 and Efg1 activities had an impact on the capacity to form biofilm and the regulation of Efg1 target genes. The impact of WOR1 in this context supports a new feature of Wor1 function: that Wor1 acts in collaboration with Efg1 in the biofilm and hyphal regulatory network.
What is the nature of the Wor1-Efg1 collaboration? What mechanism enables Wor1 levels to affect Efg1-responsive gene expression? One model is that Wor1 interacts directly with Efg1 to modify its activity. Most Efg1 direct targets are upregulated in P75010+WOR1 compared to P75010+empty vector, suggesting that a hypothetical Wor1-Efg1 complex may have greater activation ability than Efg1 alone. A second model is that Wor1 acts more indirectly to promote expression or activity of known biofilm and hyphal activators that share targets with Efg1. The best candidates from our data are Brg1 and to a lesser extent Tec1, based on the parallels in dosage effects. Specifically, increased dosage of BRG1, TEC1, or WOR1 affected an overlapping set of genes (Fig. 6) and increased biofilm formation (Fig. 7A) in strain P75010. BRG1 and TEC1 RNA levels were elevated ~1.5-fold in P75010+WOR1 compared to P75010+empty vector, increases that make this model seem possible if perhaps not persuasive. The mechanism through which Efg1 and Wor1 act to promote biofilm and hyphal gene expression and biofilm formation remains uncertain at this time.
Natural variation-driven functional discovery.
Our findings here emphasize the value of natural strain variation for functional genetic analysis in C. albicans. Natural variation has been exploited previously to define mechanisms of drug resistance (25, 26), genome evolution (27), and virulence determinants (28). However, the lack of a complete sexual cycle precludes genome-wide association studies and related mapping approaches, and the high level of sequence variation among strains makes candidate genes too numerous to explore efficiently. The type strain SC5314 has been a reliable starting point to define genetic determinants of diverse biological processes with reverse-genetics manipulations. However, the features that make it an excellent workhorse, such as strong biofilm formation and a high level of virulence in diverse infection models, may reflect genetic adaptations that amplify activities of some circuits and minimize the impacts of others. Other clinical isolates, with a different selection of amplified and minimized circuits, offer a sensitive context to reveal new gene functions and functional relationships.

summarise this: 	The near majority of the names based on Hermanubis are attested in papyri of the second and third centuries AD. Possibly the earliest example is on an ostracon dated to AD 94 (1), but the name there is abbreviated and Ἑρμανο(υβίου) is Wilcken's resolution.[16] Otherwise, the next attestations are from the second quarter of the second century. There are overall eight instances from the second century, eight from the third, and three from the fourth. One instance is from an inscription dated broadly to the "imperial period." The name disappears from the evidence after the fourth century. What is notable about this chronological distribution is that it corresponds neatly to that of the iconographical representations of the fully anthropomorphic god known conventionally as "Hermanubis." For example, the great number of Alexandrian coins that show a representation of this type on the reverse range from the reign of Trajan (or possibly as early as Domitian) to the Tetrarchic period. Likewise, the statues, statuettes, and carved reliefs of this god have all been assigned to the second or third century AD.[17]

import os
import sys
import pyperclip
import time
import string
import ctypes
from pynput.keyboard import Key, Controller
from PyQt5 import QtWidgets, QtGui, QtCore

delay = 100
first_char_delay = 2000
SETTINGS_FILE = 'settings.txt'
shift_delay = 100

def is_key_upper_case(char):
    return char.is_upper() or char in string.punctuation

class Worker(QtCore.QThread):
    clipboard_changed = QtCore.pyqtSignal(str)

    def init(self, parent=None):
        super().init(parent)
        self.clipboard_content = pyperclip.paste()

    def run(self):
        while True:
            new_clipboard_content = pyperclip.paste()
            if new_clipboard_content != self.clipboard_content:
                self.clipboard_content = new_clipboard_content
                self.clipboard_changed.emit(new_clipboard_content)
            time.sleep(0.1)

class KeyEventFilter(QtCore.QAbstractNativeEventFilter):
    def init(self, parent=None):
        super().init(parent)
        self.ctrl_pressed = False
        self.alt_pressed = False
        self.shift_pressed = False
        self.paste_triggered = False

    def nativeEventFilter(self, eventType, message):
        msg = ctypes.wintypes.MSG.from_address(message.int())
        wParam = msg.wParam

        if msg.message == 0x0100:  # WM_KEYDOWN
            if wParam == 0x11:  # VK_CONTROL
                self.ctrl_pressed = True
            elif wParam == 0x12:  # VK_MENU (ALT)
                self.alt_pressed = True
            elif wParam == 0x10:  # VK_SHIFT
                self.shift_pressed = True

            if self.ctrl_pressed and self.alt_pressed and self.shift_pressed:
                if not self.paste_triggered:
                    self.paste_triggered = True
                    if self.parent():
                        self.parent().type_copied_text()
        elif msg.message == 0x0101:  # WM_KEYUP
            if wParam == 0x11:  # VK_CONTROL
                self.ctrl_pressed = False
            elif wParam == 0x12:  # VK_MENU (ALT)
                self.alt_pressed = False
            elif wParam == 0x10:  # VK_SHIFT
                self.shift_pressed = False
            self.paste_triggered = False

        return False, 0

class SystemTrayIcon(QtWidgets.QSystemTrayIcon):
    def init(self, icon, parent=None):
        QtWidgets.QSystemTrayIcon.init(self, icon, parent)
        self.menu = QtWidgets.QMenu()
        self.settings_action = self.menu.addAction('Settings')
        self.exit_action = self.menu.addAction('Exit')
        self.setContextMenu(self.menu)
        self.activated.connect(self.icon_activated)
        self.settings_action.triggered.connect(self.open_settings)
        self.exit_action.triggered.connect(self.close_settings)
        self.settings_dialog = None

        self.worker = Worker()
        self.worker.start()
        self.worker.clipboard_changed.connect(self.handle_clipboard_change)

    def icon_activated(self, reason):
        if reason == QtWidgets.QSystemTrayIcon.Context:
            self.menu.exec_(QtGui.QCursor.pos())

    def handle_clipboard_change(self, text):
        self.worker.clipboard_content = text

    def open_settings(self):
        if not self.settings_dialog:
            self.settings_dialog = SettingsDialog()
        self.settings_dialog.show()

    def close_settings(self):
        QtWidgets.QApplication.quit()

    def type_copied_text(self):
        new_clipboard_content = pyperclip.paste().strip()
        if new_clipboard_content:
            time.sleep(first_char_delay / 1000)
            keyboard = Controller()
            for char in new_clipboard_content:
                if char in string.printable:
                    if is_key_upper_case(char):
                        keyboard.press(Key.shift)
                    keyboard.press(char)
                    keyboard.release(char)
                    if is_key_upper_case(char):
                        time.sleep(shift_delay / 1000)
                        keyboard.release(Key.shift)
                    time.sleep(delay / 1000)

class SettingsDialog(QtWidgets.QDialog):
    def init(self, parent=None):
        super().init(parent)
        self.setWindowTitle('Settings')
        self.setFixedSize(400, 300)
        self.delay_label = QtWidgets.QLabel('Delay between characters (ms):')
        self.delay_spinbox = QtWidgets.QSpinBox()
        self.delay_spinbox.setMinimum(0)
        self.delay_spinbox.setMaximum(10000)
        self.delay_spinbox.setValue(delay)
        self.first_char_delay_label = QtWidgets.QLabel('Delay before first character (ms):')
        self.first_char_delay_spinbox = QtWidgets.QSpinBox()
        self.first_char_delay_spinbox.setMinimum(0)
        self.first_char_delay_spinbox.setMaximum(10000)
        self.first_char_delay_spinbox.setValue(first_char_delay)
        self.save_button = QtWidgets.QPushButton('Save')
        self.cancel_button = QtWidgets.QPushButton('Cancel')
        self.save_button.clicked.connect(self.save_settings)
        self.cancel_button.clicked.connect(self.reject)
        layout = QtWidgets.QVBoxLayout()
        layout.addWidget(self.delay_label)
        layout.addWidget(self.delay_spinbox)
        layout.addWidget(self.first_char_delay_label)
        layout.addWidget(self.first_char_delay_spinbox)
        buttons_layout = QtWidgets.QHBoxLayout()
        buttons_layout.addWidget(self.save_button)
        buttons_layout.addWidget(self.cancel_button)
        layout.addLayout(buttons_layout)
        self.setLayout(layout)

    def save_settings(self):
        global delay, first_char_delay
        delay = self.delay_spinbox.value()
        first_char_delay = self.first_char_delay_spinbox.value()
        with open(SETTINGS_FILE, 'w') as f:
            f.write(f"{delay}\n{first_char_delay}")
        self.accept()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = QtWidgets.QApplication([])
    app.setQuitOnLastWindowClosed(False)

    tray_icon = QtGui.QIcon('icon.png')

    if not QtWidgets.QSystemTrayIcon.isSystemTrayAvailable():
        QtWidgets.QMessageBox.critical(
            None,
            'System Tray Not Available',
            'Your system does not support system tray icons. This application will not be able to run.',
        )
        sys.exit(1)

    # Read saved settings
    if os.path.isfile(SETTINGS_FILE):
        with open(SETTINGS_FILE, 'r') as f:
            lines = f.readlines()
            if len(lines) >= 2:
                delay = int(lines[0].strip())
                first_char_delay = int(lines[1].strip())

    tray = SystemTrayIcon(tray_icon)
    tray.show()

    key_event_filter = KeyEventFilter(parent=tray)
    app.installNativeEventFilter(key_event_filter)

    QtWidgets.QApplication.setQuitOnLastWindowClosed(False)
    sys.exit(app.exec_())

方法： 在这项基于网络的横断面调查中，我们于2021年7月至8月在日本调查了5688对产后妇女和婴儿(<3岁)。研究报告了婴儿哮喘、鼻炎、结膜炎或过敏性皮炎等的临床诊断。使用多水平Poisson回归，我们估算了按孕期对HPT吸烟类别交叉分类的婴儿过敏性疾病的患病率比值（PRs）和95％置信区间（CIs），并对潜在的协变量进行了调整，包括母亲吸烟和伴侣的吸烟状况。非吸烟者作为参考组。

How to Get Rid of Wasps in Your House

Wasps aren't really bad insects. They do good for us by pollinating flowers and otherwise contributing to our environment. Unfortunately, wasps are known to become aggressive around people and, if provoked, they will cause wasp stings that are itchy, red, and painful. They can also lead to dangerous allergic reactions in certain susceptible people.
How to Kill a Wasp in Your House

If you can't get the wasp to leave your home, you will need to kill it. There are several ways to kill a wasp, so it won't bother you anymore.

In the very beginning, you need to isolate the wasp. If it is flying all around the house, you should get all children and your pets out of the room. Turn on the lights so that you can actually see the wasp. If the lights are too high on the ceiling, the wasps will be attracted there and you may not be able to reach it. Then you may need a ladder and have a helper around to keep the ladder from tipping over. You can set out sugary drinks or food that will attract the wasp and turn on lamps that are lower than the ceiling if you don't have a ladder. Put on something thick and wear pants so that the wasp won't sting you on your bare skin. Then let's do the job.
1.  Use Spray

You can learn how to kill a wasp in your house by purchasing a can of spray that can kill the wasp, such as EcoExempt Jet Wasp and Hornet Killer. It is best to kill a wasp in another room of the house instead of the kitchen, so the spray doesn't get near food. Stand still and wait until the wasp is close enough to spray. Don't just spray a large cloud of the spray at the wasp. Instead, reach out with your arm and spray the insect. If you have done it right, the wasp will fall to the ground as soon as it is sprayed. Spray it again after it has fallen to the ground. Then grasp the wasp with a towel and dispose of it outside in the garbage can. Remember not to use toilet paper or tissues to pick up the wasp as it can still be alive and may sting you through the tissue. After spraying, clean the surfaces that might have been sprayed with water and soap.
2.  Use Flyswatter

This is an easy way to kill a wasp. If you have a fly swatter and only have a few wasps to tackle, a fly swatter will work very well. You need to have quick reflexes, because if you miss, you may get stung because you have irritated the wasp and it will come after you.
3.  Use Vacuum

If the wasp is not flying but is instead on the ground and walking on your floor, you can use a vacuum cleaner or a broom to get it off the floor and out of your house. It is best to do this during the springtime because wasps are just coming out of their hibernation state and are slower and more sluggish during that time. This is how to kill a wasp in your house when it is not that aggressive.
How to Prevent Wasps in Your House

If you don't feel like killing the wasp outright, you can do the following things to keep wasps out of the home:
1.  Keep Protein Foods Inside

Wasps mainly eat protein, so things that are high in protein should be kept insides rather than on your picnic table. This includes the protein in pet food as well. You should feed your pet indoors and this will keep wasps from being attracted to the pet food.
2.  Keep Sugar Away

While wasps primarily eat protein in the spring and summer, they tend to eat more sugar when it is fall time. Sugar increases the lifespan and reproductive abilities of the wasp, so they tend to gravitate toward it in the fall. If you are having a fall picnic, keep the sugary soda pops and sugary desserts inside.
3.  Set Out Some Sliced Cucumbers

If you are having an outdoor picnic and want to keep the wasps away, you can use cucumbers. Cucumbers are acidic and this is something wasps are not interested in. Cut cucumber into slices and keep them on around the barbecue area or picnic area. This will keep the wasps away so that you can enjoy your picnic.
4.  Keep Your Garbage Cans Sealed

While wasps are usually hunters, they will also scavenge for food. If your garbage can is not sealed tightly, the wasps will scavenge for the food in the container. You can also consider isolating out the protein and sugar you throw away into a separate garbage can. You don't have to lock up all your garbage that way, but make sure the can with the protein and sugar in it sealed tightly.
5.  Keep a Crumpled Brown Bag on the Ceiling

Wasps will not be attracted to an area where they believe that another wasp colony exists. If you crumple up a brown paper bag and attach it with string to the ceiling near the doors,wasps will not be able to tell the difference between a wasp's nest and a brown paper bag. They will stay away from your house and you won't have to worry about how to kill a wasp in your house.
6.  Seal the Siding of Your Home and Seal Up Awnings

Wasps like awnings and the siding of your house as places to build a wasp nest. If you seal these areas, the wasps won't be able to get through the siding and into your house. If you have a wasp nest near your home, wait until winter when they have died off and remove the nest before sealing up the house.

The term “third world” was originally used during the Cold War era to refer to countries aligned with neither the capitalist or NATO First World nor with communist or Soviet Union Second World blocs. Over time, the term has, however, taken on a broader meaning and is now commonly used to describe developing countries in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. These countries are often characterised by economic and social underdevelopment, political instability, limited access to education and healthcare, and high poverty rates. Despite its historical use, the term is now considered politically incorrect and potentially offensive as it implies a hierarchy of development and reinforces negative stereotypes about these regions of the world. This negative stereotyping of Third-World countries is also evident in the way Western feminists have portrayed Third-World women. Therefore, for this paper, the term “Third World women” will be used in the manner Western feminists employ it in their written works.
