POST WRITTEN BY
Carlos M. Meléndez
Carlos M. Meléndez is the COO and Co-Founder of Wovenware, an artificial intelligence and software development company.
May 9, 2018,07:45am EDT
uncaptioned
While the futuristic notion of a world filled with robotic workers, companions and even enemies is worlds away, pragmatic artificial intelligence (AI) is moving full steam ahead with no plans for stopping. In all of its forms and variations, it’s already starting to deliver real value to businesses.

Insurers are realizing the benefits AI can bring, automating manual underwriting processes, providing faster and better customer service and predicting risk. Retailers are seeing how AI can help them better understand shopper preferences and consumer behavior. What's more, health systems are able to predict the likelihood of patient readmissions or how to augment diagnoses and treatment plans.

Major accelerators of AI include the sheer computing power that is available today in the form of graphics processing units (GPUs), the growth of the data scientist profession and increasingly sophisticated algorithms.

PROMOTED




Perhaps the biggest factor feeding the swell of AI today is the proliferation of data being produced, stored and shared from Internet of Things (IoT)-based sensors, web pages, databases, social media sites and other tools. It’s interesting that most companies never made a conscious decision to accumulate data, per se -- it just sort of happened over time. Yet AI, which unlocks the correlations and mysteries of all that data, requires a much more deliberate pathway to adoption and is often fraught with skepticism.

Kicking The AI Tires

This skepticism may account for the fact that in a McKinsey survey of more than 3,000 companies, worldwide AI adoption outside the tech sector is still at an early, experimental stage. It found that the early adopters tend to be the larger firms deploying AI across their technology groups. They’re adopting AI to increase revenue as well as reduce costs. It also found that among those firms, it has the full support of executive leadership.

Companies that have not adopted AI technology at scale or in a core part of their business are not yet sure of the business case for AI or of the returns they can expect from it. The adoption pattern is showing a widening gap between early adopters and AI laggards. Why are companies so reluctant to join the bandwagon?

Confusion

Companies that have not adopted AI technology are unsure of the business case for AI or of the returns they can expect if they make the costly investment. They don’t understand what specific business problems they may be able to solve using AI.

Wait And See Attitude

In reality, predictive analytics, chatbots and machine learning applications have only taken off in the last few years, led by the large technology companies. Many mid-size firms are waiting to hear about measurable return on investment (ROI) from the early adopters, and for many of them, those metrics may be premature.

Fear Of The Unknown

Companies unfamiliar with AI may envision it as something to be feared, worrying that it will cause harm instead of good. They also fear that it will take away human jobs.

Investment Concerns

Probably one of the biggest reasons companies don’t adopt AI is the sheer cost of it. While partnering with a service provider can substantially offset the costs of deploying AI, companies thinking about initiating an AI project in-house would have to invest in costly GPU capacity, experienced data scientists and of course a complete disruption to their core business processes. Without complete faith in AI, that’s a tough risk to take.

Despite these concerns that are impeding greater adoption, companies of all sizes are putting themselves at a disadvantage by not becoming early adopters.

One reason they're disadvantaging themselves is that AI is a process, not a one-off technology solution. AI applications are only as good as the data that is fed to them over time, and as a result, the true gains, such as higher revenue, lower customer churn and more productive staff, take time to materialize. Companies that delay AI innovation will constantly be lagging behind the competition in realizing the benefits and gaining a return on their investment.

Additionally, being an early adopter is a serious competitive differentiator that sticks. When you’re willing to take the risk as a technology trailblazer, your customers see you as a partner willing to go out on a limb to improve their experience. Even in a commodity market, companies can stand out when they are early adopters of technologies such as AI.

Early AI adopters get to redefine their industries and how they operate. Just as software and the personal computer did, being an early adopter enables companies to influence and shape the transformation of their respective industries and become thought leaders in the process.

Yet being an early adopter doesn’t mean jumping into something quickly or blindly. AI requires a strategic approach. It means truly identifying your business problem and understanding how AI solutions can be applied to solve it.

In a Deloitte Cognitive Technologies survey of early leaders in cognitive solutions, 83% of respondents said their companies have already achieved either moderate or substantial benefits from their work with these technologies. According to the Deloitte report, “something is working and it's changing perceptions.”

AI is fast becoming a game-changer, and as it evolves, there's no doubt that the cost of deployment will decline. Those who get a reputation as a technology laggard may have a hard time recovering or ever catching up in a market that is on fire and moving full steam ahead. For those taking the plunge, being an early AI adopter just may be well worth the risk.'

summarise this: There are three forms of the personal name based on Hermanubis in papyri and inscriptions from Egypt, in addition to two "proper" polytheophoric names. (In four instances [1, 5, 17, 21] the ending of the name is abbreviated or not preserved.) The form Ἑρμάνουβιϲ, attested six times (2, 9, 11, 13, 14, 15), simply replicates the god's name without further ado, through the phenomenon of "direct theonymy" (the giving to humans of unaltered divine names) that became widespread in the Roman period.[8] In an odd instance (9) the form Ἑρμανουβιϲ in the nominative is borne by a woman, an Alexandrian nurse (ἡ τροφόϲ).[9] It is highly unusual for female theophoric names to be unmarked for gender in this way.[10] I suspect that because the element -ανουβιϲ in Ἑρμάνουβιϲ was often declined with a dental declension in -ιδ-, it might have been conflated with the similar declension of Greek feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ.[11] If this is correct, it may be preferable to read the nurse's name as an oxytone (Ἑρμανουβίϲ). There are two further forms based on the god Hermanubis: Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (six instances: 3, 4, 7, 8, 12, 20) and Ἑρμανουβίων (four instances: 3, 6, 18, 19). -ᾶϲ and -ίων were, in fact, the commonest Greek suffixes for personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ.[12] It is interesting that in a business letter (3) the two forms Ἑρμανουβίων (l.38) and Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (l.41) are used interchangeably to designate the same individual. In this case, Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ is probably a hypocoristic of Ἑρμανουβίων, since the suffix -ᾶϲ often served this purpose.[13]

Write an interesting, riveting, lengthy action-packed short story/fanfic that is a crossover between Naruto, Fate Stay Night: Prisma Illya, Majikoi, Bakemonogatari and High School DxD. 

The plot should follow the below events to a T, occurring in chronological order with nothing left out and everything explained and clarified in a plausible matter:

Part I: Origins

. The soul of the Juubi no Ookami [Ten Tailed Wolf] escapes the seal Hagoromo Ōtsutsuki, known to most people in the Elemental Nations as the Rikodo Senin [Sage of Six Paths] had placed to trap it inside the new moon he had created for eternity, and enters the body of a newborn Kushina Ōtsutsuki, the heiress of the Ōtsutsuki clan of Uzushiogakure no Sato in the Land of Whirlpools after the newborn child's soul ends up passing to the afterlife. 

Kushina's parents are unaware of this, because of their joy at finally being able to conceive a child. 

. Due to her status as the Ōtsutsuki clan heiress and also due to her soul being that of the Juubi no Ookami, an already famous and powerful yokai that became even more powerful due to it consuming a spare Shinju fruit sometime after Kaguya Ōtsutsuki did, Kushina has the Rinne-Sharingan, along with the infamous doujutsu kekkai genkai [eye jutsu bloodline] of the Ōtsutsuki clan, the Byakugan. 

As she grows, she quickly becomes a master of Fūinjutsu [sealing arts] and the long lost magical arts of the Ōtsutsuki clan, passed down from its founder Hamura Ōtsutsuki, the older brother of Hagoromo Ōtsutsuki, the legendary Sage of Six Paths. 

.Kushina keeps the fact that she has the Rinne-Sharingan a secret, as even she herself is not sure how to use this power

. She also masters the Jūken style of taijutsu [martial arts] that utilizes the ability of the Byakugan to see and observe chakra in an opponent, to quickly strike the chakra circulation points and shut them down.

. Kushina still ends up in the Hidden Leaf and becomes the jinchuriki of the Nine-Tailed Fox after the destruction of Uzushiogakure no Sato by the Hidden Rain, Cloud and Earth Villages during the Second Great Ninja War. 

However, due to her true self as the legendary Ten-Tails that the Sage of Six Paths had sealed away and her training as the heiress of the Ōtsutsuki clan, she is able to block out Kurama's influence and regain mastery of her chakra, masking the usage of her own yokai as the Ten-Tails as simply her using the Nine Tails' power. 

. She is also to finally able to understand and use some of the powers of her Rinne-Sharingan, due to secretly reading the Senju and Uchiha secret scrolls. 

. Kushina and Mikoto Uchiha share Minato in a secret polygamous relationship, despite Mikoto's engagement to the head of the Uchiha clan, Fugaku. 

. Itachi is the secret love child of Minato Namkaze and Mikoto Uchiha, and is actually a girl named Isane. Sometime after the Kyuubi attack, she awakens her Sharingan due to the stress and trauma of the event and ends up warping herself via Kamui to Kamakura, where she is found by Tesshin Kawakami, an elderly monk and legendary martial artist, and raised as his granddaughter, Momoyo Kawakami. 


Part 2: Birth

. This version of Naruto is born as a ookami hanyou [half wolf monster] akin to Inuyasha, the protagonist of the Inuyasha anime and manga series. However, he is more akin to Sesshomaru in the fact that he has elfen ears, silver hair and yellow eyes and does not have wolf ears on top of his head, unless he taps into his yokai during emotional distress. 

This is due to Kushina being a primordial goddess as well as a daiyokai [monster lord], giving her child the strengths and powers of a full-blooded yokai while simultaneously allowing him to keep his humanity.

. Due to a poorly worded request, Kushina's soul [the Ten-Tails/Juubi] gets sealed along with the soul of Kurama Ōtsutsuki inside baby Naruto when Minato uses the Shiki Fujin [Reaper Death Seal] to seal away the Nine Tails inside his son. After the sealing, Naruto's hair turns black and his eyes blue, and he gains whisker marks on his cheeks due to the seal also sealing away his yoki and yokai power, along with Kushina and Kurama. 

Part 3: Discovery

. Things remain unchanged until Naruto is about 5 or 6 years old, and is locked in his room inside the orphanage. He makes a wish about not having to be in Konoha again, and due to unconsciously pulling on Kushina's power inside the seal, finds himself being warped via a superpowered Kamui to Kyoto in Japan, where he is quickly found by salaryman Gorou Hyoudou and his wife, Miki.

. Gorou and Miki adopt Naruto as their son, Issei Hyoudou and Issei grows up in a loving home, never knowing the truth of his origins or what is sealed inside of him. He becomes fast friends with Gorou and Miki's biological children, the fraternal twins Karen and Tsukihi, who are three years younger than Issei and view him as their real older brother as time goes by. 

. Issei also grows close to Gorou's consort Irisivel von Einzbern, a German noblewoman with white hair, a large bust and red eyes, and Irisivel's children, the twins Illya and Chloe. He also develops a close relationship with Irisivel's maids Sella and Leysritt, whom all the children view as their eccentric older sisters.

. He also grows close to his adoptive older cousin Irina Shidou, although he is saddened when she has to leave with her family to England. 

. On a family trip to Kamakura, Issei meets Momoyo, and the two become fast friends despite their vast age difference. Momoyo senses Issei's familar chakra and is surprised that he has her mother's friend Kushina as well as the Nine Tails inside of him, but she keeps this suspicion to herself.

. Issei also meets the shy and reclusive, blue haired and red-eyed girl Miyako Shiina, who is actually the daughter of Gendo Ikari and his wife Yui Ayanami created as a weapon to pilot the experimental EVA Unit mechs, Rei Ayanami, and the two become fast friends. 

Unfortunately, Miyako/Rei is discovered by some of Gendo's thugs and taken back to the Geofront, although she never forgets the memories of her time with Issei. 

.Karen's abilities as an ookami yokai [wolf monster] and the heiress to the Tohkasa clan prematurely awaken during the trip and as a result, she enters a form akin to a hanyou, with her wolf ears popping out of the top of her head at inappropriate times. This earns her the name "Wanko" from Momoyo, which she finds rather distasteful. 

. Tsukihi is kidnapped near the end of the trip, and the family spends 2 sleepless days worrying about her until Sella returns, holding a sleeping Tsukihi in her arms in a princess carry style with her regular bland look on her face. The local news reports that the Matou family, a local Mafia clan infamous for their strange activities, was wiped out by an unknown assailant. Issei quickly puts two and two together, but says nothing. 

. The experience changes Tsukihi, making her even more reclused and introverted than before. Her hair turns a bright purple, and she cuts it in a short bowl cut after recovering enough to leave the hospital. She also starts wearing traditional Japanese clothes at home, constantly harassing Sella and Leysritt to teach her the arts of homemaking, which they relent and give into in the end. Surprisingly, Tsukihi is very good at this task and is praised by the two maids for her skill and diligence. 

Part 4: Adolescence

. Issei grows into a confident, charming young man, unaware of his true origins and not caring about them anyways. From elementary to middle school, he is a star student and a rather famous baseball player, which earns him the jealousy of his classmates. 

. His younger adoptive sisters Karen, Tsukihi, Illya and Chloe all have feelings for him, even though he is unaware of this and just assumes they love him a bit too dearly. 

. When Issei is a second year at Tenbi High, he catches the eye of seductive, popular English teacher Himekami Lizel. Lizel is actually the Princess of the House of Gremory, the de-facto royal family of the Underworld, Rias Gremory. Lizel under her true name of Rias, is interested in Issei due to his powerful Sacred Gear of the Red Dragon Emperor, but she finds herself falling in love with him due to his kindness and good heart. 

. One day, Issei is mistaken for his perverted friends Matsuda and Motohoma, and beaten to an inch of his life by the enraged Kendo Club in a case of mistaken identity. Lizel, seizing the opportunity, revives the young man as her Pawn, which has the unexpected result of loosening the seal and allowing his yoki, which had been building up inside of him, to finally be released and circulate through his body. 

. This, along with the fact that the reincarnation process awoke Ddraig, the legendary Welsh Dragon inside Issei's Sacred Gear, essentially reconstructs Issei into a draconic- ookami hanyou [half wolf monster]-demigod reincarnated devil hybrid, enhancing and boosting his already formidable power 100 fold. 

. Issei is surprised at his new powers when he awakens and is confused when Lizel informs him of her true identity and what he now is, but quickly adjusts to it. 

. The plot of High School DxD continues as in the canon light novels, manga and 2012-2018 anime series, with the exception of Issei defeating Riser and the Phenix heir's peerage during the Rating Game, convincing the three female Fallen Angels Raynare, Kalwarner and Millet to join his side and killing the insane priest Freed Seltzen during the attack on the church before the fight against Riser and his peerage, and Irina revealing she is a succubus during the Holy Sword search arc. 

. Issei also discovers that his younger adoptive half-sisters, the fraternal twins Illya and Chloe von Einzbern, are actually magical girls who use the Class Cards to transform and fight the supernatural monsters popping up in the city. 

He also discovers Illya and Chloe's best friend, the reserved but intelligent black-haired Japanese beauty Miyu Edelfelt, is also a magical girl. 

This discovery comes as a result of Rias and her peerage receiving a message from the Archduke to kill a group of Strays causing trouble, only to find Illya, Chloe and Miyu leaving the scene with the monsters dead. 

. Needless to say, Rias/Lizel is not pleased and a rivalry starts to develop between Rias, Akeno and Koneko and Illya, Chloe and Miyu over their shared love for Issei. Issei's little sisters and the female members of Rias' peerage, along with Rias herself eventually get along and start sharing Issei, although this is a long and eventual process that takes years. 

. Issei also discovers that Karen is the heiress of the Tohsaka clan of magi while Tsukihi is the heiress of the Matou clan of magi, with Karen being Illya's mentor in the magical arts when the latter awakened her powers. Issei also meets Miyu's mentor, the arrogant and bold Luvia Edelfelt. 

. During a meeting of the Gremory and Shitori peerages at the ORC clubhouse located in the old Tenbi school building, Student Council President Tsubawa Hanekawa surprises everybody by confessing her love for Issei under her true name of Souna Shitori, the heiress of the House of Shitori that rules the Underworld along with Rias' House the Gremory and the House of Phenix, in front of a shocked Rias and the Gremory Princess' stunned peerage. To everybody's shock, the female members of Hanekawa's peerage also confess their love for Issei as well after that, further increasing Issei's already large harem. 

Part 5: Idols and Challenges

. One day when going home, Issei meets a mysterious woman named Suruga Kanbaru, who is later revealed to be a famous women's college basketball player and also a well-known idol under the name of Hoshigaoka Stella. Kanbaru seems to be very familiar with Issei despite them not meeting before, and teases him during their conversation about "tying the knot early with so many".

. It is later discovered that Suruga/Stella is actually Serafell Leviathan, one of the leaders of the Underworld and Hanekawa/Souna's estranged older sister. Hanekawa and Issei are revealed to be childhood friends, with a younger Hanekawa even swearing to marry Issei along with Irina when all three of them were children. This causes a considerable amount of embarrassment for both Hanekawa and Irina when this is exposed to the other girls by Serafell/Kanbaru/Stella. 

. Momoyo also reunites with Issei, and starts training him how to use his ki [physical energy] and chakra. She also falls in love with him and joins his harem. 

Issei and Miyako/Rei also reunite, and the blue-haired girl confesses her love for him, joining his harem as well. 

. The youngest daughter of Issei's adoptive parents Gorou and Miki, the half-kitsune [spirit fox]. half-snake god hybrid known as Sengoku Nadeko, awakens her powers as the reincarnation of the Dragon God Ophis, causing the pureblood devil Riseviem to send his minions after her. 

Rias, Hanekawa/Souna and Rias/Lizel along with their respective peerages are assigned to guard the precocious young girl, which causes Nadeko to develop a crush on her adoptive older brother Issei when she learns that he is Rias' Pawn and a hero in the supernatural world. 

. The fight against Riseveim is long and hard, but eventually, Issei manages to meld the power of his ookami yokai [wolf monster], devil and dragon forms into a single massive form, allowing him to destroy the madman once and for all. 

. Issei's rival, the White Dragon Emperor Vali Lucifer, is revealed to be a cute girl named Emila Hermit, and joins Issei's harem along with Nadeko and Koneko's sister, the supposed criminal Kuroka after Riseviem's fall. 

This causes the collapse of Khaos Brigade as many of its members refuse to accept this supposed betrayal.

Conclusion

The story ends with Issei thinking about his life, while the girls of his harem take care of their newborn babies through the large mansion they now call home.

Rewrite and summarize all paragraphs into one with all the important statistics in the passage in 300 words, "Artificial intelligence is taking over the business world. McKinsey’s ‘The state of AI in 2020’ report estimates that 50% of companies already use AI in at least one function.

And that only scratches the surface of what’s to come. Forecasts suggest that the revenues generated by artificial intelligence will double between 2020 and 2024, signaling how many businesses will harness the technology in the name of growth.

That said, companies still struggle to adopt AI — and today, we’ll share the ten challenges we hear about the most.

Let’s dive in.

10 Challenges to AI Adoption
If you’re considering using AI in your company, it can pay to be aware of the obstacles you might face. That way, you can smooth the path to AI adoption.

These are the most common challenges out there.

1. Your company doesn’t understand the need for AI
Inertia is a powerful force. After all, if a company is doing well, teams often feel reluctant to take a risk by making a significant change.

And adopting new technology, like artificial intelligence, will feel like a major shift. Next up, there’s the challenge of convincing stakeholders to invest in a solution for which the returns can be somewhat unclear. 

Because, where AI is concerned, you won’t always know what you’re building until you’ve made a start, which is another tricky obstacle to overcome.

2. Your company lacks the appropriate data
The only way to build and train effective AI is with a sufficient amount of high-quality data. And the better the data, the better the outcomes.

But a lack of stakeholder buy-in can cause companies to underinvest in the data management systems required to enable AI, leaving them unable to train an algorithm on how to solve their business problems accurately.

That said — if your company uses a CRM tool to collect customer demographics, purchase behavior, or on-site interactions, you may have a data set you can use. And where there are gaps, online data libraries, even synthetic data, can often fill them. 

But if your company isn’t interested in AI in principle, then you won’t know what data you need, let alone how to structure it.

3. Your company lacks the skill sets
Data is only half the equation. You need the right skill sets to make AI work. 

Yet many companies struggle to hire data and machine learning specialists, leaving them unable to take ambitions further.

And even where companies have a degree of in-house expertise, a lack of experience in the right fields can hinder progress; it can even affect hiring, as departments won’t know which roles to fill or how to assess candidates.

In some circumstances, heads of departments are simply underqualified to lead an AI implementation, resulting in inefficient processes, integration issues, or ongoing manual work that undermines the solution’s value.

4. Your company struggles to find good vendors to work with
Despite its growth, AI adoption in most businesses remains modest. 

One reason for this is many organizations have worked with AI agencies that don’t truly understand how to use the technology to deliver business value.

As a result, many companies have had negative experiences when dipping their toes in AI development, making them reluctant to dive in. Whereas had they worked with reputable and experienced AI vendors at the outset, the results would speak for themselves.

Better still, had the vendors offered to tackle a small business problem first, proving the value of AI, the stakeholders would have found it easier to embrace more ambitious plans later on.

5. Your company can’t find an appropriate use case
Some organizations think that by implementing AI just for the sake of it, they will encourage company-wide adoption.

Sadly, this strategy often has the opposite effect.

When a company doesn’t have a solid use case, it’ll struggle to create a solution that delivers business value. As a result, there’ll be no way to prove that artificial intelligence can solve everyday challenges.

In all likelihood, the strategy will only reinforce the indifference felt towards the technology, so it’s best to delay adopting AI until you know how you’ll use it.

6. An AI team fails to explain how a solution works
In our experience, people will only trust a computer model if they understand how it works. And if an AI team can’t offer an explanation, implementations can end there.

The challenge only grows in situations where a model’s output contradicts a stakeholder’s thinking — because stakeholders always want to know why a decision is wrong (and why they should consider changing their mind).

Let’s put this in a medical context for clarity. Suppose a doctor diagnoses a patient, only for an algorithm to disagree. 

The doctor will need to know the logic behind the machine’s thinking before they can sign off on the alternate diagnosis (say, by seeing that a model used symptoms like sneezing and headaches to diagnose flu, not characteristics like a patient’s age or weight).

But offering transparency isn’t always simple as ‘black box’ models often spit out a prediction without a rationale.

Leaving stakeholders without a way to validate the output.

7. Different AI teams fail to work as a unit
In large companies, AI teams often end up working in silos, meaning that while they use the same technologies, they do so in isolation.

As a result, they build different infrastructures and adopt different workflows, which only complicates broader AI adoption. You can avoid this by using a ‘hub-and-spoke’ structure, whereby one central unit aligns all teams around a standardized approach.

That way, any investment in AI benefits the whole organization and brings economies of scale as you roll solutions out.

8. Management fears having to overhaul legacy systems
It’s astonishing how many organizations still rely on outdated infrastructure, applications, and devices to power their IT operations. And management often chooses not to adopt AI for fear of the costs of upgrading these systems.

But the truth is: cloud computing means you can deploy AI without overhauling an antiquated IT network, using ‘Data Lake’ functions in hybrid environments. Doing so might require your company to have an operational framework on-premises.

But you still get to benefit from more effective, AI-driven operations.

9. Some solutions are just too complex to integrate
Even if you manage to design a ground-breaking AI solution, there’s no guarantee your company will adopt it.

That’s something Netflix discovered, at a great cost, in 2009.

The streaming giant offered a one-million-dollar prize to any developer that could increase the accuracy of its recommendation engine. And while one team managed to optimize it by some 10%, Netflix never integrated the upgrade. Why so?

They said it required too much engineering effort, and so: complexity meant the solution never saw the light of day.

10. Regulation often proves the biggest hurdle of all
Suppose you want to build a cloud-based banking platform in Poland: owing to regulations, that would only be possible if your data centers are in Poland too.

Many AI projects face requirements like these. And in industries like finance, they often halt solutions in their tracks. The same can be said of the question of accountability — that is, of who should take responsibility when AI makes a mistake.

Let’s return to the medical context for an example.

Suppose a doctor changes their diagnosis based on a machine’s recommendation, only for the machine to be wrong. Is the practitioner to blame? Or is it down to the algorithm’s developer?

These are ethical questions that regulators are yet to answer. Then, there’s the question of data management.

When you build an AI solution, you need to collect vast quantities of data and, whether that data is sensitive or not, you need to keep it adequately secure. Fail to do so, and regulations mean your company could face a significant fine.

Therein lies a risk that many organizations would rather not take.

No challenge is too great
While the challenges to AI adoption are many, you should have every confidence that you can implement artificial intelligence in your company.

Truth be told: an awareness of the pitfalls is a crucial first step. 

After all, if you know the obstacles you might face, you’ll be all the more prepared to design a strategy that maximizes your chances of success. There’s no hiding that successful AI adoption relies on investment, stakeholder support, and robust workflows.

But the potential benefits are huge, and no challenge is too great to overcome."

按照 概论、洞察、创意张力、执行，这四个步骤，以简洁的方式写出以下内容案列分析。
标题：东鹏瓷砖：刘擎×李雪琴南山对话，寻味陶渊明的悠然生活

3月9日东鹏瓷砖发布潮系列新品，并携手品牌挚友刘擎和李雪琴演绎短片《见南山》。片中两人以陶渊明的诗为引子，结合现代生活探讨新时代如何保持一颗悠然之心。
开篇，刘擎由“采菊东篱下，悠然见南山”发问：“现代的人，还找得到去南山的路吗？”李雪琴回复：“来的时候我也这样问司机了，他说去深圳得加钱。”两人心领神会地相视一笑，正式拉开南山对话的序幕。
短片共分为四个章节，每个章节名皆源于陶渊明的诗句。

第壹折·久在樊笼里，复得返自然
这一段，李雪琴和刘擎针对“是归隐还是逃避”展开讨论。
李雪琴：有时候我也想像陶渊明一样，回南山种地。您说他是真的享受归隐呢，还是在逃避呢？
刘擎：其实你不是想问陶渊明，而是在问你自己。
 这也恰好对应了当代许多人“心有田园”而“实不可至”的现状。

第贰折·种豆南山下，草盛豆苗稀
本段对话围绕“焦虑”展开。
李雪琴：所谓焦虑，就是怕自己做得不够好吧。
刘擎：但人的本能，就是会把自己和他人做区分。
 焦虑、同伴压力，同样作为被人们热议的话题，在这里，刘擎老师一针见血地击中焦虑本源——比较心。

第叁折·行行失故路，任道或能通
关于焦虑、迷茫，刘擎和李雪琴进一步给出解答——
刘擎：所以我们总是容易被眼前的东西困住，但有的事情离得太近反而看不清楚。
李雪琴：就像雪化了之后不是水，而是春天。

"When organic species grow arrogant enough to travel the stars...They find it waiting. "Prometheus' cleansing fire." The fire that keeps the universe clean...Of parasites like you." ―Iris Humphries (from Alien: Bloodlines) An analysis of Chemical A0-3959X.91 – 15.[1] Chemical A0-3959X.91 – 15, also known as Agent A0-3959X.91 – 15[1] and referred to colloquially as the "black goo", "black liquid" or "pathogen", was an extremely potent and virulent mutagenic pathogen, consisting of millions of small micro-organisms, that was manufactured by the alien species known as the Engineers. It was designed to act in a dual role: to create life-forms, and also to act as a biological weapon. Due to its volatile nature at ambient temperature, the Engineers housed the pathogen within special containers known as Steatite Ampules. Those affected by the mutation were designated as Anathema[2] and were subject to various violent mutations. Contents 1 Effects 2 History 3 Trivia 4 Appearances 5 References Effects "While it exists in a viscous liquid form, it atomizes when released in the atmosphere, killing or altering all living things unlucky enough to be within range. While the full extent of its potential is unknown, any corporation that finds out about it wants it. The only thing known for certain about Agent A0-3959X.91-15 and its derivatives is that its mutagenic capabilities are responsible for the creation of Abominations, Neomorphs, and other abhorrent creatures. Simply put: any living thing that comes into direct contact with the black goo will either die horribly, give birth to monsters, or become a monster themselves." ―Description of the pathogen (from Alien: The Roleplaying Game) According to David, who spent a decade extensively studying the pathogen and its effects, the primary purpose of the Chemical A0-3959X.91 – 15 pathogen is to cleanse planets of unwanted non-botanical life forms.[3] Once exposed to another organism, the virus has three known affects. Incompatible hosts are killed outright, while compatible hosts are mutated into highly-aggressive monsters dubbed Anathema or Abominations, and/or gestate a parasitic organism within them. These parasitic organisms, once released can then impregnate a second host, which leads the second host to gestate a hybridized predatory organism known as a neomorph. [4] In the case of the Anathema, the pathogen begins mutating its host by rewriting their DNA. The host becomes exceedingly aggressive and seemingly mindless as it attacks any living thing in sight. Geologist Fifield was exposed to the pathogen during the USCSS Prometheus' expedition of LV-223, mutating his appearance and causing him to attack his fellow crew members. He was also extremely resilient to physical injury, being able to withstand multiple gunshots. It was not until he absorbed multiple shotgun blasts, was scorched repeatedly with a flamethrower and run over twice with an RT Series Group Transport that he was finally killed. The pathogen also mutated several worms found in the Engineer temple, turning them into the aggressive Hammerpedes. These creatures exhibited acidic blood and the ability to regenerate lost body parts, though the latter is an ability commonly found in flatworms on Earth. However, the regeneration of Hammerpedes is much faster than that of normal flatworms, suggesting that the pathogen can enhance a host's natural abilities.[5] Charlie Holloway was also exposed to the pathogen and began mutating before Meredith Vickers killed him to prevent the virus from coming aboard the Prometheus. The virus appeared to affect his reproductive system. Prior to his physical mutation (while carrying the virus), Holloway was engaged in sexual intercourse with his lover, Elizabeth Shaw, impregnating her with the Trilobite. It is not clear if his sperm was mutated causing an abnormal conception or if he was simply carrying a Trilobite that moved into Shaw, though the latter seems more probable given that Shaw was sterile. Whether Trilobites prefer to move into female subjects or if Holloway would have naturally given "birth" to the Trilobite is also unknown. Although Shaw was exposed to the infected Holloway and had sex with him, she was not infected with the liquid and only received the Trilobite embryo. This suggests that the Black Liquid itself is not sexually transmitted, but it affects the host's reproductive system. This is further implied by the presence of an umbilical cord-like extension linking the Trilobite with Shaw, seen during its removal.[5] The chemical's induced mutations seems to be random with it never creating the same effect, however, it often does seem to accelerate the healing ability of its host though anyone or anything with cancer will have their condition greatly accelerated to the point of eventual death. Astrobiologist Francis Lane theorized that a Construct could be used as a "filter" for the virus and injected the pathogen into Elden causing him to mutate into a Construct/Engineer hybrid. He seemly became a "carrier" for the virus as vegetation begin to grow wherever he touched and his bite caused a Yautja to mutate similarly to Fifield. As demonstrated by David's experiments on Planet 4, when the pathogen infects a viable host it gestates a hybridized parasitic life form which may kill the host upon emergence. Examples of these hybridized creatures include the Trilobite on LV-223 (from Holloway, which passed to Shaw), the insect-like motes on Planet 4 from the mutated fungus, and the parasites which burst from the dying Engineers on Planet 4 after David released the virus. These parasites then seek out another host to attack or infect if possible. Once infected, the host will gestate and birth some form of predatory hybridized creature bearing Xenomorph-like qualities. The appearance of these hybridized creatures depends on the host organism due to a DNA reflex process, though they possess common traits such as elongated eyeless heads and a tendency for acidic blood. Examples include the Deacon on LV-223 and the Neomorphs on Planet 4. In the absence of viable hosts, the virus and the creatures spawned from it can enter an indefinite period of dormancy, becoming undetectable until awakened by a suitable host. David utilized Chemical A0-3959X.91 – 15 as part of his experiments to create the Xenomorphs seen on Planet 4, using it to splice a variety of Neomorph variants together.[3] The speed and intensity of the mutation process depends on the amount of virus taken on by the host and the manner in which it is exposed i.e ingestation or skin absorption. Holloway's mutation happened at a slower rate than Fifield's, which was more instantly obvious and horrific. Holloway had only a tiny drop of the black liquid in his drink, but Fifield took a full dose of the liquid in his face when he fell down in the temple. At very high doses, the mutation happens so fast that the body breaks down and it kills the victim quickly e.g. the Sacrifice Engineer, who drank a full cup of liquid though it is uncertain if this is indeed the same type of liquid as the rest. Based on our research findings, we can draw the conclusion that the interaction between ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion accelerates the overall corrosion rate of copper tubes, which ultimately leads to their failure.
Interestingly, because stress corrosion accelerates corrosion rates, corrosion products don’t accumulate enough to produce an expansion effect, resulting in an interactive corrosion morphology that is thinner than that of single ant nest corrosion.
Moreover, the starting points for single ant nest corrosion is often irregular, while the starting points for interactive corrosion are concentrated at the bottom of the internal groove, where tensile stress is highest.
Overall, these conclusions help us to better understand the mechanisms behind copper tube failure caused by ant nest corrosion and stress corrosion, which can lead to more effective preventative measures in future applications. Thank you for listening.

Discussion
To our knowledge, this is the first study to compare individual estimates of amounts of served food with the actual weight of the food served in a laboratory setting, where less disruptive data collection techniques are applied. Other studies assessing the accuracy of estimated portion sizes have used photographs as the reference for the assessment (Huybregts et al, 2007, Turconi et al, 2005), fake food items (Bucher et al, 2011, Bucher et al, 2012, Bucher et al, 2013) or average portion size weights based on repeated measures (Ngigi, Verbeke, & Pérez-Cueto, 2011). In contrast, the present study was performed using actual food items. In addition, the use of the Intelligent Buffet allowed the exact weight of food served by each participant to be recorded in a relatively undisturbed manner.

There are a few potential sources of bias in this study, one being measurement bias. An attempt was made to limit this by calibrating the equipment used for data collection before use and by following standard laboratory procedures. Therefore, if any measurement bias were to occur, it would be systematic throughout all observations. Secondly, the sample size may have been insufficient. However, the calculated power of 0.99 for the observed mean differences of both vegetables and whole grains suggests that the sample was enough to support the rejection of the null hypothesis. Previous studies have used a similar sample size with sufficient power (Mekhmoukh, Chapelot, & Bellisle, 2012). Thus, we assume that the result of the Wilcoxon signed-rank test was accurate (absence of type II error), and that it was correct to reject the null hypothesis since there was a significant difference between the two variables – the estimated and actual amount of food served.

Our findings are in agreement with similar studies conducted in a number of different countries and settings, including Norway (Uglem et al., 2008), the Netherlands (Lechner et al., 1997), Australia (Pollard et al., 2008) and Denmark (Sørensen et al., 2013), suggesting that individuals commonly have difficulties estimating food portion sizes with accuracy. The findings of a statistically significant difference between participants’ self-estimation and the actual weight of the vegetables served led to the rejection of the null hypothesis (Table 1). The participants in this study were also asked to estimate the weight of a whole grain serving in a relatively complex meal. It would be of interest to determine whether the results would have been more accurate if a simpler meal had been served (e.g. a bowl of cereal). However, the everyday diet commonly consists of prepared meals or ready-to-eat foods and individuals are often not aware of the actual composition of the foods they consume. Therefore, serving a “complex meal” is comparable with a real life situation, where participants are expected to estimate the amount of food served (Combet et al, 2013, Loh et al, 2013).

This study presents the question of whether food intake estimations that are independent of actual weighing are valid, particularly in the evaluation of adherence to healthy eating recommendations (Pérez-Cueto et al., 2012). This also underscores the importance of using alternative equipment and techniques, such as an Intelligent Buffet, in order to obtain actual data in studies measuring factors such as food intake (Bucher et al., 2012). The Intelligent Buffet presents many opportunities to conduct other studies or further research on this particular topic. In combination with video cameras, this equipment could be used for choice architectural experiments researching behaviour related to a buffet setting. For example, it could be used to investigate whether individual consumption differs in relation to the order of food items or the size of serving trays.

As seen in Table 1, there is no statistical correlation between measured and estimated values; they are independent variables meaning that there is no predictive relationship between the two measurements.

Consequently, if authors were to compare the difference between means they would probably find that the estimations are so different from the actual intake that they are neither reliable nor valid, thus supporting the use of alternative techniques such as the Intelligent Buffet when accuracy is a requirement of the research.

Generalizability of the findings is limited due to the study design and sample size. The use of a convenience sample from a university setting introduced a sample mainly consisting of higher educated participants aged between 20 and 29 years. Consequently, the results measure perception of serving size against the weight of the food served for this survey sample. A larger sample size and a different study design would need to be undertaken to explore if these findings extrapolate to the general University student community or to specific socio-economic groups within that community. The literature and the current study findings suggest the likelihood of an overestimation of portion sizes. However, further studies should be carried out before making conclusions about whether the general population is capable of accurately estimating food portion sizes and about the effects this could have on the degree of validity in studies involving self-estimation as a data collection method.

Considering that the majority of participants were highly educated people and nevertheless unable to correctly estimate the amounts of vegetables and whole grain served, and previous research highlighting that lower socioeconomic groups may find it difficult to interpret serving size recommendations, these findings may be of concern for nutrition educators (Drewnowski, Specter, 2004, Kamp et al, 2010, Robinson, 2008, Sinclair et al, 2013). Studies show that individuals from higher socio-demographic groups have a greater knowledge and comprehension of nutrition than individuals from low socio-demographic groups (Drewnowski, Specter, 2004, Pollard et al, 2008, Sinclair et al, 2013).

Furthermore, this study raises the question about whether self-estimation of portion sizes would be an appropriate way to evaluate intake of vegetables and whole grain cereals, particularly in self-serving settings, such as University Canteens, since it appears that the participants had very little ability to estimate their own intake (estimated serving vs actual serving were independent measures). Probably, this will be applicable to most other food groups, and thus this study challenges the way for instance communication towards healthy eating is delivered. It also calls for innovative ways to make the population aware of what portions are if the intention is to rely on the general ability to estimate quantities of food served or eaten.

Conclusion
The present study was the first to assess the accuracy of self-estimates of vegetables and whole grain portion sizes in a self-served meal conducted in a laboratory setting using an Intelligent Buffet combined with a quantitative questionnaire. When comparing the self-estimated amount of vegetables and whole grains with the actual amount, the analysis revealed a significant difference between the means (P < 0.001) where students’ self-reported portions were about three times bigger than the actual servings.

The study supports the use of innovative techniques such as the Intelligent Buffet. Further it supports the use of triangulation in order to obtain more precise estimations on intake and to better evaluate the real adherence to nutritional recommendations.

The questionnaires provided information on the profile of the participants and it appeared that there was little variety within the sample. Further limitations could be linked to the sample size and the methodological approach, particularly the equipment used for the data collection and data management, which had never been utilised prior to the study.

This study suggests that at least for this survey sample individuals do not provide accurate estimates of the amount of vegetables and whole grains they serve themselves on a plate. Our study findings lead us to question individuals’ ability to assess the amounts of vegetables and whole grain they serve themselves from a buffet. The degree of underestimation of servings within this small experimental group is high. On average participants assessed that they served themselves about three times what they actually did. Although there was a high awareness of the Danish 6 A Day recommendation among study participants, their ability to accurately estimate their serving size appeared to be a limited. These findings highlight the need for further research into the accuracy of self-estimated intake across the population and the implications of the overestimation of current consumption for nutrition promotion interventions.

Based on the findings of this study it is recommended that policy makers and social marketing campaign creators consider the development of more specific serving size measurements or that explicit communications e.g. regarding serves of plant foods used in nutrition education are needed to assist consumers in estimating own portions sizes in relation to general health recommendations.

How to Get Rid of Wasps in Your House

Wasps aren't really bad insects. They do good for us by pollinating flowers and otherwise contributing to our environment. Unfortunately, wasps are known to become aggressive around people and, if provoked, they will cause wasp stings that are itchy, red, and painful. They can also lead to dangerous allergic reactions in certain susceptible people.
How to Kill a Wasp in Your House

If you can't get the wasp to leave your home, you will need to kill it. There are several ways to kill a wasp, so it won't bother you anymore.

In the very beginning, you need to isolate the wasp. If it is flying all around the house, you should get all children and your pets out of the room. Turn on the lights so that you can actually see the wasp. If the lights are too high on the ceiling, the wasps will be attracted there and you may not be able to reach it. Then you may need a ladder and have a helper around to keep the ladder from tipping over. You can set out sugary drinks or food that will attract the wasp and turn on lamps that are lower than the ceiling if you don't have a ladder. Put on something thick and wear pants so that the wasp won't sting you on your bare skin. Then let's do the job.
1.  Use Spray

You can learn how to kill a wasp in your house by purchasing a can of spray that can kill the wasp, such as EcoExempt Jet Wasp and Hornet Killer. It is best to kill a wasp in another room of the house instead of the kitchen, so the spray doesn't get near food. Stand still and wait until the wasp is close enough to spray. Don't just spray a large cloud of the spray at the wasp. Instead, reach out with your arm and spray the insect. If you have done it right, the wasp will fall to the ground as soon as it is sprayed. Spray it again after it has fallen to the ground. Then grasp the wasp with a towel and dispose of it outside in the garbage can. Remember not to use toilet paper or tissues to pick up the wasp as it can still be alive and may sting you through the tissue. After spraying, clean the surfaces that might have been sprayed with water and soap.
2.  Use Flyswatter

This is an easy way to kill a wasp. If you have a fly swatter and only have a few wasps to tackle, a fly swatter will work very well. You need to have quick reflexes, because if you miss, you may get stung because you have irritated the wasp and it will come after you.
3.  Use Vacuum

If the wasp is not flying but is instead on the ground and walking on your floor, you can use a vacuum cleaner or a broom to get it off the floor and out of your house. It is best to do this during the springtime because wasps are just coming out of their hibernation state and are slower and more sluggish during that time. This is how to kill a wasp in your house when it is not that aggressive.
How to Prevent Wasps in Your House

If you don't feel like killing the wasp outright, you can do the following things to keep wasps out of the home:
1.  Keep Protein Foods Inside

Wasps mainly eat protein, so things that are high in protein should be kept insides rather than on your picnic table. This includes the protein in pet food as well. You should feed your pet indoors and this will keep wasps from being attracted to the pet food.
2.  Keep Sugar Away

While wasps primarily eat protein in the spring and summer, they tend to eat more sugar when it is fall time. Sugar increases the lifespan and reproductive abilities of the wasp, so they tend to gravitate toward it in the fall. If you are having a fall picnic, keep the sugary soda pops and sugary desserts inside.
3.  Set Out Some Sliced Cucumbers

If you are having an outdoor picnic and want to keep the wasps away, you can use cucumbers. Cucumbers are acidic and this is something wasps are not interested in. Cut cucumber into slices and keep them on around the barbecue area or picnic area. This will keep the wasps away so that you can enjoy your picnic.
4.  Keep Your Garbage Cans Sealed

While wasps are usually hunters, they will also scavenge for food. If your garbage can is not sealed tightly, the wasps will scavenge for the food in the container. You can also consider isolating out the protein and sugar you throw away into a separate garbage can. You don't have to lock up all your garbage that way, but make sure the can with the protein and sugar in it sealed tightly.
5.  Keep a Crumpled Brown Bag on the Ceiling

Wasps will not be attracted to an area where they believe that another wasp colony exists. If you crumple up a brown paper bag and attach it with string to the ceiling near the doors,wasps will not be able to tell the difference between a wasp's nest and a brown paper bag. They will stay away from your house and you won't have to worry about how to kill a wasp in your house.
6.  Seal the Siding of Your Home and Seal Up Awnings

Wasps like awnings and the siding of your house as places to build a wasp nest. If you seal these areas, the wasps won't be able to get through the siding and into your house. If you have a wasp nest near your home, wait until winter when they have died off and remove the nest before sealing up the house.

Summarize all the content in every paragraph into one paragraph with a word limit of 300 words, "Before you know the story of Akaike Technologies, it’s important to know the story of our Founder Rahul Thota and his tryst with data science and artificial intelligence in career spanning 20 years. It all began during his second year at college when he was trying to improve my grades with extra projects. Rahul decided to speak with his hostel warden, Professor Jalihal, and asked him if there was any work he could do.

Professor Jalihal came up with an interesting project for Rahul – converting a color image to black and white and then the other way around. Back then, we used MATLAB to handle this task. Using MATLAB, Rahul managed to convert the color image to black and white easily, although converting black and white to color wasn’t mathematically possible.

That project really got him into image processing, and Rahul went on to work on more projects and internships in the field. At the time, he was using tools like MATLAB and, eventually, Python became a popular alternative.

Over time, Rahul gained more experience by working with different companies and eventually started his own company, Akaike Technologies. Looking back, it’s fascinating to see how the tools and methods of learning have evolved throughout the years. Today, there are degree programs in AI/ML and a stronger emphasis on self-learning and problem-solving.

Overall, it’s been an incredible journey, and Rahul is grateful for the opportunities and advancements in the fields of image processing, computer vision, and AI that have made it possible to develop effective solutions for complex challenges we once considered impossible to solve.

You know what we realized? Python is like a cheat code for non-CS folks to do really cool things. Even though my job doesn’t involve coding, I still use it all the time. It’s such an approachable language that you can start seeing results quickly and that motivates you to keep going. Plus, you can do almost anything with it once you get the hang of it.

Speaking of programming languages, let’s talk about Matlab. Remember that image conversion project? It was easy to convert a color image to black and white in Matlab - just a few lines of code. But going from black and white to color is a totally different ball game. It’s mathematically impossible to go from one number (grayscale) to three (RGB) Nowadays, you can train an AI to do that kind of thing, but back then it was unheard of.

Anyway, Rahul always loved working with images and graphics, which is why he did a couple of internships at a government research institute. He got to play around with all sorts of satellite and remote sensing data, like capturing images of different types of vegetation. It was fascinating to see how different spectrums could indicate different ways that plants grew. Even water bodies had different characteristics! From there, Rahul moved on to work with medical imaging and surveillance at Siemens and Microsoft, where he did a ton of research on background subtraction and registering CT scans and MRIs. It was so cool to see how combining functional and structural information could give deeper insights.

But back in those early days, Rahul had to deal with very specific problems, like tracking customers walking into a showroom. There was no one “right” way for them to enter, which made it a tricky (and expensive) problem to solve. Neural networks were just a concept, and cloud adoption was just starting to become a thing. It’s amazing to see how far image processing and computer vision have come since then where you can even find solutions for unconstrained problems.

So, there are two different scenarios we can talk about. The first one is when we were working on replicating some facial detection work. We were able to come up with a new scheme where we could extract a million features from a small image. It was pretty cool because we could identify vertical and horizontal patterns at different scales, which helped us figure out the facial pattern. We did it all from scratch, which was a bit challenging!

But nowadays, things are a bit easier because you can find open source code for almost anything. This means that you can clone a project on GITHUB and replicate it. Before, a lot of the research time went into just trying to replicate past results, but now you have access to all the code. Reputed researchers even put out their own code.

In the AI space, the availability of models and new ideas with different architectures is amazing. You can search and get new pre-trained models online. But the problem isn’t finding a good face detection model or people detection model. It’s really about understanding the constraints of the client’s ecosystem and then making it work in the production setup. Once you have a solution, distributing it to the right people is what really matters. That’s the bottom line.

Previously, a small dataset of only 5000 models was available and it required a lot of time to create a large repository of data. However, now there are thousands of datasets available for different varieties. Back then, training a model would take a month due to the lack of high-level tools and the limited computing ability. Today, the same computation can be done in just a day. The availability of datasets and advances in computing have made a huge difference. In comparison to the past, there is now a much higher level of interest in AI across departments, with groups from different areas coming together to build AI solutions.

While in the past, coding was done mainly in C++, to build production models, it is no longer necessary to use low-level languages like C. The focus has shifted towards higher levels of abstraction, and there are now four-year degrees in AI from reputable institutes. While guided learning may be required when starting off, self-learning soon replaces it, and individuals become their own guide with maturity and confidence. While there is no clear answer as to whether CS or AI/ML degrees are better, the number of people graduating with guided data science degrees is increasing significantly.

There has been a lot of change in terms of the companies and sectors that were previously involved in AI research. Many companies have now spearheaded AI research, and the most promising latest research is coming from them. Even within companies, there has been a diversification of work, with a lot more focus on computer vision and advertising data.

One significant development in computer vision is the Voyalla and Johns paper, which has increased the detection accuracy of faces from 60% to 99.99%. There has been a flurry of papers published on people detection since then. Overall, advances in technology have made building companies around AI justified, and there is a lot of excitement about the possibilities."

Introduction:

Goal-setting is a popular strategy that has been utilized in a wide range of contexts, including education, sports, business, and personal development. The goal-setting theory (GST) developed by Locke and Latham in the 1960s posits that specific and challenging goals lead to improved performance through increased motivation and effort. Over the years, several technological approaches have been utilized in implementing GST, including mobile applications, social media, and gamification.

Technology-mediated goal setting (TMGS) has the potential to provide users with personalized, real-time feedback, increase engagement and motivation, and facilitate the achievement of goals. This paper provides a comprehensive review of the existing literature on TMGS and its impact on performance.

10 Most Underrated Marvel Comics, Ranked By
David Harth
Published Feb 14, 2023
Whether it's the Infinity Gauntlet's sequel story or the underappreciated Planet Hulk, Marvel's produced some brilliant, unappreciated masterpieces.
Marvel is the undisputed king of pop culture, and it all stems from the comics. Marvel's comics are at the top of the industry, and the best-known Marvel stories are considered among the best comics ever made. Over the years, the publisher's biggest stories have gotten all the plaudits they deserve, but there are plenty of Marvel comics that don't get the love they deserve.
RELATED: 10 Marvel Characters More Mature Than Their Parents
Marvel's best, most underrated comics have taken readers to amazing and unexpected places. Their stories have formed the bedrock of the Marvel Universe, but many readers have no idea that they exist. They've flown under the radar, but their quality means that fans need to learn to appreciate these modern masterpieces.
10
X-Force Is An Extremely Underrated Series
Marvel has some amazing must-read comics. The X-Men books have gotten loads of attention in the Krakoa Era, but not X-title gets the love it deserves. X-Force, written by Benjamin Percy with art by Robert Gill, follows the black ops arm of Krakoa and is a masterpiece of long-term storytelling that fits its premise better than most readers realize.
X-Force gives readers everything they could want. The book has intriguing plots, great action, and digs into its wide cast of characters. It constantly flies under the radar, but it deserves Marvel's spotlight. It's a book that every X-fan should read.
9
Children Of The Atom Deserves More Love
The Krakoa Era has given fans all kinds of stories, but they haven't all received the success they deserved. Children Of The Atom, by writer Vita Ayala and artists Bernard Chang and Paco Medina, is the six-issue story of a group of human cosplayers obsessed with the X-Men, all developing powers based on their favorite mutants.
Characterization is key to Children of the Atom, as its diverse cast is the most charming group of teen heroes introduced in a long time. The book's action is top-notch, the writing is wonderful, and it has some great mysteries. It's an underrated gem.
8
Strange Is A Perfect Modern Retelling Of Doctor Strange's Origin
The 2000s were an interesting time for Marvel, full of iconic books. Some did slip through the cracks, though, despite the stature of the characters and creators. Strange, written by J. Michael Straczynski with art by Brandon Peterson, should have attracted more notice, as it takes the plot beats of the original Doctor Strange origin and modernizes it.
RELATED: 10 Marvel Superheroes Who Overstayed Their Welcome
Starring familiar characters like Stephen Strange, Wong, Baron Mordo, and the Ancient One, the book set out to bring Strange into the 21st century. It succeeded beautifully, but the sales didn't warrant a complete series. This is a tragedy since Strange is brilliant from its first page to last.
7
Wolverine Is The Krakoa Era's Best Solo Series
Wolverine was one of Marvel's best comics of 2022. The first ongoing series for Wolverine since his return from death, the book is written by Benjamin Percy and currently drawn by Juan Jose Ryp. Much like the Percy-written X-Force, the book revolves around long-term plots, containing brilliant characterization and amazing action.
The current Wolverine is the most successful Wolverine comic in years. Percy understands the character like few other writers in recent years and gives the book's fans everything they could want. Wolverine understands its title character like few others, showing what other Marvel comics could be.
6
X-Men/Avengers: Utopia Is A Diamond In The Rough
The '00s featured some great X-Men books, but as the decade went on, the cracks started to show. However, the Dark Reign crossover X-Men/Avengers: Utopia, by writer Matt Fraction and artists Marc Silvestri, Luke Ross, Terry Dodson, and Mike Deodato Jr., closed out the decade perfectly. After riots and anti-mutant protests in the X-Men's new home of San Francisco, Norman Osborn's Dark Avengers take action against the team.
The book is an action-packed good time, full of great twists and turns throughout. Post-House Of M X-Men books have a depressing tone, but this book uses its dingy atmosphere well. The final battle alone is worth the price of admission, but this entire story is a banger.
5
The Sentry Is A Wonderful Psychological Marvel Tale
The Sentry, by writer Paul Jenkins and artist Jae Lee, introduced readers to Robert Reynolds, a man with multiple mental health issues who is having dreams about life as a superhero. These dreams reveal his secret. He is the forgotten superhero the Sentry, a hero who was forced to erase humanity's memories of him to avoid accidentally destroying the planet.
The book digs into the Sentry's history, revealing the danger he represents as a monstrous force called the Void returns. The book is a masterpiece throughout delving into the nature of humanity and heroism alike. The Sentry was meant to be a grittier Superman made to order for Marvel's world and this book established him beautifully.
4
Original Sin Is One Of Marvel's Most Underrated Events
Marvel events can be amazing, but some of the best don't get the praise they deserve. Original Sin, by writer Jason Aaron and artist Mike Deodato Jr., centered on the mystery of who killed The Watcher. With Doctor Midas, his daughter Oubliette, and the Orb running around with the Watcher's eye revealing terrible secrets, a group of heroes is drawn into a mystery that will change some of them forever.
Original Sin's biggest problem is that it never really delivered on its premise of revealing world-shaking secrets, but the main book is still a corker of a mystery. There are some great concepts in it and the art is amazing. The ending has some wonderful shocks, and it's a better event than most Marvel fans realize.
3
Earth X Details An Intriguing Marvel Future Based On Its Epic Past
Kingdom Come's look at a future DC Universe in the mid-'90s made Marvel fans want their own version. KC artist Alex Ross, working with writer Jim Krueger and the late great artist John Paul Leon, gave them that with Earth X. The series takes place in a dystopian Marvel future where everyone has superpowers. The Inhumans return to Earth with terrible news, as the heroes of yesteryear deal with a mysterious new threat called the Skull.
RELATED: 10 Short-Lived Redemptions In Marvel Comics
Earth X is both a lesson in Marvel history and an amazing view of its future. It's a love letter to the publisher's Silver Age while also being perfectly modern. It's everything anyone could love about the Marvel Universe in one wonderful package.
2
World War Hulk Is An Action-Packed Event Like No Other
World War Hulk stands apart as one of the '00s unheralded masterpieces. The Hulk and his alien Warbound return to Earth after the destruction of Sakaar, which they blame on the Illuminati. What follows is an action masterpiece pitting everyone against the Hulk.
Marvel events are built on other books, and sometimes fans need to read those to understand a new story. While it helps to read Planet Hulk, World War Hulk exists on its own terms. Hulk fighting the heroes is always awesome, and this book gives readers everything they need to understand what's going on without having to read its precursor. It's the best of the best.
1
Infinity War Is Overshadowed By Its Predecessor
Marvel has some brilliant six-issue series. Most people look at Infinity Gauntlet as the top of this heap, but they should include its sequel as well. Infinity War, by writer Jim Starlin and artist Ron Lim, gives readers the return of the Magus, an evil version of Adam Warlock, and his scheme to conquer the universe. It forces Warlock to team up with Thanos, as the heroes of Earth deal with their own problems.
Infinity War is full of great twists and turns. The plot is engaging and truly fits the book's epic scope. Starlin and Lim proved to be a great team on Infinity Gauntlet, and they deliver again here. It's much more interesting than its predecessor and deserves more love.

“As the smoke of Lucky Strike twirled around in shifting patterns, I sat on the edge of the drum riser. The crash cymbal trembled, as if to mourn our dear departed rhythm guitarist, Griffin Tiploft. With equal parts suspicion, curiosity, and mischief in our souls, we had parted ways with him, the man seemingly fond of secretive glances and hushed conversations.

Not that Les Orthogonaux were under any delusion of complete innocence. Our ever-so-acerbic tunes never failed to question the establishment, discarding subtle suggestions in favor of outright belligerence. But as with all things in life, perhaps we flew too close to the sun with our feathers clipped to pieces. The quiet Griffin had become a genuine enigma, and perhaps a threat.

Now, Otto Loosli, stoic and unbreakable, gave me “the look.” His fingers danced across his lead guitar’s fretboard, trapeze artists in a far-off circus tent. Next to him stood Reza Fazekas, who brought both sleaze and skill to our little communion, his bass grooving to an imaginary rhythm while he strummed his sitar, a snake charmer to our dormant rebellion.

The doorway creaked, and Reza pushed in a dramatic entrance. Between his fingers danced the wheels of a chair. “Voilá! Gentlemen,” Reza declared, “meet our brand new pianist!”

Seated in the wheelchair, a captivating figure, marred beauty glowing like a firefly in the smoky dimness. Burnt out wings in the form of scars decorated her face, and she clung nervously to a scarf, concealing a ghastly reminder of tragedy around her neck.

“Anneliese Ehn,” Reza proudly introduced her, “ye-ye extraordinaire!”

I furrowed my brow, lost for words as I tried to piece together this unsolved enigma. Yet, Otto appeared to be right at home, his pedantic predilections painting a vivid memory in his mind. “Ah, Anneliese,” he murmured. “The chanteuse who suffered a terrible accident not too long ago, wasn’t it? The one where you…”

She nodded, her eyes lowering behind her silken veil. “Yes, that’s… that’s me.” Her gaze shifted to the keys of a nearby piano. “But that hasn’t stopped me from playing, nor has it stolen my voice.”

As Anneliese trilled an ethereal siren’s call, I couldn’t help but wonder what had brought her and Reza together. I could see the undeniable attraction between them, and a pang of envy stroked my weary heart.

“Enough of the tragic opera,” Otto declared, his business-like demeanor shining through. “Tell us, Anneliese, what ideas do you have for Les Orthogonaux?”

With a secure grip on her scarf, she grinned, and for one ephemeral moment, I could glimpse the radiant beauty that once captivated the world. “Gentlemen,” she said, pausing for just the right dramatic effect, “I have a plan.”

So there would be life after Griffin; the stage would fill again with the freakbeat virtuosos. Les Orthogonaux would rise, and perhaps, under Anneliese’s scarred yet luminous wings, they might just touch the sun one more time.

My hands danced a waltz upon the drumheads. I was intoxicated by the atmosphere that now pulsed with Anneliese’s presence. The smoke swam through the room, wrapping her figure in a diaphanous haze. Every beat I drummed was for her, every crash a resounding echo of my growing obsession.

Otto’s keen eyes seemed to sense my torment. His voice interrupted my rhythmic longing, “Hey Marcel, loosen the grip on those drumsticks, will you? You’ll break them, or your knuckles, at this rate.”

With a sheepish laugh, I slackened my hold on the drumsticks, freeing the captive blood flow. “You know me, Otto, thunder comes naturally.”

As I sat there, I couldn’t get Anneliese out of my head. From the corner of my eye, I spotted her silhouette, as if tattooed in black ink on the dimly lit wall. Her scars glowed, a constellation of tragedy and resilience intertwined.

She sat demurely, her laughter tinting the air in a pleasant hue as Reza basked under the enchanting spell of her charm. Anneliese’s every lilting word seemed to pierce me, chipping away at the walls around my heart.

Otto chuckled, “Don’t you ever tire of walking in circles like a caged lion, Marcel?”

Relentless, my thoughts weaved a dizzying pattern of obsession, and with each step, I traced the unseen threads that bound me to Anneliese. I paced and circled, like a desperate creature, unable to cage the swarm of bees buzzing in my mind.

Her every gesture, the tilt of her head, and the enigmatic curve of her scars, all conspired against me. My old friend, melancholia, nestled warmly in my heart, expanding and pulsating with every glance I stole at her.

Suddenly, the swirling vortex of emotion gave birth to a concept. In my haunted mind, a song began to form, words and music entwining in a chaotic embrace. I approached Otto, my voice barely a whisper as I broached the idea.

“Otto, I have a song.”, I nervously confessed. “It’s about Anneliese.”

His eyes flickered, sensing the gravity of the moment. “Is it about the scars? The lust? The tragic dalliance between broken souls and serenades?” he inquired, his stoicism momentarily replaced by a lyrical fascination.

“All of that and more,” I replied, gripping my drumsticks tightly, ready to unleash the inner storm upon this veil of silence.

“Well, let’s hear it then, maestro,” Otto said, as a smirk played on his lips, his eyes gesturing towards Anneliese, whose attention remained devoted to a smirking Reza, beguiling snake charmer.

Blood rushed to my face as I hesitated, caught between my desire to whisper my love and my wounded pride. Perhaps Anneliese would never see me, the drummer in the shadows, beating a pulse that craved her attention.

But today, I would lay down the rhythm, the verse that would set the stage for our twisted love story, a monument to whispered confessions, invisible scars, and the relentless pursuit of desire.”
