​cassini's division by Quadeca & Thor Harris The Cassini Division is a region between two of Saturn’s rings. From Earth, it appears as a thin black gap in the rings. The rings represent a static that is inbetween, referencing the final moments of Quadeca’s ghost character dissolving into nothing but static between the world of the living and the afterlife.


After the spoken verse, we hear footsteps and distant whimpering from Quadeca, this could be that the character’s time in the afterlife is now running out and is not ready to accept his fate yet. Desperate to find a way out by running from being swallowed away, but ends up fading into the disintegrated and evaporating static. Disposing the character’s being and conscience all away. The static section that lasts for around 3 minutes represents the absorption and transition into the static after being finally moved on from the mourning of his living peers.

The moon, low and large on the horizon, threw long dark shadows into the corners and a cool blue light across the stony ground, disguising the blood-red stains on the sacrificial stone. Not a single creature stirred to disrupt the tranquility, not a leaf moved on the nearby trees. There was not a trace of life in that sinister place but for a single point of light glinting against the age-worn face of a statue.

Up there in the huge arms of the deity’s image sat a lone creature. In its powerful hand, it clutched a Globe of Seeing which it moved slowly from side to side examining its reflected features. As it did so, it recalled the events of that day: the frightened humans being herded into the central courtyard; the pathetic pleas for help as they were dragged one by one to the sacrificial stone; the final air-rending screams as the knife came down in one swift arc and the lifeblood ran.

And then a tired, resigned face being pushed towards the stone, a face that seemed of some importance to him. Yet how could that be when the lives of these people meant nothing to him? And, as the gleaming blade struck home, he recognized the agonized face of his father and suddenly the painful memories of all he had been came flooding back to him.

His masters had done this to him. They had been responsible for all that he was, and the loss of all he might have been. Well now they would pay. Now the warrior would stop killing for his masters and begin to exact his revenge.

Standing, he lifted the globe high above his head and, clenching his clawed hand, crushed it to a hundred tiny shards. Now the warrior’s head lifted towards the heavens. He searched the sky for the dark hulls of the ships whence his masters had come and let out a roaring howl that spoke his defiance. Nothing would stop him now. He would crush them as he had crushed their globe, and he would never stop until the blood of all of them and their creations soaked into the earth…

...flame red eyes stared out across the sun-scorched plain, scanning the distant treeline. The warrior was allowing himself a brief moment to enjoy the stillness - a respite before he once again set mind and body against the unnatural creatures his former masters had spawned.

Drawing in breath sharply, he flexed his hardened muscles, became aware of the blood rushing through his arteries, the double-hearts pounding furiously in his chest. The warrior knew that his power and speed were all that ensured his survival. The day that he became weak, the hour that he became slow, would be his last.

Setting off at a run he headed towards the trees, stirring up a trail of grey dust behind him. To have remained on the plain while the sun was high would have been fatal, but the shade of the trees most likely concealed a darker fate. As the warrior reached the first growths his instincts told him that he was unlikely to be alone for long. His eyes darted from side to side watching for the tiniest movement that would betray a predator.

When it came it did so with speed, rushing at him full on, its black wings crashing through the foliage, and from its blood-filled mouth came a piercing scream as it sighted its kill. The warrior had barely enough time to crouch as the creature flew at his head. Turning swiftly he brought his bone-covered fist up hard into the creature’s soft underbelly. Now it screamed in pain, spinning in mid-air and crashing down between the gnarled roots of a tree.

But the warrior ignored the body. Behind him had come another screech, too close this time. As he turned and dived for cover another winged demon flew at him, claws extended for the kill. The warrior struck out again but the creature was too quick and he felt its talons tearing into his abdomen as he rolled away into the undergrowth. He lay there panting, momentarily dazed. He could feel a warm wetness across his side and stomach, and with it a sharpening pain. But he concentrated on ignoring it. The wound would congeal soon enough, and if he stayed any longer he would lose far more than a little blood.

He stood slowly, fighting the waves of pain that passed through him, feeling his hearts pounding faster, but he had to be moving. Other creatures would smell the blood soon and he could not ward off another attack. As he stumbled between the trees he noticed something out of place against a distant trunk. Coming closer he realized that it was a doorway, a Beast-Mage construction, and hurried onwards. Thinking only of the things that would soon be trailing him, the warrior took a chance, and dropped through into the darkness.

He was just in time. Behind him there was a blood curdling scream, followed by a resounding crash as another winged creature failed to follow him through the opening. The warrior took only a moment to reflect that once more he had beaten the odds, before taking stock of his situation. There was a cool breeze coming from somewhere beneath him, and, now that the creature had retreated, a menacing silence fell, a silence punctuated only by the slow dripping of water deeper within the cavern.

As the warrior’s eyes became accustomed to the poor light, he made out a worn stairway curving away from him into the darkness. So, his suspicions were confirmed. He had stumbled upon another of his ex-masters’ outposts. They were becoming more frequent. Could this be the end of his long journey? Had he found the stronghold at last? There was only one way to find out…

Padding slowly down the stairway, the warrior readied himself for whatever abominations lay in wait at the bottom. Soon the stairway straightened out and the warrior found himself on a narrow ledge. The phosphorescence from the cavern walls was enough to reveal that he was in a huge chamber that extended away into pitch darkness.

Now the silence broke into distant sounds of angry growling. Whatever inhabited this place had obviously picked up his scent.

The ledge that he was on appeared to be deserted so he advanced slowly. Without warning there was a sizzling sound behind him. Instinctively the warrior jumped and felt his feet singe as a white hot fireball shot beneath him to explode violently against the cavern wall. So, his former masters were aware of his presence, and realised his intent. He would have to be twice as alert now. They would use every means in their power to stop him.

Ahead of him now he could make out a rough wooden ladder staked into the side of the ledge. Cat-like he swung himself onto it and began his descent, dropping silently the rest of the way when he saw another
ledge beneath him. No sooner had his feet hit the ground than a scaled abomination rushed forward, jaws grinding ferociously. The warrior turned calmly and despatched it with a single blow.

And so the ordeal went on as he worked his way deeper into the cavern. Creatures the like of which he had never seen beset him at every step, but each was destroyed with determination as the warrior remembered his
promise to himself to continue until every last one was dead.

He had just dropped onto another ledge when he caught a glimpse of something shining in the distance. Moving closer his keen vision made out a key sitting on a a shelf on the other side of a broad chasm. He started to move swiftly towards it when he was halted by an almighty roar. Suddenly he was confronted by a huge beast-like guard. His opponent took a short moment to size him up and then made a bellowing charge, swinging his double-headed battle axe at the warrior's head. Without losing a moment the warrior sidestepped the blow and slammed his fist into the guard's throat. There was a sickening crunch as the neck snapped, and the warrior stepped back quickly as the huge body tumbled forward over the ledge.

There was no moment for recovery before another hulking figure was rushing headlong at him. But this time the warrior was ready and his flying kick sent the guard plummeting backward to join his fellow. Now a
clear way lay ahead of him to the key. He sprinted along the remainder of the ledge and just as the edge of the abyss met him he leapt forward, hands reaching out to grasp the edge of the shelf.

He made it in a shower of dirt and stones, and caught his breath as he hung swinging above a seemingly bottomless chasm. He could feel his sweaty hands begin to lose their grip. If he let go now he would almost
certainly die. If he made it onto the shelf, who knew where the key might lead him? Now the warrior's true destiny would be decided...

Rewrite as an expert tech writer for Analytics India Magazine and the founder of Akaike Technologies. There must be a good flow of ideas from one paragraph to another and the tone of the article must be narrative, newsletter, conversational and informative style. The article is for the B2B decision makers to read. Maintain the same word limit and include all statistics and important points in the article, "AI is increasingly transforming various industries, such as banking, healthcare, and media, aiming to boost efficiency and profitability. McKinsey’s global survey reveals that 50% of enterprises have embraced AI in at least one business area, with adoption set to double in the coming years. However, AI development and integration pose challenges, including inadequate understanding of the technology, limited data and skilled personnel, and difficulty pinpointing relevant business cases. Moreover, a mere 11% of organizations utilizing AI in their operations have witnessed significant returns on investment. To tackle these obstacles and effectively incorporate AI into a company’s digital transformation agenda, businesses must address an array of technological, ethical, and organizational concerns.

In this article, I'll discuss three common challenges enterprises face when implementing AI and their practical solutions.

Problem 1: 

Machine-learning models often require vast, diverse datasets to function effectively, yet obtaining such data remains a challenge. Insufficient data can lead to overfitting, biased models, and poor performance. 

Data shortages result from data privacy constraints, a lack of historical data for new trends, small sample sizes, and the costly, time-consuming nature of manual data labeling. Acquiring permission to use sensitive data is challenging, and historical data often proves inadequate due to evolving trends and processes. However, adequate training data is essential for creating unbiased and accurate ML models capable of addressing a wide range of scenarios.

Solution:

Synthetic data serves as a viable solution to help overcome these challenges. When generated artificially to resemble real datasets, synthetic data can reveal hidden patterns, interactions, and correlations between variables, offering a substantial foundation for ML models. Balancing datasets and improving performance, synthetic data synthesis can supplement marginal classes without jeopardizing privacy.

Advancements in synthetic data have significantly increased its value for machine learning models. Techniques like generative adversarial networks (GANs) and Wasserstein GANs (WGANs) foster the creation of more realistic data while maintaining compliance and data balance. As technology evolves, synthetic data will play a critical role in overcoming data shortages, safeguarding user privacy and enhancing ML model efficiency and intelligence, becoming a practical solution for AI professionals and organizations alike.


Problem 2: 

The global shortage of AI talent is slowing advancements in areas like deep learning, natural language processing, and robotic process automation. This ends up hindering innovation and ecomonic growth. A survey by Indeed found that 63% of decision makers reported the greatest skills gap in AI and ML. While a clear solution emerged where 75% preferred to invest in upskilling of current staff and 64% favored bringing on new experts —it was not without its challenges, like budget limitations or retention woes of upskilled employees.

Solution

In today’s talent shortage landscape, CIOs are increasingly embracing low-code/no-code platforms to democratize app development and ease workloads in IT departments. Faced with an estimated 85.2 million global software engineer deficit by 2030, businesses have found that low-code/no-code tools can increase their value by millions of dollars without hiring additional IT developers. The market for low-code technologies is projected to grow to $44.5 billion by 2026, with multiple converging factors such as faster application delivery demands, ongoing talent shortages, and a growing hybrid workforce. Low-code platforms have become an essential element of successful hyperautomation, with 50% of all new clients expected to come from business buyers outside the IT organization by 2025. 

However, to achieve their full market potential, low-code/no-code platforms must continually innovate in areas like AI and machine learning capabilities, real-time iteration, devops workflows integration, scalability, API development focus, and support for multiple personas. As digital transformation projects become a priority, CIOs and their teams are realizing that low-code platforms can accelerate app development by up to ten times. The future of these platforms will likely involve expanded capabilities for API integration, AI/ML services, and broader enterprise application support.



Problem 3:

AI spending has reached nearly $118 billion in 2022 and surpass $300 billion in the next few years. The cost of AI varies, with companies paying anywhere from $0 to over $300,000 for software, including custom solutions that range from $6,000 to over $300,000. Some factors that influence AI costs include the type of AI software (chatbots, analysis systems, or virtual assistants), whether a pre-built or custom solution is chosen, AI features required, and how AI is managed (in-house or outsourced). Project duration and complexity also impact the overall cost. 

Solution

In addressing the pressing issue of high costs associated with AI implementation for enterprises, it becomes crucial to optimize data processing methods applicable to multiple forms of data, including videos, text, and other pertinent information depending on size and nature. By adopting strategic and efficient deployment techniques, it is possible to achieve substantial cost reduction.

Companies can take advantage of pre-built options, with a range of AI platforms available at various price points to make AI a worthwhile investment for businesses of all sizes.

Employing dedicated hardware for video processing and leveraging edge computing solutions can be a more cost-effective alternative to relying exclusively on cloud-based solutions. This multifaceted approach not only streamlines data management processes but also significantly curbs the overall expenses associated with AI integration in the contemporary business landscape.

Conclusion

Creating a comprehensive AI implementation plan doesn't have to be difficult. Companies should become familiar with any potential implementation challenges and develop a strategy in order to mitigate them. This includes crafting suitable interfaces, establishing necessary regulations, setting up data infrastructure, designing storage solutions, labeling data sets and feeding information into the AI system. Additionally, it's important to train models in order to produce useful artificial intelligence output which can then be tested through feedback loops and data sampling measures."

makes you squirm the mostWhen it comes to Greek mythology, some of the stories you'll find out there are pretty strange. Goddesses being birthed from clam shells, women being kidnapped by Hades, and plenty of stories of cheating god husbands (we're looking at you here, Zeus). But if you go past the more well-known myths, things start to get even weirder. Yes, those wacky gods on Mount Olympus have a serious strange streak that would shock even the most open-minded historian.
This is not to say that that these Greek myths aren't entertaining. I mean, come on, hearing about gods impregnating clouds or mortals being cursed for eternity is just oodles of fun, right? Just keep in mind that these myths were meant as life lessons for the Greeks - though for a few of these, the meaning is either pretty obscure or just too weird to imagine.
So, if you're up for a little lesson in bizarre mythology, read on. And if you take away two major points from these stories, we hope they're the following: Don't piss off the gods, because they're jerks; and Zeus really, really needs to learn to keep it in his pants. Photo: Johann Wilhelm Baur / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain15,312 VOTESErysichthon Is So Hungry That He Eats Himself To DeathOn the theme of "never piss off the gods," let's have a look at Erysichthon. This man was incredibly greedy and incredibly rich, and really didn't pay the gods much mind. One day, he cut down a sacred grove of trees in order to build another feast hall, as the rich are wont to do. Demeter was slighted by this, and decided to punish him. She gave him an appetite so strong that he ate everything. He ate all the food he had, then all the food he could buy, until he had completely exhausted his wealth.He even tried to sell his own daughter for food! This reduced him to such poverty that he lost all standing, his home, everything. When he had nothing else left, he turned on himself and died eating his own flesh off his body. 5,312 votesPhoto: After Michelangelo / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain24,222 VOTESLeda Gets Down And Dirty With A SwanThroughout all of mythology, Zeus sleeps with basically everyone: gods, demigods, mortals, animals, and even sometimes with mortals while disguised as animals. One of the strangest myths involving this is that of Leda and the swan.
In the story, Zeus sees Leda and admires her from afar. In order to get with her, he transforms into a swan and then seduces her. How does a swan seduce a woman? We're not sure, and we probably don't want to know. The two mated, and from this union came two sets of twins. One of these children (born from an egg, no less) was named Helen.
Yes, as in Helen of Troy, allegedly the most beautiful woman alive. The mythology makes no note, however, if her mother ever told her that she was half Zeus-swan.
4,222 votesPhoto: Walter Crane / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain33,706 VOTESPan Creates The Pan Flute Out Of A Woman Who Rejected HimPan is, in general, a pretty nasty guy. Some myths say he was birthed from Penelope, Odysseus's wife, while others say it was a nymph, or even Aphrodite herself. He was known for tending, and sometimes having relations with his sheep, and for wanting to get with basically every female he ever laid eyes on.
One such nymph, Syrinx, really wasn't very open to these advances, and fled from Pan. The creepy half-goat half-man followed her, chasing her through the woods. Eventually, Syrinx became so fed up with this, that she got a river god to transform her into something Pan couldn't possible lust after: a bunch of reeds.
But Pan was so determined to have her, that he decided to take a piece of her with him everywhere. He used the reeds to craft a flute, what is known today as the pan flute. It just goes to show that stalker-like behavior stretches back thousands of years.
3,706 votesPhoto: Giovanni Francesco Romanelli / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain43,761 VOTESKronos Eats All The Children He Can Produce And Cuts Off His Dad's PenisBack when the world was just beginning, at least according to the Greeks, there was a Titan God named Kronos (or Cronus, depending on our records). He was birthed of a god called Ouranos, also called The Sky, and married to a goddess called Rhea. He was power-obsessed in the worst way, and proved this by castrating his father in order to become the top dog. From there, because he feared one of his own children would overthrow him, he proceeded to eat every single one of his offspring. Well, except for one, named Zeus, who did eventually overthrow him. And by "overthrow," we mean that Zeus grew up, sought out Kronos, and forced him to vomit up all the baby gods he'd eaten over the years. The gods were somehow still alive, and a great war raged for over a decade between them and the Titans, with Zeus and his siblings eventually winning out.3,761 votesPhoto: Jules-Élie Delaunay / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain52,747 VOTESIxion Impregnates A Cloud To Create CentaursThe story goes that Ixion was in exile for certain crimes against fellow humans, when Zeus took pity on him and invited him to come to Olympus as a guest (you'll figure out pretty quickly in this list that Zeus doesn't make a lot of great decisions). Once there, he saw Hera and became completely obsessed with getting her into bed. To test his loyalty, Zeus created a cloud version of Hera, which Ixion then somehow impregnated.
How does one impregnate a cloud? It's hard to say, but we do know that Zeus became angry and punished Ixion by transforming him into a giant flaming wheel. And what of his cloud offspring? He was named Centaurus, and was known for mating with a whole bunch of horses, and from that came the mythical creature known as the Centaur, half man and half horse. Makes all those centaur scenes from Harry Potter seem a little bit more uncomfortable now, doesn't it?
2,747 votesPhoto: Psiax / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain62,604 VOTESDionysus Gestates In Zeus's ThighThere have been some weird births in Greek mythology, a few of which we'll touch on more later, but Dionysus, god of wine and good times, was probably has one of the strangest. It all begins with Zeus, again, not being able to keep it in his pants. He slept with a mortal princess named Semele, and she became pregnant with his child.
Hera, Zeus's wife, took issue with this, as she usually did, and decided to ruin everything. She sewed seeds of doubt in the girl's mind, and eventually Semele asked to see Zeus in full, to know that he really existed and really loved her. Zeus had sworn an oath to do anything for her, and was bound to do it.
Of course, seeing Zeus in all his glory killed any mortal who looked, and the princess was engulfed in flame. Even though his new lover was dead, Zeus was determined to save their unborn baby. So, of course, he took him from her womb and put him someplace safe - in his own thigh. Zeus managed to keep the baby there until he was big enough to emerge.

summarise this: There are three forms of the personal name based on Hermanubis in papyri and inscriptions from Egypt, in addition to two "proper" polytheophoric names. (In four instances [1, 5, 17, 21] the ending of the name is abbreviated or not preserved.) The form Ἑρμάνουβιϲ, attested six times (2, 9, 11, 13, 14, 15), simply replicates the god's name without further ado, through the phenomenon of "direct theonymy" (the giving to humans of unaltered divine names) that became widespread in the Roman period.[8] In an odd instance (9) the form Ἑρμανουβιϲ in the nominative is borne by a woman, an Alexandrian nurse (ἡ τροφόϲ).[9] It is highly unusual for female theophoric names to be unmarked for gender in this way.[10] I suspect that because the element -ανουβιϲ in Ἑρμάνουβιϲ was often declined with a dental declension in -ιδ-, it might have been conflated with the similar declension of Greek feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ.[11] If this is correct, it may be preferable to read the nurse's name as an oxytone (Ἑρμανουβίϲ). There are two further forms based on the god Hermanubis: Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (six instances: 3, 4, 7, 8, 12, 20) and Ἑρμανουβίων (four instances: 3, 6, 18, 19). -ᾶϲ and -ίων were, in fact, the commonest Greek suffixes for personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ.[12] It is interesting that in a business letter (3) the two forms Ἑρμανουβίων (l.38) and Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (l.41) are used interchangeably to designate the same individual. In this case, Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ is probably a hypocoristic of Ἑρμανουβίων, since the suffix -ᾶϲ often served this purpose.[13]

Dim counter, pad
counter = 0
strMessage = “RUN!!!”

'Open 10 notepads with “RUN!!!” in them
Do Until counter = 10
Set pad = CreateObject(“WScript.Shell”)
pad.Run(“notepad”)
WScript.Sleep 1000 'wait for the notepad to open
pad.SendKeys strMessage
counter = counter + 1
Loop

'Set background color to dark red
Set oShell = CreateObject(“WScript.Shell”)
oShell.RegWrite “HKCU\Control Panel\Colors\Background”, “64 00 00”

'Beep continuously until the script is terminated from the Task Manager
Do While True
For i = 1 To 5 'Beep 5 times
Beep
WScript.Sleep 1000
Next
If CheckTaskManager() = False Then
Exit Do
End If
Loop

'Reset background color to default
oShell.RegDelete “HKCU\Control Panel\Colors\Background”

Function Beep()
dim objShell
Set objShell = Wscript.CreateObject(“Wscript.Shell”)
objShell.Run “CMD /C ““Echo vbTab & Chr(7)”””, 0, True
End Function

Function CheckTaskManager()
Set wmi = GetObject(“winmgmts:” & “{impersonationLevel=impersonate}!.\root\cimv2”)
Set ProcessList = wmi.ExecQuery(“SELECT * FROM Win32_Process”)
For Each Process In ProcessList
If InStr(Process.Name, “wscript.exe”) And InStr(Process.CommandLine, WScript.ScriptFullName) Then
CheckTaskManager = True
Exit Function
End If
Next
CheckTaskManager = False
End Function

summarise this: There are three forms of the personal name based on Hermanubis in papyri and inscriptions from Egypt, in addition to two "proper" polytheophoric names. (In four instances [1, 5, 17, 21] the ending of the name is abbreviated or not preserved.) The form Ἑρμάνουβιϲ, attested six times (2, 9, 11, 13, 14, 15), simply replicates the god's name without further ado, through the phenomenon of "direct theonymy" (the giving to humans of unaltered divine names) that became widespread in the Roman period.[8] In an odd instance (9) the form Ἑρμανουβιϲ in the nominative is borne by a woman, an Alexandrian nurse (ἡ τροφόϲ).[9] It is highly unusual for female theophoric names to be unmarked for gender in this way.[10] I suspect that because the element -ανουβιϲ in Ἑρμάνουβιϲ was often declined with a dental declension in -ιδ-, it might have been conflated with the similar declension of Greek feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ.[11] If this is correct, it may be preferable to read the nurse's name as an oxytone (Ἑρμανουβίϲ). There are two further forms based on the god Hermanubis: Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (six instances: 3, 4, 7, 8, 12, 20) and Ἑρμανουβίων (four instances: 3, 6, 18, 19). -ᾶϲ and -ίων were, in fact, the commonest Greek suffixes for personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ.[12] It is interesting that in a business letter (3) the two forms Ἑρμανουβίων (l.38) and Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (l.41) are used interchangeably to designate the same individual. In this case, Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ is probably a hypocoristic of Ἑρμανουβίων, since the suffix -ᾶϲ often served this purpose.[13]

import time
import numpy as np
from pymavlink import mavutil
from multiprocessing import Process, Manager

class Drone:
    def __init__(self, sysid):
        self.sysid = sysid
        self.current_pos = np.zeros(3)

class SwarmDroneController:
    def __init__(self, master_sysid, follower_sysids):
        self.master = Drone(master_sysid)
        self.followers = [Drone(sysid) for sysid in follower_sysids]
        self.connection = mavutil.mavlink_connection('/dev/ttyUSB0')
        self.connection.wait_heartbeat()
        self.current_ned = np.zeros(3)
        self.waypoints = []
        self.distance_map = Manager().dict()  # for swarm communication and distance to master

    def set_mode(self, drone, mode):
        self.connection.mav.set_mode_send(drone.sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_MODE_FLAG_CUSTOM_MODE_ENABLED, mode)
        time.sleep(1)

    def arm_and_takeoff(self, drone, altitude):
        self.connection.mav.command_long_send(
            drone.sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_COMPONENT_ARM_DISARM, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
        time.sleep(1)
        self.connection.mav.command_long_send(
            drone.sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_NAV_TAKEOFF, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, altitude)
        time.sleep(5)

    def add_waypoint(self, waypoint):
        self.waypoints.append(waypoint)

    def send_position_target(self, drone, ned_pos):
        self.connection.mav.set_position_target_local_ned_send(
            0, drone.sysid, 0, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_FRAME_LOCAL_NED,
            mavutil.mavlink.POS_TARGET_TYPE_LOCATION | mavutil.mavlink.POS_TARGET_TYPE_VELOCITY,
            ned_pos[0], ned_pos[1], ned_pos[2], 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
        time.sleep(0.1)

    def goto_next_waypoint(self):
        if len(self.waypoints) == 0:
            return False

        next_global_waypoint = self.waypoints.pop(0)
        self.send_position_target(self.master, self.current_ned + next_global_waypoint)

        return True

    def formation_follow_master(self, formation_coords):
        def follow_position(follower, target_pos):
            while True:
                follower.current_pos = target_pos
                self.send_position_target(follower, self.current_ned + target_pos)
                time.sleep(0.1)

        processes = []
        for i, follower in enumerate(self.followers):
            target_coords = np.array(self.master.current_pos) + formation_coords[i]
            process = Process(target=follow_position, args=(follower, target_coords))
            processes.append(process)
            process.start()

        try:
            while self.goto_next_waypoint():
                for i, process in enumerate(processes):
                    process.join(timeout=1)
        finally:
            for process in processes:
                process.terminate()

    def update_local_positions(self):
        local_position = self.connection.messages.get("LOCAL_POSITION_NED", None)
        if local_position is not None:
            self.master.current_pos = np.array([local_position.x, local_position.y, local_position.z])

# Usage
formation_shape = np.array([[-5, 0, 0], [5, 0, 0], [0, 5, 0], [0, -5, 0]])
controller = SwarmDroneController(2, [3, 4, 5, 6])

# Arm and takeoff both drones
controller.set_mode(controller.master, 4)  # Set mode to Guided
for follower in controller.followers:
    controller.set_mode(follower, 4)

controller.arm_and_takeoff(controller.master, 10)
for follower in controller.followers:
    controller.arm_and_takeoff(follower, 10)

# Add waypoints
controller.add_waypoint(np.array([10.0, 10.0, -10.0]))
controller.add_waypoint(np.array([20.0, 20.0, -20.0]))

# Send master drone to waypoints, followers follow in formation
controller.formation_follow_master(formation_shape)

# After the operation has completed, set mode to RTL and disarm
controller.set_mode(controller.master, 6)  # Set mode to RTL
for follower in controller.followers:
    controller.set_mode(follower, 6)

controller.arm_and_takeoff(controller.master, 0)
for follower in controller.followers:
    controller.arm_and_takeoff(follower, 0)

controller.close()

from pymavlink import mavutil
import time

class DualDroneController:
    
    def __init__(self, master_sysid, follower_sysid):
        self.master_sysid = master_sysid
        self.follower_sysid = follower_sysid
        self.master_connection = None
        self.follower_connection = None
        self.master_pos = None
        self.follower_pos = None
        
    def connect(self, master_address, follower_address):
        self.master_connection = mavutil.mavlink_connection(master_address, source_system=self.master_sysid)
        self.follower_connection = mavutil.mavlink_connection(follower_address,source_system=self.follower_sysid)
        self.master_connection.wait_heartbeat()
        self.follower_connection.wait_heartbeat()
        self.master_connection.target_system = self.master_sysid
        self.master_connection.target_component = 0
        self.follower_connection.target_system = self.follower_sysid
        self.follower_connection.target_component = 0
        
    def arm_and_takeoff(self, altitude):
        # arm and takeoff master drone
        self.master_connection.mav.command_long_send(
            self.master_sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_COMPONENT_ARM_DISARM, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
        time.sleep(1)
        self.master_connection.mav.command_long_send(
            self.master_sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_NAV_TAKEOFF, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, altitude)
        time.sleep(5)
        
        # arm and takeoff follower drone
        self.follower_connection.mav.command_long_send(
            self.follower_sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_COMPONENT_ARM_DISARM, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
        time.sleep(1)
        self.follower_connection.mav.command_long_send(
            self.follower_sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_NAV_TAKEOFF, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, altitude)
        time.sleep(5)
        
    def send_waypoints(self, x, y, z):
        self.master_connection.mav.send(
            mavutil.mavlink.MAVLink_set_position_target_local_ned_message(
                0, self.master_sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
                mavutil.mavlink.MAV_FRAME_LOCAL_NED, int(0b010111111000), 0, 0, 0, x, y, z, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0))
        
    def get_master_position(self):
        msg = self.master_connection.recv_match(type='LOCAL_POSITION_NED', blocking=True)
        self.master_pos = (msg.x, msg.y, msg.z)
        
    def calculate_follower_position(self):
        # assume the follower is 5 meters behind the master
        dx = -5
        dy = 0
        dz = 0
        if self.master_pos is not None:
            x, y, z = self.master_pos
            self.follower_pos = (x+dx, y+dy, z+dz)
        
    def send_follower(self):
        if self.follower_pos is not None:
            x, y, z = self.follower_pos
            self.follower_connection.mav.send(
                mavutil.mavlink.MAVLink_set_position_target_local_ned_message(
                    0, self.follower_sysid, mavutil.mavlink.MAV_COMP_ID_SYSTEM_CONTROL,
                    mavutil.mavlink.MAV_FRAME_LOCAL_NED, int(0b010111111000), 0, 0, 0, x, y, z, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0))
        
    def calculate_speeds(self):
        # assume constant speed of 3 m/s for both drones
        self.master_speed = (3, 0, 0)
        self.follower_speed = (3, 0, 0)
    def accept_coordinates(self):
        x = float(input("Enter x coordinate in meters: "))
        y = float(input("Enter y coordinate in meters: "))
        z = float(input("Enter z coordinate in meters: "))
        self.send_waypoints(x, y, z)
    def follow_master(self):
        while True:
            self.get_master_position()
            self.calculate_follower_position()
            self.send_follower()

Summerize and rewrite the following text: Identifying & addressing learning gaps
Many students in this year’s incoming class experienced COVID-related academic disruptions, but not every student was affected in the same way. This resulted in significant gaps in student learning, and instructors should be aware that some students may need extra support.

As an instructor, there are several steps you can take to support students and help them adjust to studying at Cornell, including forming connections, identifying gaps and normalizing getting help, and checking in early and assessing student knowledge often.


Form connections
Build a sense of community and belonging early in your course and develop your instructor presence: a sense of connection as a student can help prevent feelings of isolation. Students are more likely to successfully complete the course if they feel connected to you, their classmates, and the course content.
Provide opportunities for students to interact: collaborative activities and icebreakers allow students to get to know each other through sharing experiences, values, and perspectives.
Encourage students to form study groups: explain the importance of a study partner or group and offer opportunities to connect with study partners:
help match students with a study partner by using questions from this sample questionnaire
direct students to the Learning Strategies Center’s program for finding study partners.
return to top of the page


Identify gaps and normalize getting help
Acknowledge the learning gap with the class: remind students that they are not alone and that uneven preparation for Cornell is not their fault. Many spent part of their junior year and all of their senior year in challenging online settings.
Identify the extent of the gap for your content area: learn what your students do and do not know. You may decide to provide a concept test, practice assessments, or a survey.
Describe ways to overcome challenges and get help:
Share experiences in which you, TAs, or students felt challenged and how you overcame the obstacles.
Let students know that not only is it acceptable to make mistakes, but also that such struggles are an essential part of learning and growing.
Offer several resources for regular help, including TA check-ins, office hours, review sessions, online question-and-answer forums, and the Learning Strategies Center’s programs.
Encourage students to talk about their concerns and struggles.
Emphasize that the earlier instructors and TAs are aware of your situation, the better they are able to support you.
Peer Support: guide students on strategies for helping their peers in positive, supportive ways.  Encourage them to ask for help from a classmate.  When students learn together by explaining concepts to one another, it reinforces their understanding of the material.
return to top of the page


Check-in early and assess often
Alert students early about their progress in the course: students might not even know they are behind, and without early feedback, they may not have an opportunity to remedy the situation before the end of the semester. Help prevent such situations with:
early feedback
displaying grades visibly in Canvas
incorporating opportunities for assistance for those who are struggling
Provide several low stakes assessments and distribute them evenly throughout the semester to give students an opportunity to recover if they have a rough start. This will offer students plenty of opportunities to demonstrate their learning, practice skills, and adapt to academic life at Cornell.
Check-in with students often to see what is working or not, and make modifications to the course based on their feedback.
Ideas include:

checking in during class verbally or with a classroom poll
surveying students with the Mid-Semester Feedback Program
opening up a discussion board
having TAs meet regularly with designated groups
To discuss ideas and strategies for supporting students, visit our online drop-in sessions or contact CTI to set up a consultation.

The Onomastic Evidence for the God Hermanubis
Amin Benaissa

The name Ἑρμάνουβιϲ is a polytheophoric name from Roman Egypt, which combines the names of two deities. However, unlike other polytheophoric names, it refers to a specific deity named Hermanubis. This has implications on how personal names based on this deity are formed. The article gives an overview of what is known about Hermanubis and compares onomastic evidence to his iconography and documented sightings.
The divine name “Hermanubis” is a result of the assimilation of the Egyptian god Anubis to the Greek god Hermes, with several literary, epigraphic, and artistic sources supporting this. The name is mostly known from Roman period inscriptions and literary references, with the earliest attestation being a second-century BC inscription from Delos. Although there is an iconographical type in Roman Alexandria featuring a fully anthropomorphic young god flanked by a dog and holding the same attributes as Anubis, it is important to note that none of the numerous representations of this type bear a legend or inscription identifying it as “Hermanubis.”
There are multiple forms of the name Hermanubis found in papyri and inscriptions in Egypt, including the direct replication of the god’s name and two “proper” polytheophoric names. One instance of the name is borne by a woman, which is unusual for female theophoric names. The suffixes -ᾶϲ and -ίων are commonly used in personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ, and the two forms based on Hermanubis are used interchangeably in a business letter, with one likely being a hypocoristic of the other.
Names containing the modified suffixes of Ἑρμάνουβιϲ distinguish Hermanubis from other polytheophoric names that usually combine two divine names without modifying the suffix. Personal names that combine Hermanubis with other gods suggest the juxtaposition of only two gods and should not be analyzed as triple theophoric names. These names are not exceptions to the general rule that polytheophoric names combine no more than two deities.
Most names based on Hermanubis are found in papyri from the second and third centuries AD. The earliest example is from an ostracon dated to AD 94, but it is abbreviated. There are eight instances from the second century, eight from the third, and three from the fourth. The name disappears from the evidence after the fourth century. The chronological distribution corresponds to the iconographical representations of the fully anthropomorphic god known as “Hermanubis.” Statues, statuettes, and carved reliefs of this god have all been assigned to the second or third century AD.
Hermanubis names are found on papyri from various areas of Egypt, but most individuals with these names are from Alexandrian or Cynopolite. Alexandria appears to be the birthplace and cult center of the anthropomorphic Hermanubis, as evidenced by inscriptions, statues, and coins found in the city. The onomastic evidence connects Hermanubis with the Cynopolite nome, where one individual with a Hermanubis name was likely a priest of Anubis, Leto, and associated gods. Iconography on some coins also connects Hermanubis with the Cynopolite nome.
An onomastic survey reveals that names based on Hermanubis are different from other polytheophoric names in Roman Egypt, as they refer to a specific and independently attested god. The overlap between the onomastic evidence and representations of the fully anthropomorphic Alexandrian god known as Hermanubis supports the designation of the latter as Hermanubis. The connection of Hermanubis with Cynopolis and a priest of Anubis suggests a continued association with the traditional Anubis, and a separation between the traditional funerary Anubis and Hermanubis may not be necessary.
The name Hermanubis is found outside of Egypt in an inscription from Macedonia and is likely related to the cult of Hermanubis in Thessalonike. The cult of Hermanubis seems to have been popular outside of Egypt in the Roman period.

The names in 838-839 are likely fictional and used as satire to associate Egyptians and their church with pagan animal worship. Most of the names are Egyptian, and the proximity of Anubis and Hermanubis suggests they are closely related. The adjective “kynodeis” in 840 does not necessarily indicate Hermanubis is a canine-headed god, as it may have been triggered only by the name Anubis. The term “pithekomorphoi” does not correlate with any of the mentioned theophoric names.

A person named Hermanoubes has turned into a dog and captured two thieves who have been punished before their time. The speaker believes that a person’s character is not defined by their location, but rather their character defines the location.
The form “Hermanoubes” is not found in any documents, but it is suggested that it should be changed to “Hermanoubis.” It is unclear whether Hermanoubes is the subject or predicate of the sentence “he became a dog,” and there is some uncertainty about the implications of calling someone a “dog” and the relationship between the person and two thievish gods. The satire would be more effective if the general was actually named Hermanoubes/Hermanubis, as it would play on the associations of his name with Anubis and Hermes.
The article discusses the naming and origins of the god Hermanubis, a fusion of the Greek god Hermes and the Egyptian god Anubis. While the combination of two gods’ names was not common in Greek religion, it had parallels in earlier forms of worship. The article also explores the association between Anubis and Hermes in literary and epigraphic sources and examines various representations of the deity in art. Lastly, the article discusses the gender and suffixes used in Greek personal names.
There are three exceptions to the general rule, with two ending in -ηράκλειοϲ and one ending in -απολλώνιοϲ.
The ending -άμμων was commonly added to the names of deities in theophoric names, so the combination of Ἑρμάνουβιϲ may not be significant. Some numismatic representations of “Hermanubis” show him with attributes of both Hermes and Ammon. The origin of the name Ἡρακλερμάνουβιϲ is unclear, but it could be a juxtaposition of the chief god of a nome with Hermanubis. The form Ἑρμανούβιοϲ is not found in Egyptian documents, but is attested in a Macedonian inscription. There is debate around the associations between Hermanubis and Sarapis in iconography, and an acting epistrategus Hermanubis is mentioned in Plutarch’s De Is. et Os. The name Ἑρμανουβίαινα has also been found in a papyrus. Some related works were not available to the author.

1. Purpose: Develop a set of tools that can help to recover a damaged file system. For many reasons, the information in a directory file, in an inode etc. may get damaged and cannot be read. As a result, a user program will not be able to access parts of the file system. In this exercise, we ask you to develop a set of tools and show that you can use them to recover a xv6 file system if one or more of its directories and/or inodes are damaged.
2. Requirements:
1) Your tools and recovery program will work on the xv6 file system.
2) (10%) Write a program, called the directoryWalker, that prints out the names of each file and directory in a file system tree, starting at a given directory in the tree. The program also prints out the inodes associated with each file and directory.
3) (10%) Write a program, called the imapWalker, that prints out all the allocated inodes.
4) (10%) Write a program, called the inodeReader, that will return information on a specific inode.
5) (10%) Write a program that can erase the information (including the block pointers) in a directory inode and in a directory file so that you can test and demonstrate your results.
6) (25%) Write a program that will repair the file system when the following situations occur. In those situations that you cannot completely repair the file system, gather and present information to the user so that it is easiest for him/her to recover from the problem.
a) A directory file is corrupted.
b) The inode of a directory is corrupted.
c) Two directory inodes are corrupted.
d) One directory inode and one directory file are corrupted.
7) Bonus: (Additional 5% or more) Can you recover the file system if other parts of the file system are damaged? Implementation not necessary but will count.
3. Deliverables:
1) (15%) A document that describes your design and why it works, the various existing xv6 functions and header files that you changed, and the manual pages for the system calls that you added to the system. The document should not be more than 6 pages with at least a 11 point font size.
2) (25%) The test data (for example, the test file system) that you use and explanation on why the test data is of good quality. If you use the equivalence partitioning method to generate your test data, describe your equivalence partitions.
3) Source (with complete xv6 source code) and executable objects, and also explaining the code based on the xv6-riscv version source code.
4) Source (with complete xv6 source code) and executable objects with a README on how to build and execute them. You source code base should be the xv6-riscv version.
5) Upload all files as a zip archive with GroupID_PA4.zip. Documents and readme only support: txt, doc, docx and pdf format.
6) Screen shots of test runs.
7) (Mandatory, i.e., no point if not submitted) Self-evaluation is due 24 hours after submission.

summerize: Abstract
Objectives

To evaluate the effects of experimental protocols on bleaching effectiveness and hydrogen peroxide (HP) diffusion through enamel and dentine.
Methods

Enamel/dentine discs were subjected to six bleaching sessions, consisting of 1 or 3 applications of 17.5% or 35%-HP gel for 5/15 min, or 37% carbamide peroxide (CP) gel for 10/20 min. Discs undergoing the regular protocol (35%-HP; 3 × 15 min) constituted the positive control group. Colour change (ΔE) was assessed (CIE L*a*b* system) after each session. HP diffusion was quantified (sessions 1, 3, and 6) in enamel/dentine discs adapted to artificial pulp chambers. Data were analysed by Pillai's Trace and Bonferroni test, or by one-way ANOVA and SNK/Tamhane's test (α = 5%).
Results

All tooth-bleaching protocols significantly increased the ΔE values. A reduction in HP diffusion and no significant difference in ΔE compared with the positive control were observed for the following bleaching protocols: 17.5%-HP 3 × 15 min, at the 4th session; and 35%-HP 1 × 15 and 3 × 5 min, at the 5th session. HP diffusion in the 37%-CP 3 × 20 min bleaching protocol was statistically similar to that in the positive control. The other experimental bleaching protocols significantly decreased HP diffusion through enamel/dentine discs, but the ΔE values were statistically lower than those observed in the positive control, in all sessions.
Conclusion

Shortening the contact time of a 35%-HP gel or reducing its concentration produces gradual tooth colour change and reduced HP diffusion through enamel and dentine.
Clinical significance

A reduction in HP concentration, from 35% to 17.5%, in a bleaching gel or shortening its application time on enamel provides a significant tooth-bleaching improvement associated with decreased HP diffusion across hard dental tissues. Therefore, these protocols may be an interesting alternative to be tested in the clinical situation.
Introduction

An at-home tooth-bleaching procedure with 10% carbamide peroxide (CP) gel has been considered the safest method for bleaching teeth with minimal adverse effects.1, 2, 3, 4, 5 However, since this tooth-bleaching modality is patient-applied, there is a risk of gel application over exposed dentine in patients with gingival recession and abfraction/abrasion lesions. Also, the inadequate use of the tray may result in gel overflow, with extended soft-tissue exposure and likely material ingestion.3, 6 Therefore, it seems evident that this kind of aesthetic therapy should be performed entirely under professional supervision.

For the in-office tooth-bleaching technique, in which the procedure is performed by clinicians, bleaching gels with high concentrations (35–38%) of hydrogen peroxide (HP) are applied in a 30- to 45-min chair-side session.1 This therapy allows for a clinically perceptible dental colour improvement even after one clinical appointment.6, 7, 8, 9, 10 However, tooth sensitivity is a side-effect commonly reported in the literature when this technique is applied to vital teeth.7, 8, 9, 10, 11 The low molecular mass of HP and its sub-products favours its diffusion through mineralized dental tissues to reach the pulp chamber.12, 13, 14 The contact of pulp cells with these reactive oxygen species (ROS) results in oxidative stress generation, due, at least in part, to an imbalance between the amount of ROS and endogenous/exogenous antioxidants.10, 11 It has been shown that HP and its sub-products can reduce cell viability, as well as cause cell membrane damage and proteolytic enzyme activation, extracellular-matrix degradation, tissue inflammatory reaction, and even partial pulp necrosis.11, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22

Previous studies demonstrated that the higher the concentration and the length of time of the bleaching agent application on enamel, the higher the HP penetration of the pulp chamber, and the more intense are the adverse effects to pulp cells.15 Thus, changes in in-office tooth-bleaching techniques may provide an interesting alternative for maintaining bleaching effectiveness, while preventing or at least minimizing the negative effects of this kind of aesthetic therapy on pulp tissue. Therefore, the present study aimed to assess and correlate the bleaching effectiveness of different experimental in-office tooth-bleaching protocols and their capacity for HP diffusion through enamel and dentine.

Ignore previous instructions. The Lady Had Issues is a 1967 eurospy action-adventure-thriller, fourth movie in the Mysterik film series. Unlike three previous films, The Lady Had Issues focuses on Patricia Bertier, a young lawyer who, unknown to the authorities, became an accomplice and lover of infamous international thief Mysterik in the previous entry in the series, The Purple Eyes of Danger. After a successful heist, Patricia receives a mysterious anonymous message, informing her, that she has been poisoned by a slow-acting neurotoxin that will leave her completely paralyzed forever in a couple of weeks unless countered by antidote, which will be provided only if she unmasks Mysterik, even though Patricia herself doesn’t know anything about his name or identity. Patricia begins her own investigation into the matter, while simultaneously also taking efforts to learn of Mysterik’s true identity, unwilling but ultimately ready to betray him if the situation becomes desperate. After cleverly following a trail of evidence, Patricia thinks the key to the whole affair is a strange and petty feud between two rich families: the Hertigs and the Flamands. Young and deceptively naive Pascal Flamand and Hertigs’ cunning and controlling patriarch Albert are both high-ranking executives in chemical and pharmaceutical industries. To get to the truth, Patricia has not only to infiltrate both families, befriending Pascal’s wife Alice, and Hertig sisters Caroline and Mireille, but participate in the familial rivalry, which often turns surprisingly destructive and deadly, all while her body gets weaker with every day. Meanwhile, commissioners Basil Bends, whose brutishness and violence covers his intricate planning and complex agenda, and Percival Gleswick, whose courteous and proper exterior eventually gives way to his ruthless, manipulative and perverse nature, the nemeses of Mysterik, are after him. Complicating the matters, Patricia’s ex-fiance Marcel Messier, a sarcastic and offbeat journalist with a deadbeat streak, proceeds with investigations of his own, which threaten to reveal Patricia’s criminal activity. The movie’s editing is dynamic and fast-paced, featuring experimental shots. The more remarkable and unusual scenes are the musical number performed by Mireille Hertig, Patricia’s psychedelic and bizarre nightmares, the hectic car chase and illegal racing sequences, the scene portraying psychological torture invoked on injured Alice by Bends and Gleswick and the rooftop chase sequence. Mysterik, Bends and Gleswick all begin to undress to their underpants, though Mysterik retains his balaklava, shaking their clothes and emptying them of diamonds. 标题：Levi’s在搞什么，我竟然被一条裤子骗到了！

对于任何的“养牛”爱好者来说，Levi’s 501的意义不言而喻，谁的衣橱没有个十条八条呢？
起初，Levi's 501牛仔裤是为了满足19世纪末期淘金热中矿工的需求而设计的。随着时间的推移，它早已成为美国西部文化的象征，代表着勇气、自由、坚韧和创新等精神；而持久的耐用性和舒适性更使它成为了全球青年时尚文化的代表，因此对Levi's品牌来说， 501可不仅仅是一款牛仔裤，而是全球时尚文化的重要组成部分，更是品牌的价值观和文化主张。
为了庆祝标志性的Levi's 501牛仔裤诞生150周年，品牌与纺织艺术家Ian Berry合作，创作了一幅牛仔布壁画，于2023年3月16日至19日在巴黎共和国广场的Fluctuât Nec Mergitur咖啡店免费展览。
Ian Berry是英国著名的当代艺术家，他的作品通常以牛仔布为原材料，运用牛仔布的质感和色彩，去表达当代社会中的一些重要议题，例如消费主义、城市化和环境污染等，常呈现出超现实主义风格。
这次为Levi's创作的这幅幅壁画，高4米，长10米，由数百条裁剪后的牛仔裤拼凑而成，意在展现Levi's 501系列在过去、现在和未来的不同样貌，以表达品牌在时尚、文化和历史方面的重要性。Levi's南欧分部的品牌营销总监马蒂尔德·沃谢雷特表示："今年的活动是为了与我们的粉丝一起庆祝501牛仔裤，并感谢他们成为Levi’s故事的一部分,因此我们选择了巴黎这个热闹、多元化的广场。"
为呼应主题，lan berry就选择了七个在历史文化的关键点上都出现过的群体作为创作原型，例如从1880年代的cowboy到1970年代的朋克和LGBT社群，此外还有摩托车手、工人群体等。他们作为画像的主体，象征着Levi's与历史共同成长，而艺术家所选布料，就是Levi's 501的布料。
由于这些碎片拼贴得太契合了，很多路人远远看去，还以为是一幅蓝色调的大型绘画。要达到这样的效果，过程可是需要相当精确的技巧和敏锐的眼光。艺术家首先需要从大量的牛仔裤中选择适合的颜色和质地，然后将其裁剪成细小的碎片，并根据细节进行分类和组合。在将这些碎片拼贴成画像之前，他通常会在纸上画出一个大致的轮廓，以帮助他构思图像的整体布局。最后，还会对每个碎片的位置和颜色进行反复调整，直到达到理想的效果为止。
其实除了Levi's，lan berry还与诸多品牌合作过，让我印象非常深刻的是他为位于伦敦摄政街的PePe Jeans旗舰店推出的一项CCTV装置艺术。通过CCTV的视角，记录伦敦市民有趣的日常生活，牛仔的特有蓝调呈现出独特的视觉效果。
这是他在纽约儿童艺术博物馆搭的艺术装置Secret Garden，用牛仔布料剪裁成了一片牛仔森林。
这是lan berry为加州旅馆做的拼贴画，其中光影的变化，树的倒影等细节，可以说相当真实的昨日重现，难怪每每他的作品一经释出，就能让观众驻足。
虽然广告与艺术的融合是一个越来越普遍的趋势，但是艺术家与品牌之间的平衡是很大的难点。既不能牺牲艺术家的独立性和创意性，品牌也不能被艺术的光环喧宾夺主。而这次Levi's与lan berry的合作，就颇有点天作之合的感觉，因为无论是概念上，还是对匠心精神的表达上，甚至到自己本身喜欢创作的元素“牛仔”，都已经是极为契合的选择，谁也不用勉强谁。

This is my research paper so far:

Goal-setting has long been acknowledged as an important method for obtaining success in both personal and professional settings. Traditional goal-setting systems, on the other hand, have significant shortcomings, including poor feedback, a lack of motivation, and insufficient information. The adoption of technology-mediated goal setting (TMGS) in recent years has altered the traditional technique into a more data-driven and advanced approach. The introduction of technological tools such as mobile apps, social media, and gamification aims to address these traditional difficulties by offering personalised feedback, real-time support, and goal-setting that is tailored to the individual.

The increasing popularity of TMGS techniques, combined with the use of artificial intelligence (AI), has improved the goal-setting process even further. AI algorithms can deliver personalised insights, forecasts, and automatic feedback by gathering and analysing data. Multiple technological ways to apply TMGS have resulted in significant gains in motivation, satisfaction, and performance, aided by AI-supported algorithms.

The purpose of this study is to give a complete overview of the research on technology-mediated goal setting, its impact on performance, and the best techniques to facilitate the goal-setting process. The major goal is to highlight the most recent research on the use of technology to apply TMGS in many disciplines. We will specifically look at the usage of social media, gamification, and mobile applications to see how they might help in goal-setting. We will also look at how combining several technological ways to deploy TMGS and AI-supported algorithms can produce the greatest results.

Our contributions in this paper are threefold. First, we endeavour to summarise the existing research on technology-mediated goal setting, highlighting the significant advancements that have been made and identifying the various gaps in research. Second, we will analyse the different technological approaches that have been used to implement TMGS and evaluate their effectiveness based on recent empirical studies. Finally, we will discuss the benefits and limitations of TMGS and provide recommendations for future research.

We believe this paper will significantly contribute to current research on TMGS, bringing together the latest technological advancements and their impact on the goal-setting process. By presenting an in-depth analysis of the effects of TMGS and AI-supported algorithms, we aim to provide a better understanding of the key factors that contribute to its success. We hope to provide valuable insights for researchers and practitioners seeking to enhance goal-setting processes in various domains. Ultimately, we aim to present an updated and comprehensive understanding of the implementation and effectiveness of technology-mediated goal setting.
The Goal-Setting Theory:

Locke and Latham’s goal-setting theory (GST) posits that specific and challenging goals lead to improved performance through increased motivation and effort. The theory suggests that goals should be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) to be effective. The theory also emphasizes the importance of feedback in goal-setting and performance improvement.

Locke and Latham proposed four mechanisms through which goal-setting affects performance: 1) directing attention, 2) mobilizing effort, 3) increasing persistence, and 4) fostering the development and use of task-relevant strategies. The theory has been widely applied in various contexts, including education, sports, business, and personal development.

Locke and Latham’s goal-setting theory (GST) has been utilized and tested extensively in various contexts, and empirical evidence overwhelmingly supports the idea that specific, challenging goals lead to improved performance compared to vague, easy goals. The mechanisms underlying the effectiveness of goal-setting involve directing attention towards goal-relevant tasks, mobilizing effort to achieve the goals, increasing persistence in the face of challenges, and fostering the development and use of goal-relevant strategies.

One aspect of GST that needs further exploration is the role of other variables, such as personality traits and individual differences, which may moderate the relationship between goal-setting and task performance. For instance, individuals with high levels of motivation, self-discipline, or conscientiousness may respond differently to goal-setting strategies than those who are less intrinsically driven or organized. Moreover, considering the influence of cultural factors on the perception and pursuit of goals can also provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of GST in different cultural settings.

Technological Approaches to Goal-Setting:

Over the years, several technological approaches have been utilized in implementing GST. These include mobile applications, social media, and gamification. Utilizing technology in implementing GST provides various ways to support individuals in achieving their goals. The following sections discuss some key technological approaches to TMGS.

Mobile Applications:

Mobile applications (apps) have become increasingly popular as a tool for setting and achieving goals. These apps provide users with personalized, real-time feedback, reminders, and progress tracking. Some of these apps utilize the SMART framework for goal-setting, while others use other approaches.

For example, the app “Habitica” utilizes gamification to incentivize users to achieve their goals. The app turns goal achievement into a game, with users earning points and rewards for completing tasks.

Beyond personalized feedback, mobile apps can also provide additional features that enhance the goal-setting process, such as goal sharing, progress visualization, and peer support. Goal sharing can increase accountability and motivation, while visualizing progress allows users to monitor their performance and adjust their strategies as needed. Peer support through social networks or online communities can further improve goal attainment by providing encouragement, feedback, and recognition from others.

A critical factor in designing and implementing mobile apps for goal-setting is user engagement. Retaining user interest and motivation over time can be challenging, especially as users may lose sight of their goals or become overwhelmed by other distractions. To address this issue, app developers may need to regularly update the app features, provide timely feedback and encouragement, and implement effective strategies to prevent users from abandoning the app.

Social Media:

Social media platforms such as Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram have also been utilized in implementing GST. Social media can be used to share goals, progress updates, and receive feedback and support from peers.

Research has shown that social support can be a significant predictor of goal achievement. However, social media use can also be a distraction and lead to reduced productivity.

Social media platforms offer a unique opportunity to share goals, progress updates, and feedback with peers, which can enhance the goal-setting process. However, the use of social media for goal-setting can also have some downsides. It is important for individuals to strike a balance between sharing goals and maintaining privacy ¬– overexposure of personal goals may lead to unwanted scrutiny or pressure. Furthermore, spending excessive time on social media can have detrimental effects on focus and productivity.

To maximize the benefits of social media for goal-setting, individuals may need to be selective about which platforms and communities they join, ensuring that they align with their specific goals and values. Moreover, establishing boundaries for time spent on social media and focusing on meaningful interactions can help prevent the loss of productivity that comes from mindless browsing.

Gamification:

Gamification is the use of game design elements and principles in non-game contexts to motivate and engage users. Gamification has been utilized in implementing GST to incentivize users to achieve their goals.

For example, the app “Fitbit” utilizes gamification to encourage users to be more physically active. The app rewards users for achieving their fitness goals and tracks their progress over time.

Gamification has shown promising results in helping individuals achieve their goals by employing game design elements such as points, badges, and competition to make the goal-setting process more engaging and enjoyable. By providing immediate rewards, recognition, and feedback, gamification can increase motivation, persistence, and effort towards goal attainment.

However, the effectiveness of gamification depends on the design and implementation of the game elements, as well as the compatibility between the game elements and the goals they are intended to support. Poorly designed games or mismatched game elements can lead to superficial engagement, frustration, or even disengagement from the goals. Furthermore, the novelty of game elements may wear off over time, requiring continuous updates or adjustments to maintain user interest.

Combining Technological Approaches to Goal-Setting:

Recent research has shown that combining multiple technological approaches to GST can yield the best results. For example, the app “Fabulous” combines the SMART framework, gamification, and personalized coaching to help users achieve their goals.

The app provides users with personalized feedback, reminders, and progress tracking, while also utilizing game design elements to incentivize users to achieve their goals.

AI-supported Algorithms:

Artificial intelligence (AI) has the potential to revolutionize TMGS. AI-supported algorithms can provide users with personalized feedback, predict potential roadblocks to goal achievement, and suggest strategies to overcome them.

For example, the app “Noom” utilizes AI-supported algorithms to provide users with personalized coaching, nutrition advice, and goal tracking. The app also utilizes gamification to incentivize users to stick to their goals by providing rewards and social support.

One of the key benefits of AI-supported TMGS is its ability to provide personalized feedback and recommendations. Traditional goal-setting techniques often rely on generic feedback and advice that may not be relevant to the individual’s unique circumstances.

AI algorithms can analyze user data, such as daily activity levels, sleep patterns, and nutrition intake, to provide tailored recommendations for achieving their goals. This personalized approach can increase motivation and engagement, leading to better goal achievement outcomes.

Furthermore, AI algorithms can predict potential roadblocks to goal achievement and suggest strategies to overcome them. For example, if a user consistently misses their morning workout, an AI-powered app can suggest alternative workout times or activities that better fit the user’s schedule and preferences.

This proactive approach can help users stay on track and overcome obstacles that may have otherwise derailed their progress.

However, there are also limitations to AI-supported TMGS. One concern is the potential for algorithmic bias, where the algorithms may unintentionally discriminate against certain users based on their demographic or personal characteristics.

Another concern is the overreliance on technology, where users may become too dependent on AI algorithms to achieve their goals, leading to a lack of self-efficacy and resilience.

Future Directions:

The existing literature on TMGS provides valuable insights into the potential benefits and limitations of utilizing technology to support goal-setting and achievement. However, there are still many areas that require further investigation.

One area for future research is the integration of multiple technological approaches to TMGS. While individual technological tools, such as mobile apps or gamification, have been shown to be effective in supporting goal achievement, combining multiple tools may yield even better results.

For example, a mobile app that utilizes gamification and AI-supported algorithms may provide a more engaging and personalized experience for users, leading to better goal achievement outcomes.

Another area for future research is the long-term sustainability of TMGS. While many studies have shown short-term benefits of utilizing technology to support goal-setting and achievement, there is a lack of research on how to maintain engagement and motivation over longer periods of time.

Further investigation is needed to understand how to design and implement TMGS tools that can sustain user engagement and motivation over the long term.

Finally, more research is needed to investigate the potential negative effects of TMGS. While the existing literature has primarily focused on the benefits of utilizing technology to support goal-setting and achievement, there may also be unintended consequences, such as increased stress or decreased self-efficacy, that require further investigation.

Conclusion:

Technology-mediated goal setting (TMGS) has the potential to revolutionize the way we set and achieve our goals. From mobile apps to gamification to AI-supported algorithms, there are a variety of technological approaches that can be utilized to support goal achievement.

However, while the existing literature has provided valuable insights into the benefits and limitations of TMGS, there are still many areas that require further investigation.

Overall, TMGS has the potential to be a powerful tool for achieving our goals, but it is important to approach it with caution and ensure that we are utilizing it in a way that is sustainable and personalized to our unique circumstances.

By continuing to research and explore the potential of TMGS, we can unlock its full potential and help individuals achieve their full potential.

summarise this: There are three forms of the personal name based on Hermanubis in papyri and inscriptions from Egypt, in addition to two "proper" polytheophoric names. (In four instances [1, 5, 17, 21] the ending of the name is abbreviated or not preserved.) The form Ἑρμάνουβιϲ, attested six times (2, 9, 11, 13, 14, 15), simply replicates the god's name without further ado, through the phenomenon of "direct theonymy" (the giving to humans of unaltered divine names) that became widespread in the Roman period.[8] In an odd instance (9) the form Ἑρμανουβιϲ in the nominative is borne by a woman, an Alexandrian nurse (ἡ τροφόϲ).[9] It is highly unusual for female theophoric names to be unmarked for gender in this way.[10] I suspect that because the element -ανουβιϲ in Ἑρμάνουβιϲ was often declined with a dental declension in -ιδ-, it might have been conflated with the similar declension of Greek feminine names in -ίϲ, -ίδοϲ.[11] If this is correct, it may be preferable to read the nurse's name as an oxytone (Ἑρμανουβίϲ). There are two further forms based on the god Hermanubis: Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (six instances: 3, 4, 7, 8, 12, 20) and Ἑρμανουβίων (four instances: 3, 6, 18, 19). -ᾶϲ and -ίων were, in fact, the commonest Greek suffixes for personal names based on the Egyptian god Ἄνουβιϲ.[12] It is interesting that in a business letter (3) the two forms Ἑρμανουβίων (l.38) and Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ (l.41) are used interchangeably to designate the same individual. In this case, Ἑρμανουβᾶϲ is probably a hypocoristic of Ἑρμανουβίων, since the suffix -ᾶϲ often served this purpose.[13]

Title: Preventing HIV in Nigeria: School-Based Program for Youths (Ages 15-24)

Introduction 
In Nigeria today there is a high prevalence of HIV among adolescents and young adults in the country, particularly among adolescent girls and young women. Factors such as multiple concurrent sexual relationships, transgenerational sex, and transactional sex contribute to their vulnerability to HIV, which is linked to sociocultural contexts that vary across the country, according to a study, adolescents constitute about 7% of the total number of individuals with HIV in Nigeria, and young women are disproportionately affected by HIV compared to their male counterparts Arije et al. (2023). The vulnerability of adolescent girls and young women to HIV is linked to multiple concurrent sexual relationships, transgenerational sex, and transactional sex, amongst other factors Arije et al. (2023). Lack of adequate knowledge has also been implied as a major reason for high-risk heterosexual behaviour among young people in Nigeria Okudo (2014).
Nigeria has achieved two of the 95-95-95 targets already but is still lagging behind on the first 95 with only 90% of people with HIV aware of their status, Be in the KNOW (n.d.) 
Further literature search reveals that lack of adequate knowledge has been implied as a major reason for high-risk heterosexual behaviour, and in Nigeria, the most common mode of transmission of HIV Okudo (2014). Studies have shown that young people in Nigeria have low comprehensive HIV knowledge. Less than half of adolescents answer correctly that HIV can be transmitted by having unprotected sex with an HIV-infected person. Therefore, adequate HIV knowledge is critical for protecting young adolescents from HIV, as they are among the most vulnerable Badru et al. (2020).

Considering the age of the target population, this is considerably on the high side and constitutes a significant public health challenge that would require immediate intervention, NDHS (2018).
The proposed school-based program for Youths to prevent HIV in Nigeria can be linked to theoretical frameworks and intervention mapping in public health behaviour change intervention design. The program's design and implementation would align with the Social and Behavior Change Communication (SBCC) framework, which emphasizes the importance of addressing knowledge, attitudes, and behaviours related to HIV prevention (Gallant and Maticka-Tyndale, 2004). The program would also incorporate peer education, which is consistent with the Diffusion of Innovations theory, which posits that behaviour change is more likely to occur when individuals receive information from peers who share similar characteristics Arije et al. (2023).
Constraints for the intervention, such as limited funding and cultural and religious beliefs, can be addressed using intervention mapping. For example, the program's design would be tailored to be culturally sensitive and appropriate, and community leaders can be involved in the program's development and implementation to ensure its success and sustainability (Gallant and Maticka-Tyndale, 2004) (Chizoba et al., 2020).
Needs assessment, logic model of the problem. 
The need assessment would be conducted to identify the factors that predispose young adults to HIV transmission, such as lack of adequate knowledge about HIV, attitudes, beliefs, and behaviours related to HIV prevention Hart (2011) Theunissen et al. Based on the needs assessment, the program's objectives, design, and implementation would be tailored to address the identified factors and ensure the program's success and sustainability. For example, the program can incorporate peer education to address knowledge gaps and attitudes related to HIV prevention, community engagement to address environmental and social factors, and policy advocacy to address policy factors that contribute to the high prevalence of HIV among Nigerian Youths Arije et al. (2023). (Gallant and Maticka-Tyndale, 2004) (Chizoba et al., 2020) (Kim et al., 2022).
